These results demonstrate that CoT distillation improves compact student models and that response length is a critical factor in mathematical reasoning quality.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that distilling reasoning knowledge from large models (DeepSeek-R1) to compact models (Qwen2.5-7B) via Chain-of-Thought training improves mathematical reasoning accuracy by 4.76%, with response length being a critical factor in answer quality.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the mathematical reasoning capabilities of compact language models by transferring knowledge from larger reasoning models through distillation, making high-quality reasoning more accessible on resource-constrained hardware.
AI.method: The method involves: 1) Building a Chain-of-Thought training corpus using historical math competition problems (2011-2025) via a dual-agent framework, 2) Fine-tuning the Qwen2.5-7B student model using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on Apple Silicon hardware with MLX framework, and 3) Conducting multiple training runs limited to 200 iterations to avoid overfitting based on initial validation loss analysis.
AI.result: Results show: 1) Base model accuracy: 64.67%, teacher model: 91.40%, 2) Fine-tuned student achieves 69.43% mean accuracy (4.76% improvement) on competition problems, 3) Generalizes to 73.1% on MATH-500 benchmark, 4) Response length significantly affects accuracy - drops from 69.43% at R1 (220 words) to 41.9% at R6 (31.2 words), with two-person speed section being most sensitive to token reduction.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Chain-of-Thought distillation effectively improves compact model performance for mathematical reasoning, with careful attention to training iteration limits to prevent overfitting, and that response length is a critical factor affecting reasoning quality.

title: Beyond the Library: An Agentic Framework for Autoformalizing Research Mathematics
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in mathematical reasoning, they frequently produce subtle errors that evade human detection. Formal mathematical languages like Lean 4 offer mechanical proof checking, strongly motivating the need for autoformalization: the automatic translation of natural language mathematics into verifiable code. Recent trends indicate that general-purpose LLMs, heavily optimized for standard programming, now outperform smaller models explicitly fine-tuned for Lean. Leveraging this shift, we introduce an agentic autoformalization framework powered by general coding LLMs. At the core of our system is an orchestrator that manages a multi-agent pipeline tailored for research-level mathematics. Because cutting-edge research frequently relies on concepts outside the scope of existing libraries like Mathlib, our system dynamically extends necessary type definitions and validates them via a novel Auxiliary Lemma technique before formalizing the primary theorems. We applied our approach to PutnamBench, producing machine-checked Lean proofs for a random sample of 32 problems. Furthermore, we evaluate our system on five papers from the ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC) spanning combinatorics, communication complexity, mechanism design, and learning theory, successfully formalizing their main theorems and validating the generated formalizations with human experts; for all five we also formalize the proofs alongside the statements, and notably two of them are proved with no axioms beyond Lean's kernel. All of our formalizations are available at https://beyondthelibrary.github.io/formal_arxiv .

AI.tldr: An agentic autoformalization framework using general coding LLMs to translate natural language mathematics into verifiable Lean 4 code, achieving successful formalization of complex research-level theorems including those from ACM STOC papers.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) produce subtle mathematical errors that evade human detection. Formal languages like Lean 4 offer mechanical proof checking, creating a need for autoformalization. Recent trends show general-purpose LLMs optimized for standard programming now outperform smaller models fine-tuned for Lean, suggesting a shift towards using general coding LLMs for mathematical formalization.
AI.method: An agentic autoformalization framework with an orchestrator managing a multi-agent pipeline tailored for research-level mathematics. The system dynamically extends necessary type definitions (since research concepts often fall outside existing libraries like Mathlib) and validates them via a novel Auxiliary Lemma technique before formalizing primary theorems.
AI.result: Successfully produced machine-checked Lean proofs for 32 random problems from PutnamBench. Formally validated main theorems from five ACM STOC papers spanning combinatorics, communication complexity, mechanism design, and learning theory. Formalized proofs alongside statements, with two proofs requiring no axioms beyond Lean's kernel.
AI.conclusion: The agentic autoformalization framework using general coding LLMs effectively translates natural language mathematics into verifiable Lean code, even for cutting-edge research papers that rely on concepts beyond existing libraries. The approach demonstrates practical viability for formalizing research-level mathematics.

title: Fora: From Weight-Space to Function-Space Protection in Capability-Preserving Fine-Tuning
summary: Full fine-tuning adapts large language models to new tasks but can erode capabilities they already possess. Existing remedies protect through proxies such as parameter distances, importance penalties, output matching, or dominant singular directions of the weights, but none directly asks which activation directions the preserved capability relies on. We argue that a capability is characterized more faithfully by the activation subspace it induces than by the singular geometry of the weight matrix, and develop function-space protection, instantiated as FORA (Function-space Orthogonal Residual Adaptation). From label-free calibration inputs, FORA estimates, per layer, the principal directions $Q$ of the input-activation covariance and forms a right projector $P_Q = I - QQ^T$. Paired with a left projector $P_U$ from the weight SVD, the update is $ΔW = P_U M P_Q + U_2 D_δ V_2^T$: a high-capacity branch structurally barred from reading capability-relevant function directions, plus a narrow spectral channel for controlled plasticity. The construction extends to parameter-efficient adaptation via $M \to (α/r) BA$. Across three settings on Qwen3-1.7B, including COGS and GSM8K learned while preserving translation and translation learned while preserving math, FORA consistently improves preservation over weight-space projection and standard regularization, with only a small new-task trade-off in the math-preservation setting. A controlled ablation isolating the projection source shows that the advantage comes not from projection itself, but from projecting onto capability-derived rather than weight-derived directions. Code is available at https://github.com/zrui239/FORA.

AI.tldr: FORA is a new fine-tuning method that protects existing model capabilities by projecting weight updates onto function-space directions orthogonal to preserved capabilities' activation subspaces, outperforming weight-space projection methods.
AI.motivation: Full fine-tuning of LLMs can erode existing capabilities while adapting to new tasks. Existing methods protect capabilities through proxies like parameter distances or weight singular directions, but none directly addresses which activation directions the preserved capability relies on.
AI.method: FORA (Function-space Orthogonal Residual Adaptation) protects capabilities by estimating principal directions Q of input-activation covariance from label-free calibration inputs. It creates a right projector P_Q = I - QQ^T paired with a left projector P_U from weight SVD. The update is ΔW = P_U M P_Q + U₂D_δV₂^T: a high-capacity branch structurally prevented from reading capability-relevant function directions plus a narrow spectral channel for controlled plasticity.
AI.result: Across three settings on Qwen3-1.7B (COGS and GSM8K learned while preserving translation, and translation learned while preserving math), FORA consistently improves preservation over weight-space projection and standard regularization, with only small new-task trade-off in math-preservation setting.
AI.conclusion: FORA demonstrates that capability preservation benefits from projecting onto capability-derived activation directions rather than weight-derived directions. The advantage comes not from projection itself, but from using function-space directions aligned with preserved capabilities.

title: Cross-Domain Feature Expansion for Tabular Medical Data via Knowledge Graphs Injection
summary: Acquiring comprehensive cross-domain biomedical profiles is often costly and time-consuming, resulting in severe data scarcity in medical research. To address this challenge, we propose MedKGTab, a knowledge-injected framework specifically engineered for cross-domain feature expansion in tabular medical data. MedKGTab seeks to infer uncollected biomedical features from available ones by exploiting their inherent statistical dependencies and established medical correlations. By employing a row-column dual-attention mechanism, MedKGTab operates directly on raw structured tabular data, inherently capturing exact numerical distributions without the structural loss caused by tokenization. Crucially, MedKGTab integrates data-driven statistical priors with the SPOKE biomedical knowledge graph, achieving an optimal synergy between the data and knowledge channels. Within this synergy, the representations derived from the data channel are modulated by the injected biomedical knowledge, ensuring the final generated data are grounded in empirical medical research. Experimental results demonstrate that MedKGTab achieves high data fidelity and realistic data representation in cross-domain feature expansion. It outperforms both SOTA medical large models (e.g., Baichuan M3-plus) and specialized tabular models designed for medical data generation. Furthermore, MedKGTab consistently delivers superior performance across various data generation scenarios, whether inferring missing features within the same dataset or generalizing across different medical cohorts.

AI.tldr: MedKGTab is a knowledge-injected framework for cross-domain feature expansion in medical tabular data that combines statistical dependencies with biomedical knowledge graphs to infer uncollected features.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain biomedical data collection is expensive and time-consuming, leading to data scarcity in medical research. There's a need to infer missing features from available ones.
AI.method: Uses row-column dual-attention mechanism on raw tabular data to preserve numerical distributions. Integrates data-driven statistical priors with SPOKE biomedical knowledge graph, modulating data representations with injected medical knowledge.
AI.result: Achieves high data fidelity and realistic representation in cross-domain feature expansion. Outperforms both state-of-the-art medical large models and specialized tabular models for medical data generation. Works well for both missing feature inference within datasets and generalization across different medical cohorts.
AI.conclusion: MedKGTab effectively addresses data scarcity in medical research by synergistically combining statistical patterns with biomedical knowledge, enabling realistic and accurate cross-domain feature expansion while maintaining data integrity.

title: Explaining Machine Learning and Memorization with Statistical Mechanics
comment: PhD thesis defended on January 15, 2026. Supervisor: Daniele Tantari. Committee: Elena Agliari, Aurelien Decelle, Daniele Tantari, Fosca Gianotti, Fabrizio Lillo
summary: Artificial neural networks (NNs) and machine learning (ML) algorithms are poorly understood from a theoretical perspective, which makes it difficult to fully realize their potential and overcome their weaknesses. For instance, ML algorithms train NN weights by moving them along a low-dimensional subspace of their allowed values, but this implicitly low-dimensional learning structure is not properly exploited to improve training because its nature is not well understood. Moreover, trained NNs are easily confused by pervasive adversarial attacks whose theoretical underpinnings are still unclear. This thesis aims to improve our theoretical understanding of NNs and ML, with a particular focus on adversarial attacks and implicitly low-dimensional learning. For this purpose, we use mathematical tools from statistical mechanics to study different types of NNs and ways in which they can fit the data. In particular, we study two classes of models that fit the data with various degrees of learning and memorization: dense associative memory (DAM) and restricted Boltzmann machines (RBM). In the process, we investigate connections between different versions of these models that are useful to make analytical investigations more efficient.

AI.tldr: The paper uses statistical mechanics to theoretically understand neural networks, focusing on adversarial attacks and low-dimensional learning patterns in dense associative memory and restricted Boltzmann machines.
AI.motivation: Neural networks and machine learning algorithms are poorly understood theoretically, which limits their potential and ability to overcome weaknesses like adversarial attacks. The low-dimensional learning structure in NN training is not properly exploited due to lack of understanding, and adversarial attacks are pervasive but theoretically unclear.
AI.method: The research uses mathematical tools from statistical mechanics to study different types of neural networks. Specifically, it examines two classes of models that fit data with varying degrees of learning and memorization: dense associative memory (DAM) and restricted Boltzmann machines (RBM). The study investigates connections between different versions of these models to make analytical investigations more efficient.
AI.result: The paper doesn't specify concrete results in the abstract, but the research aims to improve theoretical understanding of neural networks, with particular focus on adversarial attacks and implicitly low-dimensional learning through the study of DAM and RBM models.
AI.conclusion: This thesis contributes to the theoretical foundation of neural networks and machine learning by applying statistical mechanics to understand adversarial attacks and low-dimensional learning structures, potentially leading to more robust and efficient training methods.

title: ClawArena-Team: Benchmarking Subagent Orchestration and Dynamic Workflows in Language-Model Agents
summary: Production large language-model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed not as lone problem-solvers but as managers: a main model creates specialized subagents, delegates work, and orchestrates their parallel, asynchronous returns through dynamic workflows. Whether one model can actually run such a team is largely unmeasured: existing benchmarks score a policy's own task-solving or a fixed multi-agent system's emergent behavior, but none isolate the management ability of the single LLM acting as leader. We introduce ClawArena-Team, a benchmark of 41 multi-turn, multimodal, multi-directory scenarios spanning 258 evaluation rounds and 72 staged updates that measures this management ability. The main agent is deliberately constrained: it natively perceives only text and directly accesses only part of the workspace. It commands a fixed, locally served subagent pool, so score differences reflect management skill, not raw capability. All scoring is execution-based with no LLM judge: an overall score -- the Subagent-Management Score (SMS) -- multiplies task correctness by a least-privilege and modality-routing factor. Across twelve proprietary, community-hosted, and self-hosted models, experiments show that the management bottleneck is privilege granting rather than perception (no model exceeds 50% workspace-permission precision); that cost and management quality are decoupled (API cost spans over 100 times while the overall score spans under 4 times, with the cheapest open models on the Pareto frontier); and that most leaderboard scores cluster within a 9.9-point band while orchestration behaviors diverge by more than an order of magnitude. Code and data will be released.

AI.tldr: A benchmark called ClawArena-Team is introduced to measure LLM agents' management abilities in orchestrating multi-agent teams, revealing that management skills are bottlenecked by privilege control rather than perception, and that cost and management quality are surprisingly decoupled.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as team managers rather than solitary problem-solvers, but existing benchmarks only measure individual task-solving or fixed multi-agent systems, failing to isolate the management ability of a single LLM acting as team leader.
AI.method: Created ClawArena-Team benchmark with 41 multi-turn, multimodal, multi-directory scenarios spanning 258 evaluation rounds and 72 staged updates. The main agent is constrained to text-only perception and limited workspace access, commanding a fixed subagent pool to isolate management skills from raw capabilities. Uses execution-based scoring with no LLM judge.
AI.result: Across 12 models, management bottleneck is privilege granting (no model exceeds 50% workspace-permission precision). Cost and management quality are decoupled - API cost spans over 100x while overall scores span under 4x, with cheap open models on Pareto frontier. Leaderboard scores cluster within 9.9-point band while orchestration behaviors diverge by an order of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces a novel benchmark to measure LLM management abilities, revealing critical insights about privilege control as the main bottleneck and the surprising decoupling of cost from management performance, suggesting that effective team management is a distinct skill that can be achieved economically.

title: Visualizing High-Dimensional Graph Embeddings via Informed Multi-View Projections
summary: Graphs are commonly visualized in 2D, where humans readily interpret spatial relationships, yet such layouts often distort higher-dimensional structure. We propose to embed graphs in high-dimensional space and search for informative 2D viewpoints that optimize aesthetic and readability metrics (e.g., edge crossings and angular resolution), enabled by a novel differentiable surrogate for edge crossings. Numerical experiments show that these viewpoints consistently outperform standard 2D layouts, and can even surpass methods explicitly designed to optimize these metrics. We further introduce DataFly, an interactive system for exploring multiple candidate viewpoints through seamless navigation. A usability study demonstrates that our approach reveals structural patterns that remain hidden in conventional 2D visualizations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes embedding graphs in high-dimensional space and finding optimal 2D viewpoints using differentiable edge crossing surrogates, plus an interactive system called DataFly for exploring these viewpoints.
AI.motivation: Traditional 2D graph visualizations distort higher-dimensional structure and fail to reveal complex structural patterns, while humans naturally interpret spatial relationships better in 2D views.
AI.method: Embed graphs in high-dimensional space and search for informative 2D viewpoints optimizing aesthetic metrics using novel differentiable surrogate for edge crossings, enabling gradient-based optimization.
AI.result: The high-dimensional viewpoints consistently outperform standard 2D layouts and can even surpass methods explicitly designed to optimize these metrics. DataFly system enables interactive exploration revealing hidden structural patterns.
AI.conclusion: High-dimensional embeddings with optimized 2D viewpoints provide superior graph visualization that reveals structural patterns hidden in conventional 2D visualizations, enhanced by interactive exploration systems.

title: HealthAgentBench: A Unified Benchmark Suite of Realistic Agentic Healthcare Environments for Challenging Frontier AI Agents
summary: As AI agents become increasingly capable of complex, long-horizon reasoning, rigorous and holistic evaluation is essential for measuring progress toward real-world healthcare applications. We introduce HealthAgentBench, a suite of 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories each with its unique environment. The benchmark suite spans diverse workflows throughout the patient journey and a broad range of modalities. Each task is designed to replicate an end-to-end clinical workflow: given minimal instructions, an agent must explore raw healthcare data, operate within a complex environment, and execute multi-step solutions that go beyond naive prompting. A final task success rate is reported to provide a single, interpretable metric for HealthAgentBench overall performance for each agent. Evaluating frontier agents on HealthAgentBench, we find that overall task success rate remains low, underscoring the difficulty of the suite. The strongest and the most cost effective agent, Codex GPT-5.5, achieves only approximately 42% success rate. Beyond aggregate performance, HealthAgentBench reveals nuanced strengths and weaknesses across task categories. Frontier agents show promise in automatically developing research modeling pipelines over EHR data, but medical imaging remains especially challenging, particularly for Claude Code models, while Codex GPT-5.5 shows emerging capability. Tasks that combine large search spaces with compositional reasoning requirements remain difficult for all current agents. Together, these results suggest that HealthAgentBench provides a challenging and realistic benchmark with substantial room for future progress. We release our benchmark at https://github.com/microsoft/HealthAgentBench.

AI.tldr: HealthAgentBench is a comprehensive benchmark suite with 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories that evaluates AI agents' ability to handle complex, multi-step clinical workflows using diverse healthcare modalities and data types.
AI.motivation: As AI agents become increasingly capable of complex, long-horizon reasoning, there's a need for rigorous and holistic evaluation to measure progress toward real-world healthcare applications, requiring benchmarks that replicate end-to-end clinical workflows.
AI.method: Created 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories, each with unique environments, spanning diverse patient journey workflows and modalities. Tasks require agents to explore raw healthcare data, operate in complex environments, and execute multi-step solutions beyond naive prompting, with success rate as the primary performance metric.
AI.result: Overall task success rates remain low (best agent Codex GPT-5.5 achieves ~42%), revealing nuanced strengths and weaknesses. Agents show promise in developing research modeling pipelines over EHR data, but struggle with medical imaging tasks (especially Claude Code models). Tasks combining large search spaces with compositional reasoning remain difficult for all current agents.
AI.conclusion: HealthAgentBench provides a challenging and realistic benchmark with substantial room for future progress in AI healthcare agents, highlighting current limitations while offering a standardized evaluation framework to drive development in this critical domain.

title: Can Tabular In-Context Learners Generalize to Biomolecular Property Prediction?
summary: Predicting biomolecular properties from limited labeled data is a central bottleneck in protein engineering and small-molecule design. As strong pretrained encoders now supply rich fixed-length representations, the difficulty has shifted from representation learning to building a data-efficient predictor for the few-shot regime. Tabular foundation models such as TabPFN3 and TabICL are unlikely candidates for this role: they are in-context learners pretrained on synthetic tables drawn from random causal graphs, a generative prior with no obvious correspondence to the processes that produce protein sequences or molecular graphs. That this tabular, causal inductive bias should transfer to biomolecular data at all is unintuitive, yet we find it does. Treating each method as a predictor-representation pair, we evaluate across two domains. Over a fixed ESMC representation, tabular in-context learning is consistently competitive for protein fitness regression on ProteinGym and a diverse esterase dataset. For small-molecule classification with ECFP/RDKit descriptors, no single pairing dominates across TDC ADMET, MoleculeNet, FS-Mol, and DrugOOD; representation choice becomes a primary determinant, as expected when the predictor's own prior is indifferent to molecular structure. We conclude that tabular foundation models are strong performers on biomolecular prediction tasks, but that their performance depends strongly on the sequence or molecular representation used.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models like TabPFN3 and TabICL, despite being pretrained on synthetic causal data, surprisingly perform well on biomolecular prediction tasks in few-shot regimes, though their performance depends heavily on the molecular representation used.
AI.motivation: Predicting biomolecular properties from limited labeled data is a major bottleneck in protein engineering and small-molecule design. While pretrained encoders now provide good fixed-length representations, the challenge has shifted to building data-efficient predictors for few-shot learning. The paper explores whether tabular foundation models - typically pretrained on synthetic causal data - can effectively transfer to biomolecular domains despite their mismatched inductive biases.
AI.method: The authors treat each method as a predictor-representation pair and evaluate across two domains: protein fitness regression and small-molecule classification. For proteins, they use ESMC representation and test on ProteinGym and esterase datasets. For small molecules, they use ECFP/RDKit descriptors and evaluate across multiple benchmark datasets including TDC ADMET, MoleculeNet, FS-Mol, and DrugOOD. They compare tabular in-context learning models against other approaches.
AI.result: 1) For protein fitness regression with ESMC representation, tabular in-context learning is consistently competitive. 2) For small-molecule classification, no single predictor-representation pairing dominates across all datasets. 3) Representation choice becomes a primary determinant of performance when the predictor's own prior is indifferent to molecular structure. 4) Surprisingly, despite being pretrained on synthetic causal graphs unrelated to biomolecular processes, tabular foundation models demonstrate strong performance on biomolecular prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: Tabular foundation models are surprisingly effective performers on biomolecular prediction tasks in few-shot regimes, but their success depends strongly on the sequence or molecular representation used. The transfer of tabular, causal inductive biases to biomolecular domains works better than expected, though the representation becomes critically important when the predictor lacks domain-specific priors.

title: AI-Assisted Discovery of Convex Relaxations via Dual Agents
summary: Recent work shows that LLM agents can improve sharp-constant inequalities by searching for extremal constructions, which yield upper bounds. We address the complementary side: a lower bound holds for every admissible function and follows from a convex relaxation of the nonconvex problem, with tighter relaxations giving stronger bounds. We instantiate the autoresearch paradigm to discover such relaxations: a coding agent proposes valid tightening constraints, a theory agent verifies each one and searches for counterexamples, and every reported bound is certified by an explicit dual-feasible point checked in rigorous interval arithmetic. On two optimization constants studied by \citet{tao2025alphaevolve} - the first autocorrelation inequality ($C_{6.2}$) and the Erdős minimum-overlap constant ($C_{6.5}$) - we improve the certified lower bounds from $1.28$ to $1.2937$ and from $0.379005$ to $0.37912$, respectively.

AI.tldr: LLM agents improve lower bounds for mathematical constants by discovering convex relaxations of nonconvex optimization problems, achieving certified improvements on two specific constants.
AI.motivation: While previous work showed LLM agents can improve upper bounds by searching for extremal constructions, there's a complementary need to improve lower bounds, which require a different approach involving convex relaxations of nonconvex problems.
AI.method: Uses an autoresearch paradigm with three components: a coding agent proposes valid tightening constraints for convex relaxations, a theory agent verifies each constraint and searches for counterexamples, and all reported bounds are certified by explicit dual-feasible points checked with rigorous interval arithmetic.
AI.result: Improved certified lower bounds for two optimization constants: first autocorrelation inequality constant (C_{6.2}) improved from 1.28 to 1.2937, and Erdős minimum-overlap constant (C_{6.5}) improved from 0.379005 to 0.37912.
AI.conclusion: The autoresearch paradigm with specialized LLM agents can effectively discover convex relaxations that yield provably better lower bounds for mathematical optimization constants, demonstrating the complementary value to previous work on upper bounds.

title: A Bayesian Filtering Approach for Learning Lagrangian Dynamics from Noisy Measurements
summary: This paper proposes a Bayesian filtering-based approach for learning the dynamics of a physical system from partial, noisy measurements. We model the system dynamics using a Lagrangian mechanics formulation. As in Lagrangian neural networks (LNNs), we parameterize the kinetic and potential energies with neural networks. The unknown external forces in the Lagrangian formulation are modeled as white Gaussian noise. The corresponding Euler--Lagrange equations then yield a continuous-time stochastic state-space model (SSM) that describes the system dynamics. The neural network parameters and system states are then jointly learned via a maximum-likelihood method using Gaussian-approximation-based Bayesian filters. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated on pendulum and Duffing oscillator examples, and its performance is compared with conventional LNNs and with approximate Bayesian filters using known system models.

AI.tldr: Bayesian filtering approach to learn physical system dynamics from partial noisy measurements using Lagrangian neural networks with unknown forces as Gaussian noise.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of learning physical system dynamics from incomplete and noisy measurements, which is common in real-world applications. Traditional Lagrangian neural networks lack handling of unknown external forces and measurement noise.
AI.method: The method combines Lagrangian mechanics with neural networks: parameterizes kinetic and potential energies using neural networks, models unknown external forces as white Gaussian noise, derives continuous-time stochastic state-space model from Euler-Lagrange equations, and jointly learns neural network parameters and system states via maximum-likelihood using Gaussian-approximation-based Bayesian filters.
AI.result: The proposed method was successfully demonstrated on pendulum and Duffing oscillator examples. It showed effective learning of system dynamics from partial, noisy measurements and performed comparably or better than conventional Lagrangian neural networks and approximate Bayesian filters using known system models.
AI.conclusion: The Bayesian filtering approach enables effective learning of physical system dynamics from partial, noisy measurements by incorporating uncertainty modeling of unknown forces and using probabilistic inference methods, offering improved robustness compared to conventional approaches.

title: Agentic RAG-VLM: Affordance-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Self-Reflective Planning for Robotic Grasping
summary: Generalizable robotic grasping in cluttered environments is essential for deploying manipulators in unstructured human spaces, yet existing VLM-based methods rely on visual similarity for object matching, neglecting physical affordances such as handle graspability and material fragility, and operate open-loop without spatial reasoning or failure recovery, limiting their effectiveness when objects are densely packed or physically diverse. We present Agentic RAG-VLM, a unified framework that bridges VLM-based semantic understanding and physically grounded grasp execution by integrating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with vision-language models (VLMs) and agentic self-reflective planning. Agentic RAG-VLM introduces three tightly coupled components: (1) a Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG (HAA-RAG) that encodes four-dimensional affordance descriptors, including type, material, fragility, and graspable region, and retrieves strategies by functional affordance compatibility rather than visual appearance; (2) a Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner that constructs spatial relationship graphs from VLM perception and translates proximity, occlusion, and support constraints into concrete grasp parameter adjustments; and (3) an Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline with a 14-type failure taxonomy and three-level adaptive retry for closed-loop grasp refinement. Evaluated on a 12-task benchmark spanning single-grasp, interactive, and long-horizon scenarios with 360 trials per configuration, Agentic RAG-VLM achieves 78.3 percent overall success, a 53.3 percentage-point absolute gain over VLM-only baselines, demonstrating that affordance-aware retrieval, scene graph reasoning, and agentic recovery are jointly essential for robust manipulation.

AI.tldr: Agentic RAG-VLM: A unified framework combining retrieval-augmented VLMs with agentic planning for robust robotic grasping that addresses affordance awareness, spatial reasoning, and failure recovery in cluttered environments.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based grasping methods rely on visual similarity for object matching, ignoring physical affordances (graspability, fragility) and operate open-loop without spatial reasoning or failure recovery, limiting effectiveness in densely packed or physically diverse environments.
AI.method: Three tightly coupled components: (1) Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG (HAA-RAG) encoding 4D affordance descriptors (type, material, fragility, graspable regions) for functional affordance-based retrieval; (2) Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner building spatial relationship graphs from VLM perception for grasp parameter adjustments; (3) Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline with 14-type failure taxonomy and three-level adaptive retry for closed-loop refinement.
AI.result: Evaluated on 12-task benchmark with 360 trials per configuration, achieved 78.3% overall success rate, representing a 53.3 percentage-point absolute gain over VLM-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: Affordance-aware retrieval, scene graph reasoning, and agentic recovery are jointly essential for robust manipulation in cluttered environments, with the proposed framework demonstrating substantial improvements over existing VLM-based methods.

title: PPT-Eval: A Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents on PowerPoint Tasks
comment: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning, Seoul, South Korea. PMLR 306, 2026
summary: Creating and editing slides is a rich, multimodal activity that is ubiquitous in professional and educational settings, making it an ideal testbed for real-world computer-use agents. Microsoft PowerPoint is among the most widely adopted and feature-rich environments for presentation creation. We introduce PPT-Eval, a benchmark of 120 PowerPoint tasks across 12 files that cover both content creation and presentation editing scenarios, organized by difficulty. A central challenge in this domain is evaluation: tasks are complex, multimodal, and often admit many valid solutions. Moreover, today's agents frequently make only partial progress, which binary success metrics fail to capture. To address this, we design a robust evaluation framework to help create task-specific rubrics for PowerPoint tasks, taking inspiration from and building on past works for rubric-based evaluation. These rubrics award partial credit for intermediate steps, penalize unnecessary changes and poor aesthetics, and provide natural language feedback. This nuanced approach proves highly effective, achieving a Kendall's τ-b correlation of 0.77 with human judgments. We find that existing frontier agents still struggle with solving PowerPoint tasks, with strong models like Claude-4.5-Opus achieving only a 45% success rate and an average partial score of 57%. The benchmark is located at: https://microsoft.github.io/ppteval.

AI.tldr: PPT-Eval is a comprehensive benchmark of 120 PowerPoint tasks across 12 files for evaluating multimodal computer-use agents on slide creation and editing, featuring a robust rubric-based evaluation framework that accounts for partial progress.
AI.motivation: Microsoft PowerPoint is widely used for presentations, making it an ideal testbed for real-world computer-use agents. Current evaluation methods fail to capture partial progress and nuanced performance in complex, multimodal tasks that admit multiple valid solutions.
AI.method: Created PPT-Eval benchmark with 120 PowerPoint tasks across 12 files covering content creation and editing scenarios organized by difficulty. Developed a robust evaluation framework using task-specific rubrics that award partial credit for intermediate steps, penalize unnecessary changes and poor aesthetics, and provide natural language feedback.
AI.result: The evaluation framework achieves strong correlation with human judgments (Kendall's τ-b = 0.77). Current frontier agents perform poorly, with Claude-4.5-Opus achieving only 45% success rate and 57% average partial score, demonstrating significant challenges remain.
AI.conclusion: PPT-Eval provides a valuable benchmark for evaluating multimodal agents in real-world office productivity tasks, showing current agents still struggle with complex PowerPoint operations despite advances, highlighting the need for improved evaluation methods that capture nuanced performance.

title: Towards Inclusive Mobility Modeling: Characterizing and Evaluating Elderly Trajectory Patterns in Urban Systems
summary: The rapid advance of smart cities increasingly depends on trajectory data mining, yet underrepresented demographic groups, particularly the elderly, are often sparsely represented in public mobility datasets. This underrepresentation can introduce systematic bias into mobility modeling and downstream urban planning. Using the 2016-2020 Jersey City subset of the Citi Bike System Data, this study quantitatively examines how the absence of underrepresented subgroups' mobility signatures affects mobility modeling, using synthetic trajectory generation as a case study. The analysis reveals that elderly riders exhibit a structurally distinct mobility signature, including localized activity spaces (958 m vs. 1,189 m for young riders), lower mobility entropy (1.82 vs. 4.15), and asymmetric off-peak temporal patterns. To demonstrate that relying on majority-dominated training data yields biased synthetic outcomes, we further evaluate both a first-order Markov chain and a Qwen3-4B model fine-tuned with QLoRA across three demographic training settings: the full population, young riders only, and elderly riders only. Results show that models trained on majority-dominated populations systematically misrepresent elderly mobility behavior, particularly for spatial mobility metrics. The Markov model trained on the full population overestimates elderly step length by 4.5% and dwell time by 8.9%, whereas the elderly-specific model achieves substantially lower errors across most metrics. Comparisons between the Markov and LLM-based frameworks further show that higher-capability models do not necessarily improve subgroup-level fidelity under limited demographic data. These findings underscore the importance of demographic representation in mobility modeling and its downstream applications for underrepresented populations.

AI.tldr: The study examines how underrepresentation of elderly populations in mobility datasets biases trajectory modeling, showing that majority-dominated training data systematically misrepresents elderly mobility patterns even with advanced models.
AI.motivation: Underrepresented demographic groups like the elderly are sparsely represented in public mobility datasets, which can introduce systematic bias into mobility modeling and downstream urban planning applications for smart cities.
AI.method: Using 2016-2020 Jersey City Citi Bike data, researchers analyzed elderly vs. young rider mobility patterns, then evaluated synthetic trajectory generation using two models (first-order Markov chain and Qwen3-4B fine-tuned with QLoRA) across three demographic training settings: full population, young-only, and elderly-only.
AI.result: Elderly riders show distinct mobility signatures (localized activity, lower entropy, asymmetric off-peak patterns). Models trained on majority-dominated data systematically misrepresent elderly mobility, with the Markov model overestimating step length by 4.5% and dwell time by 8.9%. Higher-capability LLM models didn't improve subgroup fidelity under limited demographic data.
AI.conclusion: Demographic representation is crucial in mobility modeling - models trained on underrepresented groups' specific data yield better accuracy, and simply using more advanced models doesn't solve representation bias, highlighting the need for inclusive data collection for equitable urban planning.

title: ComplianceGate: Classifier-Gated Multi-Tier LLM Routing for Inference in Regulated Industries
summary: Large language models deployed in regulated industries operate under two constraints: compliance enforcement and cost efficiency. Personally identifiable information (PII) in user queries can reach model endpoints before the system determines whether that data should leave its jurisdictional boundary. Serving all queries through a single large model consumes full GPU capacity regardless of query complexity while offering no mechanism for geographic routing. Mixture-of-Experts architectures do not address this routing occurs between expert layers within the model after data has already arrived at the endpoint, with all experts loaded in memory regardless of query complexity. We propose a classifier-gated routing architecture that enforces compliance by design. A trained encoder classifier sits before any decoder inference, evaluating each query for complexity and data sensitivity, then routing it to an appropriately sized dense model in the appropriate geographic location. PII-containing queries route to local endpoints before any LLM computation begins, making data residency violations structurally impossible. Simple queries reach small, fast models at a fraction of the cost. Our evaluation on 600 queries demonstrates 39% median latency reduction, 33-52% cost savings depending on query distribution, and generation throughput of 122-200 tokens/second versus 50-64 for the baseline. The encoder classifier achieves 99.2% accuracy with near-perfect PII recall at 7ms inference overhead, establishing pre-inference classification as a practical path to compliance-by-design LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: A classifier-gated routing system that evaluates queries for complexity and PII before LLM inference, routing them to appropriately sized models in correct geographic locations for compliance and cost efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLMs in regulated industries face compliance constraints (data residency for PII) and cost inefficiency from using large models for all queries regardless of complexity. Current systems don't filter PII before reaching endpoints and lack geographic routing.
AI.method: Proposes a classifier-gated routing architecture with a trained encoder classifier that sits before decoder inference. It evaluates queries for complexity and PII sensitivity, then routes them to appropriately sized dense models in appropriate geographic locations. PII-containing queries route locally before any LLM computation.
AI.result: Evaluation on 600 queries shows 39% median latency reduction, 33-52% cost savings depending on query distribution, and generation throughput of 122-200 tokens/sec vs 50-64 for baseline. Classifier achieves 99.2% accuracy with near-perfect PII recall at 7ms overhead.
AI.conclusion: Pre-inference classification provides a practical path to compliance-by-design LLM deployment, structurally preventing data residency violations while improving cost efficiency and latency through intelligent query routing.

title: Long-term Traffic Simulation via Structured Autoregressive Modeling
summary: Interactive traffic simulation is a vital world model for autonomous driving. A central challenge in long-horizon simulation is modeling sustained multi-agent interactions, which is further exacerbated by dynamic token cardinality as agents continuously enter and exit the scene. In this work, we propose that the solution lies in the synergy between the architectural inductive biases and statistical priors of large-scale sequence models, e.g., Large Language Models (LLMs). Our probing experiments reveal that the transferability of attention mechanisms and the distributional consistency between motion tokens and natural language enable small-scale, heavily frozen LLMs to rapidly adapt to traffic modeling. Building on this insight, we introduce RosettaSim, a unified framework that projects scene topology, agent states, and spawning intents into a structured autoregressive stream with variable length, achieving both strong short-term accuracy and stable long-horizon simulation fidelity. Furthermore, evaluating extended rollouts presents yet another hurdle, as one-to-one agent correspondence inevitably fades over time. To address this, we introduce Retrieval-based Traffic Evaluation (RTE), which retrieves semantically similar real-world scenarios as context-aware reference anchors. Experiments on the Waymo Open Sim Agent Challenge (WOSAC) demonstrate that RosettaSim achieves state-of-the-art performance in both short- and long-term simulation. Furthermore, RTE exhibits a stronger correlation with standard metrics ($r=0.83$) than existing approaches ($r=0.74$), indicating improved alignment with long-horizon simulation fidelity.

AI.tldr: RosettaSim uses frozen LLMs to model multi-agent traffic interactions, achieving SOTA performance in long-horizon simulation with a novel evaluation method (RTE) that better aligns with simulation fidelity.
AI.motivation: The core challenge is modeling sustained multi-agent interactions in traffic simulation with dynamic token cardinality (agents entering/exiting scenes), which is difficult for current approaches.
AI.method: Proposes RosettaSim: a unified framework projecting scene topology, agent states, and spawning intents into structured autoregressive streams using heavily frozen LLMs. Uses Retrieval-based Traffic Evaluation (RTE) that retrieves semantically similar real-world scenarios as reference anchors.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on Waymo Open Sim Agent Challenge in both short- and long-term simulation. RTE shows stronger correlation with standard metrics (r=0.83 vs 0.74) indicating better alignment with long-horizon fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The synergy between LLMs' architectural inductive biases and statistical priors enables effective traffic modeling. Frozen small-scale LLMs can rapidly adapt to traffic modeling, and RTE provides better evaluation for long-horizon simulations.

title: AETDICE: Unified Framework and Offline Optimization for Nonlinear Multi-Objective RL
summary: Optimizing nonlinear preferences in multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL) is essential for capturing complex trade-offs like risk aversion or fairness. However, such non-linearity has historically bifurcated nonlinear MORL objectives into two distinct paradigms: Scalarized Expected Return (SER) and Expected Scalarized Return (ESR). While SER requires global-level optimization and ESR requires non-Markovian policies, leading to fragmented optimization strategies, we bridge this divide through the Aggregation-Expectation-Transformation (AET) framework. By unifying both criteria through a tripartite decomposition of scalarization, AET provides a principled foundation for general nonlinear MORL. Building on this framework, we propose AETDICE, a tractable offline RL algorithm for AET objectives. By utilizing DICE-style density-ratio estimation in an augmented state space, AETDICE enables sample-based optimization from static datasets. Our framework resolves long-standing barriers and captures respective trade-offs induced by AET framework, which existing methods fail to address.

AI.tldr: The paper bridges the SER-ESR divide in nonlinear MORL with AET framework and proposes AETDICE for offline optimization.
AI.motivation: Nonlinear preferences in MORL are essential for capturing complex trade-offs like risk aversion or fairness, but historical non-linearity has bifurcated objectives into SER (needs global optimization) and ESR (needs non-Markovian policies), creating fragmented optimization strategies.
AI.method: 1) AET framework unifies SER and ESR through tripartite decomposition of scalarization; 2) AETDICE algorithm uses DICE-style density-ratio estimation in augmented state space for sample-based optimization from static datasets.
AI.result: The framework resolves long-standing barriers and captures respective trade-offs induced by AET framework that existing methods fail to address, providing principled foundation for general nonlinear MORL.
AI.conclusion: AET framework bridges the SER-ESR divide in nonlinear MORL, and AETDICE enables tractable offline optimization from static datasets, solving historical fragmentation in optimization strategies.

title: Thinking Before Retrieving: Robust Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval via Strategic Planning and Self-Criticism
summary: Composed image retrieval requires identifying a target image from a gallery by integrating a reference image with a textual modification instruction. In a training-free zero-shot setting, this task relies on constructing a retrieval-oriented textual query within a frozen vision--language embedding space at inference time. Existing approaches predominantly rely on a single-pass generation strategy that fuses the reference context and modification text into a unified description. This strategy makes it difficult to detect or correct semantic distortions and omissions during generation. Consequently, the preservation of reference attributes and the integration of textual requirements interfere with each other, which degrades retrieval precision. To address these challenges, we introduce PEC-CIR, a training-free framework that structures query construction as a multi-stage reasoning pipeline. The framework operates through a Planner--Executor--Critic architecture where the Planner extracts explicit constraints, the Executor generates multiple candidate target descriptions, and the Critic evaluates these candidates based on constraint compliance. By reframing query construction as a staged inference process instead of a single-pass output, PEC-CIR reduces the propagation of generative errors by explicitly evaluating candidate queries before retrieval, thereby improving retrieval stability.

AI.tldr: PEC-CIR is a training-free framework for composed image retrieval that uses a Planner-Executor-Critic architecture to improve retrieval precision by reframing query construction as a multi-stage reasoning pipeline instead of single-pass generation.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for composed image retrieval in zero-shot settings rely on single-pass generation strategies that fuse reference context and modification text into unified descriptions. This makes it difficult to detect/correct semantic distortions and omissions, causing interference between preserving reference attributes and integrating textual requirements, which degrades retrieval precision.
AI.method: PEC-CIR introduces a training-free framework that structures query construction as a multi-stage reasoning pipeline with Planner-Executor-Critic architecture: Planner extracts explicit constraints, Executor generates multiple candidate target descriptions, and Critic evaluates candidates based on constraint compliance.
AI.result: By reframing query construction as a staged inference process instead of single-pass output, PEC-CIR reduces propagation of generative errors by explicitly evaluating candidate queries before retrieval, thereby improving retrieval stability.
AI.conclusion: The multi-stage reasoning approach in PEC-CIR addresses the limitations of single-pass generation strategies in composed image retrieval, enhancing retrieval precision through explicit constraint evaluation and error reduction during query construction.

title: Transformers as Bayesian In-Context Experimenters: Smoothness-Adaptive Efficient ATE Estimation
summary: Adaptive experiments for average treatment effects (ATE) require randomized allocations balancing valid inference with statistical efficiency. The oracle design is a covariate-dependent Neyman rule governed by unknown arm-conditional outcome variances. We investigate whether this sequential variance-estimation and allocation process can be amortized via in-context learning. We introduce Bayesian in-context experimenters: transformer policies trained to imitate a Bayesian posterior Neyman teacher. The teacher updates nonparametric beliefs over potential outcomes using experimental history to assign posterior Neyman treatment probabilities. This design converges to the oracle rule, supporting efficient ATE inference. Transformers constructively implement this mapping through attention-based sufficient statistics and projected gradient descent, imitating Bayesian updating for Gaussian-series priors. To address unknown outcome smoothness, we combine smoothness-indexed experimenters using a mixture-of-experts transformer. The gate acts as a hierarchical posterior over smoothness classes, concentrating on near-oracle experts. By bounding the complexity of the transformer class, we prove this amortized policy can be learned via empirical risk minimization using supervised pretraining. Experiments confirm accurate teacher imitation, adaptive allocation, and improved ATE precision over baselines.

AI.tldr: Transformers can be trained via imitation learning to perform adaptive experiments for ATE estimation, achieving near-oracle allocation efficiency through attention-based Bayesian posterior approximation and smoothness-indexed mixture-of-experts.
AI.motivation: Adaptive experiments for ATE estimation require balancing valid inference with statistical efficiency. The optimal oracle design (Neyman rule) depends on unknown arm-conditional outcome variances, creating a sequential variance-estimation and allocation challenge that could benefit from amortization via in-context learning.
AI.method: Introduce Bayesian in-context experimenters: transformer policies trained to imitate a Bayesian posterior Neyman teacher. The teacher uses experimental history to update nonparametric beliefs over potential outcomes and assign posterior Neyman treatment probabilities. Transformers implement this mapping through attention-based sufficient statistics and projected gradient descent. For unknown outcome smoothness, combine smoothness-indexed experimenters using mixture-of-experts transformer with gate acting as hierarchical posterior over smoothness classes.
AI.result: The design converges to the oracle rule, supporting efficient ATE inference. Bounded complexity of transformer class enables learning via empirical risk minimization with supervised pretraining. Experiments show accurate teacher imitation, adaptive allocation, and improved ATE precision over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Transformers can successfully amortize the sequential variance-estimation and allocation process for adaptive ATE experiments through in-context learning, achieving near-oracle performance via imitation of Bayesian posterior Neyman allocation rules.

title: Agentic-Ideation: Sample Efficient Agentic Trajectories Synthesis for Scientific Ideation Agents
summary: Ideation plays a pivotal role in scientific discovery. Recent LLM, especially AI Scientist systems, show promising potential for automated ideation. However, existing approaches predominantly rely on pre-defined agentic workflows. This constraint severely limits the flexibility required to navigate the vast search space of scientific literature and the complex action space of research reasoning. Recently, training Agentic LLMs has emerged as a promising direction, offering flexible reasoning frameworks and the capability for autonomous tool utilization. However, there remains a non-trivial challenge: applying previous agentic data synthesis methods to scientific ideation suffers from prohibitively high data synthesis cost. To bridge this gap, we propose Agentic-Ideation, a novel framework comprising an automated trajectory synthesis pipeline and a specialized agentic LLM trained for scientific ideation. Specifically, we first define a comprehensive tool space incorporating three external tools and three cognitive tools. Then we introduce an Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis strategy. By leveraging a reference idea as oracle guidance, this approach steers the multi-agent system to efficiently reconstruct the logical reasoning and tool invocation paths, transforming aimless trial-and-error into directed trajectory generation. Finally, we train the agent on these synthesized trajectories, employing a masking strategy on tool execution results. This ensures the model focuses on decision-making logic without interference from external feedback. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the SOTA workflow-based baseline by \textbf{11.91\%} in overall quality. Furthermore, our approach improves the sample efficiency of high-quality data synthesis by \textbf{over 10$\times$}.

AI.tldr: Agentic-Ideation: A framework for training agentic LLMs for scientific ideation using efficient trajectory synthesis with oracle guidance, outperforming workflow-based baselines by 11.91%.
AI.motivation: Existing AI Scientist systems rely on pre-defined workflows that limit flexibility for navigating scientific literature and research reasoning. Training agentic LLMs offers better flexibility but suffers from prohibitively high data synthesis costs for scientific ideation tasks.
AI.method: Proposes Agentic-Ideation framework with: 1) Comprehensive tool space (3 external + 3 cognitive tools), 2) Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis using reference ideas to steer multi-agent systems for efficient trajectory generation, 3) Training agent on synthesized trajectories with masking strategy on tool execution results to focus on decision-making logic.
AI.result: Method outperforms state-of-the-art workflow-based baseline by 11.91% in overall quality. Improves sample efficiency of high-quality data synthesis by over 10x compared to previous approaches.
AI.conclusion: Agentic-Ideation successfully addresses the data synthesis cost challenge for scientific ideation by introducing an efficient oracle-guided trajectory synthesis pipeline, enabling effective training of agentic LLMs for flexible scientific discovery.

title: ISM:Self-Improving Strategy Memory for Continual Mathematical Reasoning
comment: 3rd AI for Math Workshop at ICML 2026 Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning
summary: We propose Intelligent Schema Memory (ISM), a self-evolving memory-augmented system that improves mathematical reasoning for a frozen LLM under continual learning with hard episodic resets. ISM maintains a compact, self-refined bank of strategy schemas learned from both successful and failed episodes, with symbolic tools that check intermediate steps and certify answers.Without updating model parameters, ISM outperforms passive, retrieval, and reflection baselines on MATH-Hard and OlympiadBench, using 64% and 86% fewer schemas respectively than the strongest passive baseline. These results show that small, actively maintained, and verified strategy memories can support reliable continual mathematical reasoning under strict episodic isolation.

AI.tldr: ISM is a self-evolving memory system that enhances mathematical reasoning in frozen LLMs through continual learning with episodic resets, using a compact schema bank and verification tools without parameter updates.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of enabling continual mathematical reasoning in frozen LLMs without parameter updates, particularly under strict episodic isolation where traditional learning approaches fail due to catastrophic forgetting.
AI.method: ISM maintains a compact, self-refined bank of strategy schemas learned from both successful and failed episodes. It uses symbolic tools to check intermediate steps and certify answers. The system operates through hard episodic resets while keeping model parameters frozen.
AI.result: ISM outperforms passive, retrieval, and reflection baselines on MATH-Hard and OlympiadBench, achieving better performance while using 64% and 86% fewer schemas respectively than the strongest passive baseline.
AI.conclusion: Small, actively maintained, and verified strategy memories can effectively support reliable continual mathematical reasoning under strict episodic isolation, demonstrating that memory augmentation can overcome limitations of parameter-frozen models in continual learning scenarios.

title: Delta-JEPA: Learning Action-Sensitive World Models via Latent Difference Decoding
summary: Learning visual world models for planning requires compact latent dynamics that remain sensitive to actions, yet reconstruction-free joint-embedding objectives can collapse to action-insensitive representations. We propose Delta-JEPA, an end-to-end reconstruction-free world model that augments latent forward prediction with a Latent Difference Action Decoder (LDAD). Unlike inverse decoders that infer actions from concatenated endpoint embeddings, LDAD reconstructs the executed action from the latent displacement between consecutive observations. This displacement-level supervision directly regularizes transition geometry: adjacent embeddings cannot collapse without losing action information, and different actions are encouraged to induce distinguishable latent changes for rollout-based planning. Delta-JEPA uses only latent prediction and action reconstruction, avoiding pixel reconstruction and distribution-matching regularizers. Across four visual continuous-control tasks, Delta-JEPA improves planning over JEPA-based and representation-learning world model baselines. Ablations show that displacement-based action decoding is consistently more effective than endpoint concatenation, and action-sensitivity analyses show clearer action-conditioned latent responses. These results indicate that supervising latent differences is a simple and effective mechanism for collapse-resistant and action-sensitive world model learning.

AI.tldr: Delta-JEPA improves visual world models for planning by adding latent displacement action decoding to prevent representation collapse while maintaining action sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Current reconstruction-free joint-embedding world models can collapse to action-insensitive representations, which is problematic for planning tasks that require compact yet action-sensitive latent dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes Delta-JEPA with Latent Difference Action Decoder (LDAD) that reconstructs executed actions from latent displacement between consecutive observations instead of inferring actions from concatenated endpoint embeddings.
AI.result: Delta-JEPA improves planning performance across four visual continuous-control tasks compared to JEPA-based and representation-learning baselines, with ablations showing displacement-based action decoding is more effective than endpoint concatenation.
AI.conclusion: Supervising latent differences is a simple and effective mechanism for collapse-resistant and action-sensitive world model learning, enabling better planning in visual continuous-control tasks.

title: Probing Memorization of Tabular In-Context Learning
comment: Accepted at 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data, 2026
summary: Large tabular models (LTMs), i.e., tabular foundation models leveraging in-context learning (ICL), achieve state-of-the-art performance on tabular tasks. While LLMs are known to unintentionally memorize training data, the memorization dynamics of LTMs remain largely unexplored. We investigate the potential for parametric memorization in tabular ICL. We introduce ICLMEM, a probing framework designed to separate context-based predictions from parametric memorization. Our zero-information multiple-choice context strips away valid contextual patterns to force the model to fall back on its parametric memory. Our controlled fine-tuning setup establishes membership ground truth and accounts for common pitfalls, e.g., distribution shift, feature contamination, base-rate fallacy, and the pre-trained base model acts as reference to calibrate for sample difficulty. Our controlled evaluation on a leading real-world-trained LTM detects moderate memorization signals in 8 out of 10 tasks ($\text{AUC}$ up to $0.67$ and TPR at $1\%$ FPR $>0.1$). Notably, memorization signals are strongest for low-cardinality and binary tasks. However, they largely vanish under realistic training conditions. Our findings show LTM memorization signals under specific circumstances (single-task fine-tuning with fixed samples across many epochs and small query size). To protect sensitive data, appropriate measures must be taken, which we discuss.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ICLMEM framework to detect parametric memorization in Large Tabular Models (LTMs) under controlled settings, finding moderate memorization signals but limited real-world impact.
AI.motivation: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are known to unintentionally memorize training data, the memorization dynamics of Large Tabular Models (LTMs) using in-context learning remain unexplored, raising concerns about data privacy and security.
AI.method: The paper introduces ICLMEM, a probing framework that separates context-based predictions from parametric memorization using zero-information multiple-choice contexts and controlled fine-tuning setup. The framework includes membership ground truth establishment, controls for distribution shift, feature contamination, base-rate fallacy, and uses pre-trained base model as reference for sample difficulty calibration.
AI.result: Evaluation on a leading real-world-trained LTM detected moderate memorization signals in 8 out of 10 tasks (AUC up to 0.67 and TPR at 1% FPR >0.1), with strongest signals for low-cardinality and binary tasks. However, memorization largely vanishes under realistic training conditions.
AI.conclusion: LTMs show memorization signals only under specific circumstances (single-task fine-tuning with fixed samples across many epochs and small query size). Appropriate privacy protection measures are needed for sensitive data, and the paper discusses potential safeguards.

title: Embodied CAD: Solver-Grounded LLM Agents for Parametric B-Rep Assembly Modeling
comment: This paper contains 12 pages, 7 figures. This is an original unpublished manuscript submitted to the arXiv preprint server, with no prior publication or conference presentation
summary: Large language models can write plausible CAD scripts, but reliable industrial CAD modeling requires more than syntactically valid code: every feature, placement, and assembly relation must be accepted by an exact geometric kernel while remaining editable as parametric boundary representation geometry. We present Embodied CAD, solver-grounded LLM agents for parametric B-Rep assembly modeling. Instead of generating a complete script in one pass, the agent iteratively selects actions from a stratified L0-L4 CAD skill library, resolves them into typed geometric operations, executes them in a CAD backend, and uses solver feedback to plan, repair, and learn. The framework combines action grammar constraints, deterministic parameter resolution, and solver-derived rewards for supervised warm-up and GRPO-style refinement. We evaluate Embodied CAD on multi-step mechanical, industrial equipment, and mold-oriented assembly tasks using solver-aligned metrics: executable rate, skill accuracy, operation-family accuracy, exact policy accuracy, and task completion success. The results show that solver-grounded planning executes all strong-planner workflows in the current benchmark, while learned controllers reach high executable rates and expose the remaining gap between valid tool calls and exact long-horizon policy prediction.

AI.tldr: Embodied CAD: LLM agents for parametric B-Rep assembly modeling that iteratively execute CAD operations with geometric kernel feedback, achieving high executable rates through solver-grounded planning and learning.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs can generate syntactically valid CAD scripts but lack reliability for industrial CAD modeling, which requires exact geometric kernel acceptance and parametric editability while handling complex assembly relations.
AI.method: Embodied CAD uses solver-grounded LLM agents with a stratified L0-L4 CAD skill library. Agents iteratively select actions, resolve them into typed geometric operations, execute in a CAD backend, and use solver feedback for planning, repair, and learning. The framework combines action grammar constraints, deterministic parameter resolution, and solver-derived rewards with supervised warm-up and GRPO-style refinement.
AI.result: Evaluation on mechanical, industrial equipment, and mold-oriented assembly tasks shows solver-grounded planning executes all strong-planner workflows in the benchmark, while learned controllers achieve high executable rates. The study reveals a gap between valid tool calls and exact long-horizon policy prediction.
AI.conclusion: Embodied CAD demonstrates the effectiveness of solver-grounded LLM agents for reliable industrial CAD modeling, though challenges remain in bridging the gap between local executable actions and complete, exact long-term policy prediction for complex assembly tasks.

title: Learning Gaussian Graphical Models from a Glauber Trajectory Without Mixing
summary:
We study the task of learning the structure of a $d$-sparse Gaussian graphical model on $n$ variables from a single trajectory of Glauber dynamics. Beyond algorithmic considerations, many applications present temporally correlated observations rather than i.i.d.\ samples. In the classical i.i.d.\ setting, under comparably general sparsity and minimum edge-strength assumptions, sublinear-in-$n$ sample guarantees are known, but achieving them in polynomial-time remains open. Motivated in part by this gap, we give a polynomial-time algorithm that recovers the conditional-independence graph from a single Glauber trajectory, with a trajectory-length guarantee that does not depend on the mixing time.
  Technically, our algorithm has three components. First, we estimate the conditional variances and rescale the trajectory to reduce to the unit-diagonal case, without changing the underlying graph. Second, we design a local edge test that extracts adjacency information from short update windows by isolating pairwise influence. Third, we aggregate these local statistics using a robust median-based estimator, and prove accuracy despite temporal dependence arising from a single trajectory.

AI.tldr: A polynomial-time algorithm that learns sparse Gaussian graphical model structure from a single trajectory of Glauber dynamics, with sample complexity independent of mixing time.
AI.motivation: Many real-world applications have temporally correlated observations rather than i.i.d. samples. In classical i.i.d. settings, sublinear sample guarantees exist but achieving them in polynomial time remains open. This gap motivates developing efficient algorithms for learning from dependent data like Glauber dynamics trajectories.
AI.method: Three-component algorithm: 1) Estimate conditional variances and rescale trajectory to unit-diagonal case without changing graph structure; 2) Design local edge test that extracts adjacency information from short update windows by isolating pairwise influence; 3) Aggregate local statistics using robust median-based estimator to handle temporal dependence.
AI.result: A polynomial-time algorithm that recovers the conditional-independence graph from a single Glauber trajectory with trajectory-length guarantee that does not depend on the mixing time. This overcomes the challenge of temporal dependence in single-trajectory learning.
AI.conclusion: The work provides an efficient solution for learning sparse Gaussian graphical models from dependent data, bridging the gap between theoretical sample complexity guarantees and practical polynomial-time algorithms for temporally correlated observations.

title: Spatial Reasoning via Modality Switching Between Language and Symbolic Representation
summary: Human reasoning is inherently multimodal: when problems become difficult, we rarely think in words alone. We often externalize our reasoning by sketching diagrams or drawing grids to understand the underlying conceptual structure and avoid mistakes. Building on this premise, our research investigates: (a) whether grounding multi-hop textual-spatial stories into geometry-aware modalities, such as layouts or grids, improves reasoning compared to natural language-based inference; and (b) whether a model can decide when to rely on natural language reasoning and when to switch to a structured modality. We address these questions by introducing a switching metric based on trustworthiness and complexity signals, which estimates when grounding a spatial story into structure is likely to improve performance. This takes a first step toward principled modality selection in Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning. Across our settings, switching from natural language-based reasoning to a grid-based representation improves LLM performance by up to 42\%, highlighting the importance of modality choice in shaping reasoning outcomes.

AI.tldr: LLMs can improve reasoning by switching from natural language to structured grid representations for spatial problems, achieving up to 42% performance gains through principled modality selection.
AI.motivation: Human reasoning is multimodal - we often use diagrams or grids for difficult problems. The research investigates whether grounding spatial stories into geometric structures improves reasoning over pure natural language, and whether models can decide when to switch modalities.
AI.method: Introduces a switching metric based on trustworthiness and complexity signals that estimates when grounding spatial stories into structured representations (grids/layouts) will improve performance, enabling principled modality selection.
AI.result: Switching from natural language reasoning to grid-based representations improves LLM performance by up to 42% across the experimental settings, demonstrating the importance of modality choice.
AI.conclusion: This work takes a first step toward principled modality selection in LLM reasoning, showing that structured geometric representations can significantly enhance performance on spatial reasoning tasks compared to natural language alone.

title: TDGT: A Tabular Data Generation Toolkit supporting adaptive GPU-accelerated Bayesian mixture models, diffusion-based models, and latent-space generative modeling
comment: 47 pages (33 main body, 14 pages supplementary material), 30 figures (12 figures in the main body, 18 supplementary figures), 9 tables (3 tables in the main body, 6 supplementary tables)
summary: The growing demand for privacy-preserving data sharing has positioned synthetic data generation as a critical component of responsible AI workflows. Despite notable advances in generative modeling, existing solutions often lack integration of adaptive generation strategies, multi-metric evaluation, and accessible end-to-end generators within a unified web-based toolkit. In this work, we introduce TDGT (Tabular Data Generation Toolkit), a web-based toolkit for synthetic tabular data generation and fidelity assessment. TDGT introduces the Adaptive Bayesian Mixture Synthesizer (ABMS), a novel algorithm that autonomously determines the optimal number of mixture components through iterative cluster quality optimization, eliminating the need for manual hyperparameter configuration. Building upon ABMS, we further propose VAE-ABMS, a hybrid architecture that couples Variational Autoencoder-based latent space learning with adaptive Bayesian mixture synthesis, enabling high-fidelity generation of complex, nonlinear tabular distributions. For large-scale scenarios, TDGT provides a GPU-accelerated variant of ABMS leveraging CUDA-based k-means clustering and Gaussian mixture fitting. Synthetic data fidelity is assessed through eleven statistical fidelity metrics spanning distributional divergence, structural correlation, and sample-level similarity, complemented by privacy risk indicators including k-anonymity scoring and disclosure rate estimation. The web-based toolkit supports a real-time streaming interface with interactive Plotly-based visualizations. TDGT is assessed across datasets from healthcare, socioeconomic modeling, and cybersecurity domains, demonstrating consistent generation fidelity and statistical coherence across heterogeneous feature types and data scales.

AI.tldr: TDGT is a web-based toolkit for synthetic tabular data generation featuring adaptive Bayesian mixture synthesizers, hybrid VAE integration, GPU acceleration, and comprehensive fidelity/privacy evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: The growing need for privacy-preserving data sharing in responsible AI workflows, coupled with the lack of integrated solutions combining adaptive generation strategies, multi-metric evaluation, and accessible end-to-end generators in unified web-based toolkits.
AI.method:
1. Adaptive Bayesian Mixture Synthesizer (ABMS) - novel algorithm that autonomously determines optimal mixture components through iterative cluster quality optimization
2. VAE-ABMS - hybrid architecture combining Variational Autoencoder latent space learning with adaptive Bayesian mixture synthesis
3. GPU-accelerated variant of ABMS using CUDA-based k-means clustering and Gaussian mixture fitting
4. Eleven statistical fidelity metrics for distributional divergence, structural correlation, and sample-level similarity
5. Privacy risk indicators including k-anonymity scoring and disclosure rate estimation
6. Web-based toolkit with real-time streaming interface and interactive Plotly visualizations
