AI.result: TDGT was evaluated across healthcare, socioeconomic modeling, and cybersecurity datasets, demonstrating consistent generation fidelity and statistical coherence across heterogeneous feature types and data scales. The toolkit successfully integrates all components into a unified web-based platform.
AI.conclusion: TDGT provides a comprehensive, accessible solution for synthetic tabular data generation that addresses current gaps in adaptive generation strategies, multi-metric evaluation, and end-to-end integration, making privacy-preserving data sharing more practical for responsible AI workflows.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models on Floating-Point Error Classification
summary: This paper investigates the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to detect and classify floating-point errors statically in software code. We introduce InterFLOPBench, a benchmark of 90 C kernels with 1 130 test samples designed to evaluate LLMs across six categories of floating-point error: cancellation, comparison, division by zero, overflow, underflow and NaN, compared across 14 LLMs. The evaluation framework treats floating-point error detection as a multi-label classification problem and employs the F1-score metric to measure performance. Results demonstrate that latest models (Qwen 3 32b, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Phi 4 Reasoning, DeepSeek R1T2, and gpt-oss 20b and 120b) achieve a performance greater than 0.88 overall F1-score. Performance varies between error categories, between explicit operations such as division by zero (Average F1-score: 0.8479) and more subtle numerical phenomena such as underflow (Average F1-score: 0.6059) and cancellation (Average F1-score: 0.6164).

AI.tldr: LLMs can effectively detect floating-point errors in code, with newer models achieving >0.88 F1-score overall, though performance varies by error type.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) for static detection and classification of floating-point errors in software code, addressing the need for automated error detection in numerical computing.
AI.method: Created InterFLOPBench benchmark with 90 C kernels and 1,130 test samples across six floating-point error categories. Evaluated 14 LLMs using multi-label classification approach with F1-score as performance metric.
AI.result: Latest LLMs (Qwen 3 32b, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Phi 4 Reasoning, DeepSeek R1T2, and gpt-oss models) achieve overall F1-score >0.88. Performance varies: explicit operations like division by zero (0.8479 F1) perform better than subtle numerical phenomena like underflow (0.6059) and cancellation (0.6164).
AI.conclusion: LLMs show strong potential for static floating-point error detection, with state-of-the-art models achieving high accuracy, though challenging error categories require further improvement.

title: The Calibration Turn in AI-Assisted Research: A Conceptual and Methodological Framework for Evidence-Licensed Claims
comment: 42 pages, 4 figures. Companion code and synthetic simulation artifacts: https://github.com/Li-Hongmin/calibration-turn-ai-assisted-research
summary: AI-assisted research has entered a stage in which the central question is not only whether systems can generate hypotheses, run experiments, or produce manuscripts, but whether their scientific claims are calibrated to the evidence that supports them. This Perspective-style paper develops a conceptual and methodological framework for evidence-licensed claims in AI-assisted research. Motivated by representative routes including specialized scientific foundation models, LLM research assistants, multi-agent co-scientists, AI Scientist pipelines, mathematical discovery agents, and self-driving laboratories, it represents AI-assisted research as five operators: hypothesis generation, model-mediated consequence derivation, external validation, belief update, and claim calibration. The central claim is that calibration is not merely cautious wording but a mechanism for managing scientific assertion rights: evidence licenses some forms of speech and withholds others. The paper distinguishes linguistic, consequence-based, interventional, and evidence-licensed semantics; defines the claim-evidence gap and epistemic debt; and treats minimal structural reconstruction across heterogeneous outputs as an upward form of claim calibration. AISim-Cal is included as an illustrative synthetic dynamics exercise, not as an empirical forecast or benchmark. The resulting principles are: no claim without license, validation does not determine claim level, and automation amplifies the need for calibration. Reliable AI-assisted research is therefore evaluated as a loop that generates hypotheses, derives testable consequences, accepts independent adjudication, updates beliefs, and outputs only evidence-licensed claims.

AI.tldr: A framework for ensuring AI-generated scientific claims are properly calibrated to supporting evidence, treating claim calibration as a mechanism for managing scientific assertion rights rather than just cautious wording.
AI.motivation: AI-assisted research has advanced beyond basic capabilities like hypothesis generation and manuscript production to the critical issue of whether scientific claims are properly calibrated to supporting evidence. The field needs principles for evidence-licensed claims as AI takes on roles in specialized scientific foundation models, LLM research assistants, multi-agent systems, AI Scientist pipelines, mathematical discovery agents, and self-driving laboratories.
AI.method: Develops a conceptual framework representing AI-assisted research as five operators: hypothesis generation, model-mediated consequence derivation, external validation, belief update, and claim calibration. Introduces distinctions between linguistic, consequence-based, interventional, and evidence-licensed semantics. Defines concepts like claim-evidence gap and epistemic debt, and treats minimal structural reconstruction as upward claim calibration. Uses AISim-Cal as an illustrative synthetic dynamics exercise.
AI.result: Establishes three key principles: no claim without license, validation does not determine claim level, and automation amplifies the need for calibration. Defines reliable AI-assisted research as a loop that: generates hypotheses, derives testable consequences, accepts independent adjudication, updates beliefs, and outputs only evidence-licensed claims.
AI.conclusion: Claim calibration is not merely about cautious wording but a fundamental mechanism for managing scientific assertion rights - evidence licenses some forms of speech and withholds others. For AI-assisted research to be reliable, it must systematically ensure claims are properly calibrated to available evidence, with the proposed framework providing necessary methodological guidance.

title: HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: Multi-Hop Question Answering Corpus for Historical Research, Parliamentary Debates from the French Third Republic (1870-1940)
summary: We present HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: a French-language dataset of multi-hop historical questions derived from parliamentary debates and newspapers of the French Third Republic. Designed in collaboration with a historian, the corpus captures complex reasoning patterns typical of historical inquiry, including cross-source synthesis, temporal reasoning, and the integration of sparse evidence. The dataset is made of 1782 questions and emphasizes multi-hop connections across heterogeneous historical documents, providing a resource for evaluating retrieval-augmented and large language model systems in domain-specific contexts. We describe the methodology for constructing the corpus, including the selection and alignment of sources, question validation, and metadata integration. While the dataset focuses on French historical documents, our methodology can be readily adapted to other languages and national corpora. Finally, we demonstrate how the corpus can support realistic evaluation scenarios for multi-hop question answering, bridging the gap between NLP benchmarks and the needs of historical scholarship.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic, a French multi-hop historical QA dataset built from parliamentary debates and newspapers of the French Third Republic, designed in collaboration with historians to capture complex historical reasoning patterns.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a domain-specific historical question answering dataset that bridges the gap between standard NLP benchmarks and the actual needs of historical scholarship, particularly addressing the complex reasoning patterns historians use, such as cross-source synthesis, temporal reasoning, and integration of sparse evidence.
AI.method: The method involves constructing a corpus of 1782 questions through collaboration with a historian, focusing on multi-hop connections across heterogeneous historical documents (parliamentary debates and newspapers). The methodology includes source selection and alignment, question validation, and metadata integration.
AI.result: The result is HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: a French-language dataset that emphasizes multi-hop connections across historical documents and supports realistic evaluation scenarios for retrieval-augmented and large language model systems in domain-specific historical contexts.
AI.conclusion: The dataset provides a valuable resource for evaluating multi-hop question answering systems in historical contexts, and while focused on French documents, the methodology is adaptable to other languages and national corpora, helping bridge NLP research with historical scholarship needs.

title: Revisiting the Volume Hypothesis
summary: Modern deep neural networks often contain far more parameters than needed to fit their training data, yet they achieve impressive generalization. A common explanation for this success is the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent (SGD). An alternative volume hypothesis posits that, within low training-loss regions, loss-landscape basins leading to strong generalization occupy much larger regions of weight space than basins that generalize poorly, and therefore SGD is simply more likely to land in the former. Recent experimental explorations of this idea present seemingly contradictory results. While in one set of experiments randomly sampling the network weights until achieving zero training error yielded poor generalization, molecular dynamics density estimates supported the volume hypothesis. We observe that these experiments were performed at different dataset size regimes, and explore an intermediate regime using the Replica Exchange Wang-Landau algorithm to estimate the joint density of states over training and test accuracies in binary networks. Across several architectures and datasets, we show that the generalization advantage of gradient learning over random sampling training generally diminishes as the training data size grows, suggesting a resolution of the paradox.

AI.tldr: The paper resolves a paradox in neural network generalization by showing that the advantage of gradient learning over random sampling diminishes with more training data.
AI.motivation: There's a contradiction between two experimental findings about neural network generalization: one showing random sampling of weights with zero training error yields poor generalization, while another using molecular dynamics supports the volume hypothesis that good generalization basins occupy larger weight space regions. This paradox needs resolution.
AI.method: The authors use Replica Exchange Wang-Landau algorithm to estimate the joint density of states over training and test accuracies in binary networks, exploring an intermediate dataset size regime between the contradictory experiments, across several architectures and datasets.
AI.result: The generalization advantage of gradient learning over random sampling training generally diminishes as training data size grows, providing a resolution to the paradox by showing the experiments were performed in different dataset size regimes.
AI.conclusion: The volume hypothesis and SGD's implicit bias explanations for generalization can be reconciled by considering training data size - the advantage of gradient methods over random sampling decreases with more data, resolving the apparent contradiction between previous experiments.

title: CryoACE: An Atom-centric Framework for Accurate and Automated Model Building in Cryo-EM
summary: Protein automodeling from cryo-EM density maps faces unique challenges in enforcing physicochemical validity and managing conformational heterogeneity. Current solvers are often limited to static predictions or require computationally intensive heuristic searches. We present CryoACE, an end-to-end framework that reconstructs precise atomic graphs for both homogeneous and heterogeneous structures. Our method features two key innovations: an atom-centric reconstruction paradigm, where density features are sampled directly at atomic coordinates and iteratively recycled to refine structures, replacing expensive voxel convolutions for efficient multimodal fusion; and a training-free guidance mechanism that leverages predicted local resolution priors to resolve dynamic ambiguity. Validated on a newly constructed high-quality dataset, CryoACE significantly outperforms existing baselines on static benchmarks and, for the first time, unveils atomic-level dynamic conformations on complex real-world datasets like EMPIAR-10345 without relying on pre-built static structures.

AI.tldr: CryoACE is an end-to-end framework that reconstructs precise atomic graphs from cryo-EM density maps for both homogeneous and heterogeneous protein structures using atom-centric sampling and training-free guidance.
AI.motivation: Current methods for protein modeling from cryo-EM density maps struggle with enforcing physicochemical validity, managing conformational heterogeneity, and often rely on static predictions or computationally intensive heuristic searches.
AI.method: CryoACE features: 1) atom-centric reconstruction where density features are sampled directly at atomic coordinates and iteratively recycled to refine structures (replacing expensive voxel convolutions), and 2) a training-free guidance mechanism that uses predicted local resolution priors to resolve dynamic ambiguity.
AI.result: CryoACE significantly outperforms existing baselines on static benchmarks and, for the first time, reveals atomic-level dynamic conformations on complex real-world datasets (like EMPIAR-10345) without relying on pre-built static structures.
AI.conclusion: CryoACE represents a significant advancement in cryo-EM protein modeling, enabling precise atomic reconstruction for both static and dynamic structures through efficient multimodal fusion and resolution-aware guidance.

title: Sequential sparse Gaussian process quantile regression
summary: Quantile regression aims to estimate the conditional quantiles of a response variable from observed data. In a Bayesian setting, Gaussian process quantile regression provides uncertainty quantification but faces significant computational challenges due to the nonconjugacy of the asymmetric Laplace likelihood and the cost of posterior inference. We develop a sparse Gaussian process framework in which the quantile function is represented through a reduced set of inducing variables and posterior inference is performed using a Laplace approximation. A decomposition of the predictive uncertainty into conditional-prior and posterior-induced variance components is then exploited to drive two complementary adaptive mechanisms: inducing-input infilling and data acquisition. These mechanisms are combined within a sequential algorithm that allocates computational effort toward the dominant source of predictive uncertainty and adaptively controls model complexity. Numerical experiments on benchmark problems demonstrate the accuracy of the Laplace approximation, the benefits of variance-based inducing-input placement, and the effectiveness of the proposed sequential enrichment strategy compared with predefined data-acquisition strategies.

AI.tldr: A novel sparse Gaussian process framework for Bayesian quantile regression that adaptively controls computational resources through uncertainty decomposition and sequential enrichment strategies.
AI.motivation: Bayesian Gaussian process quantile regression suffers from computational challenges due to nonconjugacy of asymmetric Laplace likelihood and expensive posterior inference, needing more efficient methods.
AI.method: Sparse Gaussian process framework using inducing variables with Laplace approximation for posterior inference, decomposing predictive uncertainty into components to drive adaptive inducing-input infilling and data acquisition.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show accurate Laplace approximation, benefits of variance-based inducing-input placement, and effectiveness of sequential enrichment strategy over predefined approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides efficient Bayesian quantile regression by adaptively allocating computational effort based on uncertainty components, improving model performance with controlled complexity.

title: Optimization Algorithms for Joint OFDM Waveform Design and RIS Configuration in 6G Networks: From Convex Relaxation to Foundation Models
summary: Joint OFDM-RIS optimization for 6G is a mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem covering sum-rate maximization, energy efficiency, max-min fairness, and peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR)-constrained objectives. Seventy-eight joint OFDM-RIS optimization works published between 2021 and 2026 are surveyed. No standardized benchmark exists, and cross-paper comparisons remain infeasible. This survey classifies these works into four paradigms: (I) model-based convex relaxation, (II) heuristic and metaheuristic search, (III) deep reinforcement and unsupervised learning, and (IV) emerging methods including foundation models (FM), diffusion-based generative AI, and quantum optimization. A literature synthesis of self-reported benchmarks shows that ML-based methods (Paradigm~III) report 95-99\% of model-based spectral efficiency at 10^2-10^4 x faster per-inference runtime (method-pair dependent; literature values are self-reported and exclude ML pre-training cost). A companion tutorial benchmark at N=16, N=64, and N=128 reveals a critical scaling property: GPU-based neural network inference (DDQN, PPO, graph neural network (GNN), unsupervised DL) is N-invariant, with identical runtime at N=16 and N=128, while iterative solvers (AO+SCA, PSO) scale polynomially. Energy efficiency (P2) and PAPR-constrained (P4) benchmarks are deferred to future work with standardized power models and waveform generators. Six open challenges emerge from the synthesis: the cross-paradigm benchmark deficit, real-world hardware-constrained deployment, joint waveform-RIS optimization for doubly-dispersive channels, multi-objective PAPR trade-offs, LLM safety in live network control, and diminishing returns of standalone heuristics. We specify requirements for a standardized benchmark. This study serves as a roadmap for researchers and practitioners working on joint OFDM-RIS optimization in 6G networks.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of 78 joint OFDM-RIS optimization works (2021-2026) for 6G networks, classifying methods into four paradigms and revealing that ML-based approaches achieve 95-99% of model-based spectral efficiency with dramatically faster runtime.
AI.motivation: Joint OFDM-RIS optimization is critical for 6G but poses MINLP problems across multiple objectives (sum-rate, energy efficiency, fairness, PAPR). Current research lacks standardized benchmarks, making cross-paper comparisons impossible and hindering progress evaluation.
AI.method: Survey of 78 published works from 2021-2026, classified into four paradigms: (I) model-based convex relaxation, (II) heuristic/metaheuristic search, (III) deep reinforcement/unsupervised learning, and (IV) emerging methods (foundation models, diffusion AI, quantum). Includes literature synthesis and companion tutorial benchmark at N=16/64/128.
AI.result: ML-based methods (Paradigm III) achieve 95-99% of model-based spectral efficiency with 10²-10⁴× faster per-inference runtime. Critical scaling property discovered: GPU-based neural network inference is N-invariant, while iterative solvers scale polynomially.
AI.conclusion: Six open challenges identified: cross-paradigm benchmark deficit, real-world hardware deployment, joint waveform-RIS optimization, multi-objective PAPR trade-offs, LLM safety in network control, and diminishing heuristic returns. Paper provides roadmap and standardized benchmark requirements for future joint OFDM-RIS optimization in 6G.

title: Probabilistic Inversion with Flow Matching
summary: We demonstrate the application of Flow Matching, a technique originating from generative Artificial Intelligence, to probabilistic inversion in geophysical settings, such as seismic Full-Waveform inversion. We adapt the well-established mathematical theory of Flow Matching from generative Artificial Intelligence to the context of probabilistic inversion. We evaluate the approach with two case studies: a simple 2D velocity model to illustrate the general features of the method, and the OpenFWI dataset to show its capabilities for probabilistic inversion of more complex seismic velocity models.

AI.tldr: Flow Matching technique from generative AI adapted for probabilistic geophysical inversion, tested on 2D velocity models and OpenFWI seismic data.
AI.motivation: There is a need to apply modern generative AI techniques like Flow Matching to improve probabilistic inversion methods in geophysics, particularly for seismic Full-Waveform inversion which is challenging due to complex velocity models and uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Adapted Flow Matching mathematical theory from generative AI to probabilistic inversion context. Applied the method to two case studies: 1) simple 2D velocity model to illustrate method features, 2) OpenFWI dataset for complex seismic velocity model inversion.
AI.result: Successfully demonstrated application of Flow Matching to probabilistic geophysical inversion. Showed the method's capabilities through both simple 2D examples and more complex seismic velocity models using the OpenFWI dataset.
AI.conclusion: Flow Matching from generative AI can be effectively adapted for probabilistic inversion in geophysics, offering promising capabilities for handling uncertainty in complex seismic velocity model inversion problems.

title: Smart charging of large fleets of Electric Vehicles: Independent Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning approaches
summary: The electrification of transportation through electric vehicles introduces new challenges for power grid management, such as increased peak demand, voltage fluctuations, line overloads, and the integration of variable renewable energy sources. To enable efficient integration of EVs while minimizing costs for users and avoiding network overloads, implicit coordination between EVs is required. This work compares two independent multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches for optimizing such decentralized EV charging: contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient algorithms. Using a realistic simulation environment with autonomous agents making decisions based on local environmental information (including price signals, state-of-charge, and temporal constraints), we evaluate their performance across varying congestion levels, and mixed-strategy configurations with heterogeneous agent groups under dynamic electricity pricing derived from real photovoltaic production data.

AI.tldr: Comparison of two multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches (contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient algorithms) for decentralized EV charging optimization in power grids.
AI.motivation: Electric vehicle integration creates grid management challenges like peak demand spikes, voltage fluctuations, line overloads, and renewable energy integration. Need for implicit EV coordination to minimize user costs while preventing network overloads.
AI.method: Two independent multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches: 1) contextual combinatorial bandits, and 2) policy gradient algorithms. Tested in realistic simulation with autonomous agents using local information (price signals, state-of-charge, temporal constraints) under dynamic electricity pricing from real PV data.
AI.result: Performance evaluation across varying congestion levels and mixed-strategy configurations with heterogeneous agent groups. (Specific results not provided in abstract).
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches can effectively enable decentralized EV charging optimization for power grid management with real-world data integration.

title: Patch-PODiff-ViT: Structured Latent Diffusion with Patchwise POD for Super-Resolution and Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Diffusion models enable probabilistic super-resolution and conditional generation, but pixel-space methods are computationally expensive and learned latent spaces often lack interpretable uncertainty quantification. We introduce Patch-PODiff-ViT, a structured latent diffusion framework in which the latent space is defined by patchwise Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD), a fixed linear orthonormal basis over local patches, rather than learned by a nonlinear autoencoder. This yields low-dimensional, variance-ordered tokens that preserve spatial structure and enable efficient diffusion in a structured low-dimensional latent space with a Vision Transformer. Because the decoder is fixed, linear, and orthonormal, latent coefficient uncertainty can be propagated directly to physical-space predictive variance, enabling analytic propagation of predictive variance through the linear decoder without Monte Carlo estimation in pixel space. Across sea surface temperature, medical imaging, and natural images, the method achieves strong reconstruction with fewer parameters and lower memory, while producing well-calibrated spatial uncertainty that closely matches empirical ensembles.

AI.tldr: Patch-PODiff-ViT: A diffusion framework using fixed patchwise POD basis instead of learned autoencoders for efficient super-resolution with interpretable uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion models for super-resolution are computationally expensive in pixel space, and learned latent spaces lack interpretable uncertainty quantification that can be propagated back to physical space.
AI.method: Uses patchwise Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) - a fixed linear orthonormal basis over local patches - to create a structured latent space. This replaces nonlinear autoencoder learning. Diffusion is performed in this low-dimensional space using a Vision Transformer. The linear decoder enables direct uncertainty propagation.
AI.result: Achieves strong reconstruction with fewer parameters and lower memory across sea surface temperature, medical imaging, and natural images. Produces well-calibrated spatial uncertainty that closely matches empirical ensembles through analytic propagation.
AI.conclusion: Patch-PODiff-ViT provides an efficient alternative to traditional diffusion models by using a fixed POD basis for structured latent representation, enabling both computational efficiency and interpretable uncertainty quantification that can be analytically propagated to physical space.

title: ReGRPO: Reflection-Augmented Policy Optimization for Tool-Using Agents
summary: Tool-augmented vision-language models (VLMs) can solve multimodal, multi-step tasks by calling external tools, yet they remain fragile in practice. Existing works have two common gaps. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is built mostly on successful trajectories and offers little signal for recovery after tool failures, while sparse trajectory-level RL rewards provide limited guidance on which step failed and how to repair it. We introduce ReGRPO (Reflection-augmented Group Relative Policy Optimization), a framework that learns reflection-guided correction in tool-using agents. ReGRPO starts with a structured reflective data engine: we execute near-miss actions to collect grounded failure observations, then build Reflection-of-Thought triplets (ErrorType, Evidence, FixPlan) paired with corrected actions for warm-start SFT. We then optimize reflection tokens and corrective actions jointly within local trajectories using group-relative advantages, and include a reflection-cost term to reduce unnecessary reflection. Experiments on GTA and GAIA show that, under the same backbone and tool suite, ReGRPO consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines and achieves the best results among the compared open-source controllers. Code and RoT data are available at https://github.com/showlab/ReGRPO.

AI.tldr: ReGRPO introduces a framework that enhances tool-augmented vision-language models by incorporating structured reflection mechanisms to improve recovery from tool failures, outperforming existing methods on multimodal tasks.
AI.motivation: Current tool-augmented VLMs are fragile in practice. Existing approaches have two main gaps: 1) Supervised fine-tuning mostly uses successful trajectories and offers little signal for recovery after tool failures, and 2) sparse trajectory-level RL rewards provide limited guidance on identifying specific step failures and how to repair them.
AI.method: ReGRPO (Reflection-augmented Group Relative Policy Optimization) introduces structured reflective data generation: executing near-miss actions to collect grounded failure observations, then building Reflection-of-Thought triplets (ErrorType, Evidence, FixPlan) paired with corrected actions for warm-start SFT. The framework then optimizes reflection tokens and corrective actions jointly using group-relative advantages within local trajectories, and includes a reflection-cost term to reduce unnecessary reflection.
AI.result: Experiments on GTA and GAIA benchmarks show that ReGRPO consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines and achieves the best results among compared open-source controllers, using the same backbone and tool suite.
AI.conclusion: The ReGRPO framework successfully addresses the fragility of tool-augmented VLMs by incorporating structured reflection mechanisms that enable better failure recovery, demonstrating superior performance over existing methods on multimodal, multi-step tasks.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Spacecraft Attitude Control During Atmospheric Re-Entry
summary: Deep reinforcement learning has the potential to solve attitude control problems more adaptively, precisely, and robustly by handling nonlinear dynamics, uncertainties, and failure cases more effectively than traditional attitude control approaches. We explore reinforcement learning (RL) for attitude control in spacecraft re-entry. An industry-standard proportional-integral-derivative controller with gain scheduling serves as a strong baseline for model-free RL and hybrid controllers that combine these two approaches. We formalize the application in the RL framework to apply continuous, off-policy RL. State-of-the-art RL achieves comparable performance to traditional control approaches in this domain. However, its out-of-distribution generalization is not sufficient. Hence, we use dynamics randomization to introduce challenging task variations during training and enforce generalization in a predefined operational envelope. Finally, we assess the best obtained RL-based controllers with application-specific metrics to show superior performance in comparison to traditional controllers in the operational envelope, that is, hybrid controllers are able to track the angle of attack better and are more robust under variations of mass, inertia tensor, and flap actuator bandwidth.

AI.tldr: Deep RL for spacecraft attitude control during re-entry shows superior performance to traditional PID controllers when combined with dynamics randomization for robustness, but struggles with out-of-distribution generalization without specific training techniques.
AI.motivation: Deep RL could solve attitude control problems more adaptively, precisely, and robustly than traditional approaches by better handling nonlinear dynamics, uncertainties, and failure cases in spacecraft re-entry scenarios.
AI.method: Used model-free continuous off-policy RL, compared against PID controllers with gain scheduling as baseline, implemented hybrid controllers combining RL and traditional approaches, and applied dynamics randomization during training to enforce generalization within operational envelopes.
AI.result: State-of-the-art RL achieves comparable performance to traditional control approaches in-domain, but out-of-distribution generalization is insufficient without dynamics randomization. RL-based controllers (especially hybrid ones) show superior performance in tracking angle of attack and are more robust to variations in mass, inertia tensor, and flap actuator bandwidth within the operational envelope.
AI.conclusion: Deep RL, particularly when combined with dynamics randomization and traditional control approaches in hybrid architectures, offers promising advantages for spacecraft attitude control during re-entry, providing better tracking performance and robustness to parameter variations compared to conventional PID controllers.

title: World-Model Collapse as a Phase Transition
summary: Water looks unchanged as it warms, then at a critical point it boils. We ask whether long-horizon language agents show an analogous transition in their implicit world models. In some parameter settings, changing state load by a small amount, or adding a single step of horizon, leaves behavior nearly unchanged; near a critical boundary, the same small change causes a sudden world collapse. We study this effect in a deterministic task family with exact per-step gold state. A large grid search over state cardinality, dependency density, horizon, branching, observation mode, and mutation rate reveals a phase diagram: a solved plateau, a narrow transition band, and a collapse floor. Per-step traces show the mechanism: world-state fidelity fails before action validity, so the agent is not merely choosing a bad action; it is acting from a corrupted world. Stronger models translate the critical boundary but do not remove the qualitative transition. These results make world-model collapse a measurable bottleneck for long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: Language agents exhibit phase transitions in world-model collapse similar to physical systems, with sudden breakdown of internal world models near critical parameter boundaries despite small input changes.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether long-horizon language agents show phase transitions in their implicit world models, analogous to how water suddenly boils at a critical temperature. The authors want to understand the limits and failure modes of agents' internal representations.
AI.method: Researchers studied deterministic task families with exact per-step gold state. They conducted a large grid search over multiple parameters: state cardinality, dependency density, horizon, branching factor, observation mode, and mutation rate. They analyzed per-step traces to understand the mechanism of collapse.
AI.result: The study revealed a clear phase diagram with three regions: solved plateau (where agents work well), narrow transition band, and collapse floor. Analysis showed that world-state fidelity fails before action validity, indicating agents act from corrupted world models rather than just choosing bad actions. Stronger models shift the critical boundary but don't eliminate the qualitative transition.
AI.conclusion: World-model collapse represents a measurable bottleneck for long-horizon agents. The findings suggest that even advanced language models exhibit critical phase transitions in their internal world representations, which could limit their reliability in complex, multi-step tasks.

title: Safe Online Learning via Smooth Safety-Structured Policy Composition
summary: Safe online reinforcement learning requires policies to respect safety constraints while maintaining smooth optimization dynamics. Existing approaches typically rely on either strict safety enforcement via action interventions, which introduce discontinuities in system interaction and learning, or soft safety constraint formulations, which preserve smooth learning but provide limited safety assurance. We propose AutoSafe, a safety-aware policy architecture that integrates structured safety monitoring and intervention directly into the action generation process. This design enables smooth, risk-dependent transitions between performance-driven and safety-preserving behaviors, resulting in continuous online interaction and learning dynamics. Empirical results across a suite of continuous-control benchmarks demonstrate strong safety enforcement without sacrificing learning smoothness. We further validate AutoSafe on a physical cart-pole system, highlighting its practical effectiveness for safe online learning in the real world.

AI.tldr: AutoSafe is a safety-aware policy architecture that enables smooth, continuous online reinforcement learning with strong safety guarantees by integrating structured safety monitoring into action generation.
AI.motivation: Existing safe RL approaches have trade-offs: strict safety enforcement via action interventions causes discontinuities in learning dynamics, while soft safety formulations provide limited safety assurance. There's a need for methods that maintain smooth optimization while ensuring safety.
AI.method: AutoSafe integrates structured safety monitoring and intervention directly into the policy's action generation process. It enables risk-dependent transitions between performance-driven and safety-preserving behaviors, resulting in continuous interaction and learning dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical results across continuous-control benchmarks demonstrate strong safety enforcement without sacrificing learning smoothness. Physical validation on a cart-pole system shows practical effectiveness for safe online learning in real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: AutoSafe successfully addresses the safety-smoothness trade-off in online RL by integrating safety monitoring into the policy architecture, enabling continuous learning with reliable safety guarantees for both simulated and real-world applications.

title: Wisdom Of The (AI) Crowd: Investigating Artificial Swarm Intelligence In Large Language Models
comment: 18 pages, 0 figures, 6 tables, Accepted at ECIS 2026 (European Conference on Information Systems), Track: General Track, Paper No. ECIS2026-1499
summary: Human swarm intelligence demonstrates remarkable collective accuracy but faces scalability constraints in cost, coordination, and time. We investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can approximate swarm intelligence effects through artificial swarms, addressing a critical gap in understanding AI-based aggregation mechanisms. We conducted a controlled experiment with 960 manually executed prompts across three proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5), testing intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation on eight estimation tasks. Results reveal consistent error reduction through intra- and inter-model aggregation, with significant error reductions up to 37 percentage points in MAPE across different aggregation strategies. We observed small to large effect sizes for positive correlations (Spearman's $ρ=0.242-0.568$, all $p<0.001$) between relative confidence interval widths and relative estimation errors, suggesting LLMs possess metacognitive awareness when assessing uncertainty. We discuss implications for research and practice, providing actionable insights for deploying LLM swarms in organizational decision-making.

AI.tldr: LLMs can approximate human swarm intelligence through artificial swarms, achieving significant error reduction in estimation tasks via intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation.
AI.motivation: Human swarm intelligence faces scalability issues in cost, coordination, and time, creating a gap in understanding AI-based aggregation mechanisms. The paper investigates whether LLMs can replicate swarm intelligence effects through artificial swarms.
AI.method: Conducted controlled experiment with 960 manually executed prompts across three proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5). Tested intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation on eight estimation tasks using different aggregation strategies.
AI.result: Consistent error reduction through aggregation with up to 37 percentage point MAPE reduction. Found significant positive correlations (Spearman's ρ=0.242-0.568, all p<0.001) between confidence interval widths and estimation errors, indicating LLM metacognitive awareness in uncertainty assessment.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively approximate swarm intelligence effects through artificial swarms, demonstrating practical utility for organizational decision-making with implications for both research and practice.

title: Expected Gain-based Escalation in Vertical Federated Learning
summary: Collaborative inference can improve predictive performance by integrating complementary information across agents, but applying collaborative fusion to every sample can incur unnecessary communication and computational overhead. This trade-off is particularly relevant in vertical federated learning (VFL), where clients observe different views of the same sample and fusion typically requires transmitting intermediate representations to a server. We study selective escalation in a two-round VFL inference protocol, in which a low-cost first round produces a prediction from client posteriors and a second embedding-fusion round is invoked only when it is expected to improve the final decision. We formulate routing as expected-gain score estimation: a sample is escalated when a predicted improvement in correctness justifies the additional communication. The proposed analytical score combines a calibrated pooled posterior with classwise reliability estimates of the VFL model, both obtained from held-out calibration data, yielding an interpretable router that requires no separately trained routing network. Experiments on multi-view classification benchmarks, including controlled test--time view degradation settings, show that the proposed router improves the communication-accuracy trade-off over confidence-, learned-gain-, and deferral-based baselines.

AI.tldr: A selective escalation method for vertical federated learning that improves communication-accuracy trade-off by routing samples to expensive embedding fusion only when expected to improve predictions.
AI.motivation: Collaborative inference in VFL improves performance but requires transmitting intermediate representations, which incurs unnecessary communication and computational overhead for all samples. There's a need for selective routing that only uses expensive fusion when it's beneficial.
AI.method: Two-round VFL inference protocol: 1) low-cost round produces predictions from client posteriors, 2) embedding-fusion round invoked only when expected to improve final decision. Routing based on expected-gain score estimation using calibrated pooled posterior and classwise reliability estimates from held-out calibration data, without requiring separate routing network.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-view classification benchmarks show proposed router improves communication-accuracy trade-off over confidence-based, learned-gain-based, and deferral-based baselines, including in test-time view degradation settings.
AI.conclusion: The analytical score-based router provides interpretable selective escalation that balances communication costs with accuracy improvements in VFL, using only calibration data rather than separate training.

title: Xiaomi-GUI-0 Technical Report
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) agents build on vision-language models to complete user tasks end-to-end in real applications through interface actions such as tapping, swiping, text entry, and navigation. However, existing GUI agents are trained and evaluated largely on offline trajectories, simulated environments, and standardized benchmarks. These differ substantially from real applications in interface layout, interaction logic, and abnormal-state distribution, and cannot faithfully characterize execution stability in real-world use, where account states, permission dialogs, payment authentication, and risk control continually reshape the state distribution and open a persistent gap between benchmark scores and real usability. To close this gap, we propose Xiaomi-GUI-0, a native multimodal GUI agent for real mobile environments, trained and evaluated within a real-device closed loop. At its core is a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure, where physical devices are the primary execution environment and sandboxes provide auxiliary support, so that data collection, training, rollout, and evaluation share an execution distribution close to real deployment. We construct multi-source training data spanning high-frequency head tasks, high-generalization data for long-tail intents, and capability-enhancement data for reflection and memory, and introduce an error-driven data flywheel that turns failure trajectories into corrected actions, reflective explanations, and recovery demonstrations. The model is trained through a progressive three-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning, step-level reinforcement learning, and agentic reinforcement learning. Evaluated on public benchmarks and our in-house RealMobile, Xiaomi-GUI-0 achieves 72.0% success on RealMobile and 78.9% on AndroidWorld, while substantially improving execution stability and abnormal-state recognition in real-world tasks.

AI.tldr: Xiaomi-GUI-0 is a native multimodal GUI agent for real mobile environments that closes the gap between benchmark performance and real-world usability through a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure and error-driven data flywheel.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agents are trained and evaluated on offline trajectories, simulated environments, and standardized benchmarks, which differ substantially from real applications in interface layout, interaction logic, and abnormal-state distribution. This creates a persistent gap between benchmark scores and real usability due to real-world factors like account states, permission dialogs, payment authentication, and risk control.
AI.method:
1. Real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure where physical devices are the primary execution environment with sandbox auxiliary support
2. Multi-source training data covering high-frequency head tasks, high-generalization data for long-tail intents, and capability-enhancement data for reflection and memory
3. Error-driven data flywheel that turns failure trajectories into corrected actions, reflective explanations, and recovery demonstrations
4. Three-stage progressive training pipeline: supervised fine-tuning → step-level reinforcement learning → agentic reinforcement learning
AI.result: The model achieves 72.0% success on the in-house RealMobile benchmark and 78.9% on AndroidWorld public benchmark, while substantially improving execution stability and abnormal-state recognition in real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: Xiaomi-GUI-0 demonstrates that training and evaluating within a real-device closed loop with an error-driven data flywheel can effectively bridge the gap between benchmark performance and real-world usability for GUI agents, enabling more stable execution and better handling of abnormal states in real mobile applications.

title: Dualformer: Efficient Feature Extractor for Complex-valued Blind Communication Signal Analysis
summary: Designing effective feature extractors is critical for blind signal analysis tasks such as automatic modulation recognition (AMR), signal scheme recognition (SSR), and \color{black} signal structure parsing (SSP). In this work, we propose dual-channel neural network (DualNN) that efficiently exploits complex-valued signals through parameter sharing across IQ channels. Unlike traditional real-valued or complex-valued models, DualNN is a groundbreaking framework which shares the network parameters for processing the real and imaginary parts of the complex-valued signals, and is theoretically shown to reduce generalization error while preserving expressive capacity. Specifically, we propose a novel Transformer-based architecture to implement DualNN, called Dualformer. The Dualformer segments input signals into patch-level tokens and captures multi-granularity features, enabling robust performance across diverse signal analysis tasks. Furthermore, we conduct extensive experiments comparing Dualformer with three Transformer-based baselines and four conventional DL-based approaches. Results demonstrate consistent performance improvements on AMR, SSR, and SSP tasks. Besides, the modular design of DualNN allows it to generalize well to blind signal processing tasks such as blind source separation and low-SNR spectrum sensing. This work paves the way for a broader application of DualNN architectures in unsupervised and weakly supervised complex-valued signal analysis scenarios.

AI.tldr: DualNN is a novel neural network framework that shares parameters across IQ channels to efficiently process complex-valued signals, implemented via Dualformer architecture, showing superior performance in blind signal analysis tasks like modulation recognition.
AI.motivation: Traditional models don't efficiently exploit complex-valued signals. There's a need for more effective feature extractors for blind signal analysis tasks including automatic modulation recognition, signal scheme recognition, and signal structure parsing.
AI.method: Proposes DualNN framework that shares network parameters for processing real and imaginary parts of complex-valued signals. Implements this using Dualformer - a Transformer-based architecture that segments signals into patch-level tokens and captures multi-granularity features.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show consistent performance improvements on AMR, SSR, and SSP tasks compared to three Transformer-based baselines and four conventional DL approaches. DualNN also generalizes well to blind source separation and low-SNR spectrum sensing.
AI.conclusion: DualNN is a groundbreaking framework that reduces generalization error while preserving expressive capacity for complex-valued signal analysis, paving the way for broader applications in unsupervised and weakly supervised scenarios.

title: Learning to Select, Not Relearn: Hard-Routed Mixtures of Reasoning LoRAs
summary: Composing independently trained LoRA adapters into a single large language model is useful for multi-domain adaptation, especially when the original training data cannot be shared. A common approach is to use MoE-style routing over LoRA experts, but for frozen pretrained adapters, soft weighted combinations can change the unit-scale additive update under which each LoRA module was originally trained. We propose \textbf{Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA}, a two-stage framework for composing frozen reasoning LoRA experts through unit-scale hard selection. First, domain-specific LoRA adapters are trained independently using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback to obtain reasoning experts. Then, all experts are frozen, reasoning traces are distilled from them, and only a lightweight shared router together with a small attention LoRA is trained for integration. The router selects exactly one expert per token using hard top-1 routing, while a straight-through estimator enables gradient-based training. Experiments across five benchmarks, multiple model scales, and additional model families show that Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA preserves expert behavior while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters than soft-routing mixture baselines. Our analysis further shows that normalized soft mixtures often concentrate most routing mass on a single expert, suggesting that hard unit-scale routing provides a simple and efficient abstraction for frozen LoRA expert composition.

AI.tldr: Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA enables efficient composition of frozen LoRA reasoning experts through hard unit-scale routing instead of soft weighted combinations, preserving expert behavior with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: When composing independently trained LoRA adapters for multi-domain adaptation, soft weighted combinations can change the unit-scale additive update under which each LoRA module was originally trained, potentially degrading performance. There's a need for a method that preserves expert behavior while being parameter-efficient.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: 1) Train domain-specific LoRA adapters independently using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback to obtain reasoning experts, 2) Freeze all experts, distill reasoning traces, and train only a lightweight shared router with a small attention LoRA. The router uses hard top-1 routing (selecting exactly one expert per token) with straight-through estimator for gradient-based training.
AI.result: Experiments across five benchmarks, multiple model scales, and additional model families show that Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA preserves expert behavior while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters than soft-routing mixture baselines. Analysis reveals that normalized soft mixtures often concentrate most routing mass on a single expert anyway.
AI.conclusion: Hard unit-scale routing provides a simple and efficient abstraction for frozen LoRA expert composition, maintaining the original training conditions of individual LoRA modules while being parameter-efficient compared to soft-routing alternatives.

title: Calibrating the Evaluator: Does Probability Calibration Mitigate Preference Coupling in LLM Agent Feedback Loops?
summary: When large language model (LLM) agents adapt their behavior through evaluator feedback, systematic evaluator biases propagate into the agent's learned strategy distribution - a phenomenon termed evaluator preference coupling. Prior work has documented this coupling and established a diagnostic framework (EPC) to measure it, but has not investigated whether calibration techniques can mitigate the effect. We present the first study of evaluator calibration as mitigation: applying probability calibration to the evaluator's pairwise judgments to reduce spurious preference propagation. In a controlled within-subjects experiment (N=5) comparing standard binary TTRL (win/loss) with confidence-calibrated TTRL (probability-weighted updates) using DeepSeek-V4-Pro as executor and GLM5.2 as evaluator, we find that calibration reduces the coupling coefficient gamma by 20-49% and Jensen-Shannon divergence by 45-67%. A symmetric-LR control confirms the effect is not due to reduced update asymmetry. We release the calibrated TTRL protocol and recommend it as a lightweight mitigation for LLM-as-judge deployment pipelines.

AI.tldr: Calibration techniques applied to LLM evaluators reduce systematic bias propagation into agent behavior by 20-67%, offering lightweight mitigation for LLM-as-judge pipelines.
AI.motivation: Large language model agents adapting through evaluator feedback inherit systematic biases from evaluators, a phenomenon called "evaluator preference coupling." While prior work documented this issue, no studies investigated whether calibration techniques could mitigate it.
AI.method: Used probability calibration on evaluator's pairwise judgments to reduce spurious preference propagation. Conducted controlled within-subjects experiment (N=5) comparing standard binary TTRL (win/loss) with confidence-calibrated TTRL (probability-weighted updates), using DeepSeek-V4-Pro as executor and GLM5.2 as evaluator. Included symmetric-LR control to verify effects weren't due to reduced update asymmetry.
AI.result: Calibration reduces the coupling coefficient gamma by 20-49% and Jensen-Shannon divergence by 45-67%. The symmetric-LR control confirmed effects weren't due to reduced update asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: Evaluator calibration effectively mitigates evaluator preference coupling in LLM agents. The calibrated TTRL protocol is recommended as a lightweight mitigation for LLM-as-judge deployment pipelines, and the authors release the protocol for community use.

title: BP-TTA: Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation in Dynamic Scenarios
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) enables models trained on a source domain to adapt online to unlabeled test data under distribution shifts. While recent TTA methods have moved beyond static settings and begun to consider continual domain shifts, they primarily address distribution drift and fail to account for class imbalance in dynamic scenarios. In real-world test-time streams, class imbalance and continual domain shifts often occur at the same time and interact with each other. In this paper, we propose a novel Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation (BP-TTA) method, which combines batch-balanced sampling with prototype-guided adaptation to handle the class imbalance and continual domain shift problems. BP-TTA constructs balanced adaptation batches by integrating current samples with high-confidence historical instances, effectively mitigating bias toward dominant classes and stabilizing online updates. Meanwhile, BP-TTA maintains evolving class prototypes during inference and leverages prototype similarity as a constraint for model adaptation, thereby improving the reliability of pseudo-labels and enhancing the stability of online updates under persistent domain shifts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BP-TTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art TTA methods in dynamic test-time streaming settings.

AI.tldr: BP-TTA is a novel test-time adaptation method that addresses both class imbalance and continual domain shifts simultaneously using balanced batch sampling and prototype-guided adaptation.
AI.motivation: Current TTA methods only address distribution drift and fail to handle class imbalance in dynamic scenarios. In real-world applications, class imbalance and continual domain shifts often co-occur and interact, creating a more complex challenge that existing methods don't address.
AI.method: The proposed BP-TTA method combines two key approaches: 1) Balanced batch sampling that integrates current test samples with high-confidence historical instances to mitigate bias toward dominant classes, and 2) Prototype-guided adaptation that maintains evolving class prototypes during inference and uses prototype similarity as a constraint to improve pseudo-label reliability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that BP-TTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art TTA methods in dynamic test-time streaming settings, demonstrating superior performance in handling the joint challenge of class imbalance and continual domain shifts.
AI.conclusion: BP-TTA effectively addresses the simultaneous occurrence of class imbalance and continual domain shifts in test-time adaptation by combining balanced sampling with prototype guidance, achieving more stable and reliable online adaptation in dynamic real-world scenarios.

title: Resolving superposition in AI for interpretability and cross-modal alignment in patient-neuronal images
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures (plus 14 in appendix), 1 table, NeurIPS 2026 preprint
summary: Artificial intelligence is transforming our capability to solve biological challenges. In dimensionality bottleneck regimes exacerbated by high-dimensional biological data, Neural networks force distinct concepts into the lower dimensions known as superposition. Although this superposition is widely known to hinder interpretability, its impact on corrupting the geometry of latent spaces remains critically overlooked. Here, we utilized sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on over 100,000 multiplexed images of patient-derived Parkinson's disease and healthy neurons to resolve superposition. This approach bypasses the mathematical non-uniqueness of feature attribution by shifting to interpretable latent representation analysis. We theoretically and empirically demonstrate that superposition contaminates representational metric spaces, and thereby SAEs successfully recover geometric fidelity. By treating these geometrically purified representations as single-cell state vectors, we adapted single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data analysis methodologies directly to the image domain. Finally, we introduce GW-map, utilizing Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport to align these image representations with authentic scRNA-seq data \emph{de novo}. This coupling reconstructs hierarchical neuronal pathology pathways such as Calcium-AIS scaffold, without reference spatial transcriptomics, establishing a scalable foundation for spatial biology. Code is available at https://github.com/jijihihi/Bio_superposition

AI.tldr: The paper addresses how neural network superposition corrupts biological latent space geometry and introduces a method using sparse autoencoders to recover geometric fidelity, enabling image-to-transcriptomics alignment without reference spatial data.
AI.motivation: Superposition in neural networks (where distinct concepts are forced into lower dimensions) not only hinders interpretability but also corrupts the geometry of latent spaces in biological data analysis, which has been critically overlooked despite its importance for understanding biological representations.
AI.method: Used sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on over 100,000 multiplexed images of Parkinson's disease and healthy neurons to resolve superposition. Shifted from feature attribution to interpretable latent representation analysis. Adapted single-cell RNA sequencing methodologies to the image domain and introduced GW-map using Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport to align image representations with scRNA-seq data de novo.
AI.result: SAEs successfully recovered geometric fidelity in representational metric spaces. GW-map enabled reconstruction of hierarchical neuronal pathology pathways (e.g., Calcium-AIS scaffold) without reference spatial transcriptomics, establishing a scalable foundation for spatial biology.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that addressing superposition through SAEs and geometric purification enables direct application of single-cell analysis methods to imaging data, bridging imaging and transcriptomics domains without requiring spatial reference data, thereby establishing new scalable approaches for spatial biology.

title: Ask the World Before Acting: Budgeted Environment Probing for World-Model Calibration
summary: Long-horizon language agents do not only choose actions; they carry a private model of the world from one decision to the next. When that model drifts, a later failure can be decided before the failing action is ever taken. We study a direct repair mechanism: before committing to the next task action, an agent may ask the environment about one belief field and write the answer back into its world model. This makes environment interaction a scarce calibration resource, not merely a way to advance the task. We introduce \method, a budgeted probing operator for structured belief tables. The useful probes are not the same everywhere. Procedural beliefs, such as tool dependencies, can often be repaired by targeted checks, but those checks spend steps that the task may need. Spatial beliefs, such as object locations and graph edges, rely more on structural cues; the agent's own confidence can be a poor guide when the world changes off-screen. A type-stratified analysis formalizes this probe-action frontier, and controlled experiments show that mid-planning environment evidence reduces terminal world-model error when the probe policy follows the structure of the task.

AI.tldr: Long-horizon language agents can use budgeted environment probing to repair drifting world models during task execution, with structured probing policies outperforming confidence-based approaches when world changes occur off-screen.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon language agents maintain private world models that can drift over time, leading to failures before the failing action is even taken. Environment interaction is typically used only for task advancement, not for model calibration, creating a need for systematic model repair mechanisms during execution.
AI.method: Introduces \method, a budgeted probing operator for structured belief tables that allows agents to ask the environment about one belief field before committing to the next task action, then write the answer back into the world model. The approach involves type-stratified analysis distinguishing procedural beliefs (tool dependencies) from spatial beliefs (object locations, graph edges) to determine effective probing policies.
AI.result: Controlled experiments show that mid-planning environment evidence reduces terminal world-model error when the probe policy follows the structure of the task. Procedural beliefs can often be repaired by targeted checks, while spatial beliefs rely more on structural cues and the agent's own confidence can be poor guidance when the world changes off-screen.
AI.conclusion: Environment interaction should be treated as a scarce calibration resource, not just a way to advance tasks. Structured probing during planning is an effective mechanism for repairing drifting world models in long-horizon language agents, with probe effectiveness depending on belief type and task structure.

title: Mixture-of-Control: State-Aware Fine-Tuning for Transformer-based Models
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Connecting Low-rank Representations in AI, CoLoRAI, 26 pages, 12 figures, 5 tables
summary: State-based fine-tuning has emerged as a compelling alternative to weight-based adaptation for transformers, updating lightweight controls into states rather than model weights, offering substantial memory savings while retaining parameter efficiency. However, most existing state-based methods typically apply only per-block control updates, which limits inter-block information exchange and restricts representational adaptation. Meanwhile, prior mechanisms that enable cross-block communication often introduce considerable computational overhead, reducing their practicality for efficient fine-tuning. We introduce Mixture-of-Control (MoC), a lightweight fine-tuning framework that adaptively integrates local and global control signals to enhance representation learning. MoC treats block-wise control states as experts in a sparse mixture-of-experts process, enabling efficient communication across transformer blocks. Empirical results across diverse transformer-based benchmarks demonstrate that MoC outperforms state-based methods while maintaining a comparable memory and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Mixture-of-Control (MoC) introduces a lightweight fine-tuning framework that combines local and global control signals via sparse mixture-of-experts to enable efficient cross-block communication in transformers, outperforming state-based methods while maintaining efficiency.
AI.motivation: State-based fine-tuning offers memory savings over weight-based adaptation but existing methods use only per-block control updates, limiting inter-block information exchange. Cross-block communication mechanisms in prior work introduce excessive computational overhead, reducing practical efficiency.
AI.method: MoC treats block-wise control states as experts in a sparse mixture-of-experts process, adaptively integrating local and global control signals. This enables efficient communication across transformer blocks while maintaining lightweight characteristics.
AI.result: Empirical results across diverse transformer-based benchmarks show MoC outperforms existing state-based methods while maintaining comparable memory and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MoC provides an effective balance between representation adaptation capability and practical efficiency, addressing limitations of previous state-based fine-tuning approaches through adaptive integration of local and global control signals.

title: CDR-Bench: Evaluating Faithful Execution of Compositional, Order-Sensitive Data Refinement Recipes
comment: 29 pages, 20 figures. Corresponding authors: Daoyuan Chen and Yi R. Fung
summary: Data refinement involves executing multi-step recipes over evolving text states, where both composition and execution order of processing operators determine the outcome. While existing benchmarks either isolate text editing or entangle it with code and tool execution, it remains unclear whether LLMs can directly and faithfully execute these compositional, order-sensitive data refinement recipes. To fill this gap, we introduce CDR-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring 3,462 high-quality tasks spanning four real-world data refinement domains and 29 distinct operators. Our benchmark evaluates models across atomic, order-agnostic, and order-sensitive settings, leveraging deterministic reference outputs to enable exact evaluation. Experiments on 10+ state-of-the-art LLMs reveal consistent failure patterns: performance degrades sharply in compositional settings, and order-sensitive recipe success collapses. These findings underline that current LLMs lack the procedural faithfulness required for reliable compositional data refinement.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with compositional data refinement tasks that require procedural faithfulness, as shown by CDR-Bench, a new benchmark that reveals consistent failure patterns in order-sensitive recipe execution.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks either isolate text editing or entangle it with code/tool execution, leaving it unclear whether LLMs can faithfully execute compositional, order-sensitive data refinement recipes that are crucial for real-world data processing tasks.
AI.method: Introduced CDR-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark with 3,462 high-quality tasks spanning four real-world data refinement domains and 29 distinct operators. Evaluates models across atomic, order-agnostic, and order-sensitive settings using deterministic reference outputs for exact evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments on 10+ state-of-the-art LLMs show consistent failure patterns: performance degrades sharply in compositional settings, and order-sensitive recipe success collapses. Models can't reliably handle multi-step recipes where execution order matters.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs lack the procedural faithfulness required for reliable compositional data refinement. The benchmark reveals fundamental limitations in LLMs' ability to execute multi-step, order-sensitive recipes, highlighting an important gap in current capabilities.

title: Contextual Slate GLM Bandits with Limited Adaptivity
summary: We investigate the contextual slate bandit problem with generalized linear rewards under limited adaptivity. At each round, the learner is presented with $N$ sets of items, where each item is represented by a $d$-dimensional feature vector. The learner then constructs a slate by selecting one item per set; the resulting slate yields a scalar reward sampled from a Generalized Linear Model (GLM). We propose algorithms under two limited-adaptivity settings: (a) Batched and (b) Rarely-Switching. For the batched setting, we introduce B-SlateGLinCB, which partitions the time horizon into $\mathcal{O}(\log\log T)$ batches such that each batch's policy relies only on data from previous batches. For the rarely-switching setting, we propose RS-SlateGLinCB, which adaptively performs only $\mathcal{O}(Nd\log T)$ parameter updates. Under a diversity assumption on the item sequences, we prove that B-SlateGLinCB and RS-SlateGLinCB achieve regret bounds of $\mathcal{O}(Nd^{3/2}\sqrt{T})$ and $\mathcal{O}(Nd\sqrt{T})$, respectively. Notably, both bounds are independent of the non-linearity parameter $κ$ that is typically found to scale the regret of GLM bandit algorithms. Our algorithms are computationally efficient, requiring only $\text{poly}(N)$ time per round despite $2^{Ω(N)}$ possible slates. Simulations show our algorithms outperform existing baselines with limited adaptivity and remain competitive with Slate-GLM-OFU, a fully adaptive state-of-the-art algorithm. Notably, a slightly modified B-SlateGLinCB empirically matches this baseline. Finally, we demonstrate strong performance in a practical in-context example selection task for language models.

AI.tldr: Batched and rarely-switching algorithms for contextual slate bandits with generalized linear rewards achieve κ-independent regret bounds while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the slate bandit problem under limited adaptivity constraints, where fully adaptive algorithms may be impractical due to computational or implementation constraints in real-world systems.
AI.method: Two algorithms: B-SlateGLinCB for batched setting with O(log log T) batches, and RS-SlateGLinCB for rarely-switching setting with O(Nd log T) parameter updates. Both use confidence bound approaches with computational efficiency despite exponential slate space.
AI.result: Regret bounds of O(Nd^{3/2}√T) for batched and O(Nd√T) for rarely-switching, both independent of the non-linearity parameter κ that typically scales GLM bandit regret. Algorithms run in poly(N) time per round.
AI.conclusion: The proposed limited-adaptivity algorithms achieve κ-independent regret, maintain computational efficiency, outperform existing limited-adaptivity baselines, and show practical utility in tasks like in-context example selection for language models.

title: Who Determines the Meaning of an Emotion? Affective Sovereignty as an Epistemic Consequence of Measurement Limits
summary: Emotion-sensing AI is rapidly becoming embedded in vehicles, home appliances, dialogue agents, and social infrastructure, giving rise to a sphere in which emotion is no longer confined to individual experience but is instead observed and computed at a societal scale, a domain we term the Affectosphere. Yet a central normative question in this domain has remained underexplored: who has the final authority to determine the meaning of one's own emotion? This study addresses the question from the epistemological side of measurement's structural limits. We define a meaning distribution as the distribution of labels assigned by annotators drawn from a population under a fixed annotation protocol, and decompose its uncertainty into reducible and irreducible components. We then demonstrate that, while emotion AI can assign high-confidence point labels and discriminate real differences at an aggregate level, the irreducible component of the meaning distribution for individual instances cannot be estimated with adequate coverage under realistic annotator counts, a systematic divergence we term the epistemic gap. The key finding is that high device confidence does not constitute evidence that irrecoverable meaning has been recovered. From this epistemic gap, together with an explicitly stated normative premise, namely that the output of a system which cannot recover a quantity in principle must not be treated as its authoritative determination, we derive the norm that the final interpretive authority over the meaning of one's emotion is procedurally reserved for the experiencing subject, the norm of affective sovereignty. These results suggest that the design, evaluation, and regulation of emotion AI should place explicit allocation of interpretive authority, rather than accuracy maximisation, at their core.

AI.tldr: Emotion AI creates an "Affectosphere" where emotions are measured societally, but a fundamental "epistemic gap" prevents AI from authoritatively determining emotional meaning, establishing "affective sovereignty" - the experiencing subject's procedural authority over their own emotions.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the critical normative question of who has final authority to determine the meaning of emotions in the context of emotion-sensing AI that is becoming embedded throughout society (the "Affectosphere"). The central problem is that while emotion AI can make confident measurements, the fundamental question of interpretive authority over emotions has been underexplored.
AI.method: The study approaches the question epistemologically by examining measurement's structural limits. It defines a "meaning distribution" as the distribution of labels assigned by annotators from a population under fixed protocols, then decomposes its uncertainty into reducible and irreducible components. The authors analyze the irreducible component's estimability under realistic annotator counts.
AI.result: The key finding is an "epistemic gap": while emotion AI can assign high-confidence point labels and discriminate real differences at aggregate levels, the irreducible component of meaning distributions for individual instances cannot be adequately estimated under realistic annotator counts. High device confidence does not constitute evidence that irrecoverable meaning has been recovered.
AI.conclusion: From the epistemic gap and the normative premise that systems unable to recover a quantity in principle must not be treated as authoritative, the paper derives the "norm of affective sovereignty" - final interpretive authority over emotional meaning is procedurally reserved for the experiencing subject. Emotion AI design, evaluation, and regulation should focus on explicit allocation of interpretive authority rather than accuracy maximization.

title: Zero-Shot Quantization for Object Detectors using Off-the-Shelf Generative Models
summary: With an increasing number of Object Detection (OD) models being deployed on edge devices, Zero-Shot Quantization for OD (ZSQ-OD) aims to quantize these models when access to the original training data is prohibited. Existing research on Zero-Shot Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) for OD synthesizes training sets through noise optimization. However, this approach struggles to maintain performance in low-bit regions. In this paper, we introduce GoodQ (Generative off-the-shelf models for object detector Quantization), a QAT pipeline that utilizes off-the-shelf generative models to construct a training set. We first identify three challenges that arise when introducing a generative model to the ZSQ-OD task: 1) each image contains dense information with multiple instances, 2) the class-wise distribution in the original dataset is imbalanced, and 3) the pseudo-labels assigned to the generated images can potentially act as noisy signals during QAT. GoodQ addresses these challenges by 1) introducing an Information-Dense Prompting strategy to generate multi-instance images, 2) applying Intrinsic Distribution-Aware Selection to match the pretrained class distribution, and 3) employing Teacher-guided Adaptive Noise Reduction to mitigate noise arising from the QAT process. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in low-bit ZSQ (W4A4) and extends quantization to extreme bit-widths (W3A3). Furthermore, we conduct an extensive analysis to uncover the underlying factors contributing to the efficacy of GoodQ.
