AI.tldr: GoodQ is a novel Zero-Shot Quantization-Aware Training pipeline for object detection that uses off-the-shelf generative models to create synthetic training data, addressing key challenges in generating multi-instance images, handling class imbalance, and reducing noisy pseudo-labels to achieve state-of-the-art performance in low-bit quantization.
AI.motivation: Object Detection models are increasingly deployed on edge devices, requiring quantization to reduce model size and computational cost. However, traditional quantization methods require access to original training data, which may not be available due to privacy/security concerns. Existing zero-shot quantization methods struggle with maintaining performance in low-bit regimes (W4A4 and below), particularly for object detection tasks.
AI.method: GoodQ introduces a three-component framework: 1) Information-Dense Prompting strategy to generate multi-instance images using off-the-shelf generative models, 2) Intrinsic Distribution-Aware Selection to match the original dataset's imbalanced class distribution, and 3) Teacher-guided Adaptive Noise Reduction to minimize noise from pseudo-labels during quantization-aware training. The approach leverages generative models to create synthetic training data when original data is unavailable.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance in low-bit zero-shot quantization (W4A4) and successfully extends quantization to extreme bit-widths (W3A3). Extensive analysis is conducted to understand the underlying factors contributing to GoodQ's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: GoodQ demonstrates that leveraging off-the-shelf generative models for creating synthetic training data effectively addresses key challenges in zero-shot quantization for object detection, enabling high-performance quantization even at extremely low bit-widths without access to original training data.

title: CSTrader: A Testbed for Language-Grounded Trading in a Community-Driven Virtual Asset Market
summary: Niche asset markets, such as Counter-Strike 2 (CS2) weapon skins, are small, volatile, and heavily driven by community discussions and platform rules. These properties make them hard for traditional quantitative models, but provide an ideal testbed for studying how large language models (LLMs) turn unstructured text into trading actions. We present CSTrader, a multi-agent framework for language-grounded trading in the CS2 skin market. The system first integrates heterogeneous signals from various sources, then uses specialized agents for technical analysis, liquidity, events, and (reversed) sentiment, and finally applies risk control, transaction friction, and portfolio management agents to produce buy, sell, or hold decisions under realistic trading frictions. We build a live-like evaluation environment with real CS2 data from a highly volatile period and evaluate several recent LLM backbones. Across models, CSTrader consistently outperforms both a falling market index (-15.62%) and simple single-prompt LLM baselines, achieving up to a 7.58% cumulative return with controlled risk. Ablation studies show that liquidity, reversed sentiment, and transaction friction agents are crucial for turning noisy language signals into stable profits, suggesting that niche, language-driven markets are a useful benchmark for future language-to-action research. Code is available at: https://github.com/IatomicreactorI/CSGOTrading?tab=readme-ov-file#quick-start

AI.tldr: CSTrader is a multi-agent LLM framework that analyzes CS2 weapon skin market signals from text sources to make trading decisions, outperforming market indices and simpler LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Niche asset markets like CS2 weapon skins are volatile and driven by community discussions, making them hard for traditional models but ideal for testing how LLMs can translate unstructured text into trading actions.
AI.method: Multi-agent framework that integrates signals from various sources, uses specialized agents for technical analysis, liquidity, events, and reversed sentiment, and applies risk control, transaction friction, and portfolio management agents to make buy/sell/hold decisions under realistic trading frictions.
AI.result: CSTrader consistently outperforms both the falling market index (-15.62%) and simple single-prompt LLM baselines, achieving up to 7.58% cumulative return with controlled risk. Ablation studies show liquidity, reversed sentiment, and transaction friction agents are crucial for turning noisy language signals into stable profits.
AI.conclusion: Niche, language-driven markets like CS2 weapon skins are a useful benchmark for future language-to-action research, demonstrating that properly structured multi-agent LLM frameworks can effectively translate textual signals into profitable trading decisions despite market volatility.

title: TabPATE: Differentially Private Tabular In-Context Learning Without Public Data
comment: Presented at the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data (2026)
summary: Tabular foundation models enable accurate in-context learning (ICL) from small labeled datasets, but the private records placed in context can leak through model predictions. We first show that even basic membership inference attacks succeed against tabular ICL, motivating formal privacy protection. We then introduce TabPATE, a differentially private PATE-style defense for tabular ICL that does not require public in-distribution data. TabPATE partitions the private context across teacher models, privately aggregates their labels on synthetic tabular queries, and releases the resulting labeled queries as a student context. Because tabular features are bounded and relatively low-dimensional, useful queries can be generated from feature ranges alone or from lightly privatized marginals. Across tabular benchmarks, TabPATE preserves competitive utility while reducing membership inference to near-random success, providing a practical path to private tabular ICL without public data.

AI.tldr: TabPATE: A differentially private PATE-style defense for tabular in-context learning that protects private records in context by partitioning data across teachers, privately aggregating labels on synthetic queries, and releasing safe labeled queries without needing public in-distribution data.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models enable accurate in-context learning from small labeled datasets, but the private records placed in context can leak through model predictions. Basic membership inference attacks succeed against tabular ICL, creating privacy risks that motivate formal protection mechanisms.
AI.method: TabPATE uses a PATE-style approach: 1) Partition private context across teacher models, 2) Generate synthetic tabular queries (using feature ranges or lightly privatized marginals since tabular features are bounded and low-dimensional), 3) Privately aggregate teacher labels on these queries, 4) Release the resulting labeled queries as a student context for safe ICL.
AI.result: Across tabular benchmarks, TabPATE preserves competitive utility while reducing membership inference attack success to near-random levels, demonstrating effective privacy protection without requiring public in-distribution data.
AI.conclusion: TabPATE provides a practical path to private tabular in-context learning without needing public data, offering formal privacy guarantees through differential privacy while maintaining useful model performance on tabular tasks.

title: CLOUDADV: Decision-Aligned Instance Sizing with Zero-Shot Foundation Models under Drift
summary: Cloud virtual machines are often overprovisioned, creating avoidable cost and operational inefficiency. We present CLOUDADV, an interactive engineer-facing advisory system for cloud instance sizing under workload drift. The system combines zero-shot time-series forecasting with bounded recommendation generation across day-, week-, and month-scale planning horizons. For each query, CLOUDADV constructs a structured decision context from historical utilization, forecast summaries, current VM metadata, candidate instance options, pricing, and explicit sizing heuristics. A higher-capacity LLM is used offline to generate reference recommendations, while a smaller production model is evaluated on the same prompts to assess deployment-time alignment under latency and cost constraints. Evaluation prioritizes downstream recommendation quality using simulated Azure cost savings and ex-post exceedance, with rolling-origin forecast accuracy reported as a secondary diagnostic against classical and supervised baselines. In a case study of seven production VMs, the reference recommendations reduce simulated monthly cost from about \$1,503 to \$708, yielding \$795/month in savings (52.9%) under conservative heuristic constraints, while the highest observed exceedance rate among downgraded cases is 1.5%. Although Chronos-2 does not minimize every forecasting metric, it often induces recommendation patterns similar to those of a supervised per-VM baseline. These results suggest that zero-shot foundation models can support decision-aligned provisioning in non-stationary cloud environments while reducing the operational burden of repeated per-tenant retraining, revalidation, and redeployment.

AI.tldr: CLOUDADV is an interactive advisory system that uses zero-shot time-series forecasting and bounded recommendation generation to optimize cloud instance sizing, reducing costs by 52.9% while maintaining low exceedance rates.
AI.motivation: Cloud virtual machines are often overprovisioned, leading to unnecessary costs and operational inefficiencies. There's a need for automated sizing recommendations that can handle workload drift without requiring repeated per-tenant model retraining and redeployment.
AI.method: The system combines zero-shot time-series forecasting (using Chronos-2) with bounded recommendation generation across multiple planning horizons (day, week, month). It constructs structured decision contexts from historical utilization, forecasts, VM metadata, instance options, pricing, and sizing heuristics. Uses offline higher-capacity LLM for reference recommendations and smaller production models for deployment-time alignment under latency/cost constraints.
AI.result: In a case study of 7 production VMs, CLOUDADV reduced simulated monthly costs from $1,503 to $708 (52.9% savings) under conservative heuristic constraints. The highest observed exceedance rate among downgraded cases was only 1.5%. Chronos-2 forecasting often induced recommendation patterns similar to supervised per-VM baselines.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot foundation models can effectively support decision-aligned provisioning in non-stationary cloud environments while reducing the operational burden of repeated per-tenant retraining, revalidation, and redeployment compared to supervised approaches.

title: Constrained Online Convex Optimization without Slater's Condition
summary: We study constrained online convex optimization with adversarial losses and stochastic or adversarial constraints. For stochastic constraints, existing algorithms that achieve nearly optimal regret and constraint violation bounds typically rely on regularity assumptions such as Slater's condition, while adversarial-constraint algorithms avoid these assumptions by using a rather restrictive round-wise feasible comparator. We bridge this gap with an anytime primal-dual framework that incorporates an adaptive regularizer into the dual update. The regularizer stabilizes the dual process without relying on the negative drift induced by Slater's condition. For stochastic constraints and convex losses, our algorithm achieves $O(\sqrt{T})$ expected regret and $O(\sqrt{T}\log T)$ expected cumulative constraint violation. Furthermore, we show that our algorithm also admits high-probability bounds of the same order on regret and constraint violation. For strongly convex losses, the regret bound improves to $O(\log T)$ with a violation bound of the same order. With a minor modification, the framework also applies to adversarial constraints and provides guarantees for hard constraint violation.

AI.tldr: An anytime primal-dual framework for constrained online convex optimization that achieves optimal regret and constraint violation bounds for both stochastic and adversarial constraints without requiring Slater's condition.
AI.motivation: Existing algorithms for constrained online convex optimization face limitations: stochastic constraint algorithms require strong regularity assumptions like Slater's condition, while adversarial constraint algorithms use restrictive round-wise feasible comparators. There's a need for a unified framework that bridges this gap and works without these restrictive assumptions.
AI.method: Proposes an anytime primal-dual framework that incorporates an adaptive regularizer into the dual update. The regularizer stabilizes the dual process without relying on the negative drift induced by Slater's condition. The framework handles both stochastic and adversarial constraints, and can be modified for different settings (convex vs strongly convex losses).
AI.result: For stochastic constraints with convex losses: O(√T) expected regret and O(√T log T) expected cumulative constraint violation, plus high-probability bounds of same order. For strongly convex losses: O(log T) regret with same-order violation bounds. With minor modification, the framework also applies to adversarial constraints with guarantees for hard constraint violation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed primal-dual framework with adaptive regularization successfully bridges the gap between stochastic and adversarial constraint algorithms, achieving optimal regret and violation bounds without requiring restrictive assumptions like Slater's condition or round-wise feasible comparators.

title: One Reflection Is Not Enough: Self-Correcting Autonomous Research via Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution
summary: Autonomous research agents can now draft hypotheses, write code, run experiments, and produce papers, but they remain brittle when experiments fail. Under the prevailing paradigm, failure recovery is usually delegated to a single free-form reflection: a rich trajectory of metrics, logs, and design choices is compressed into one verbal critique, which often leads either to localized trial-and-error or to hard pivots that discard useful context. We propose SAGE, a Self-correcting, Autonomous, Grounded Experimenter, to tackle this failure-recovery bottleneck. Its core mechanism, Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution (MHFA), treats recovery as a structured causal diagnosis. By analyzing dynamic trajectory features, MHFA systematically generates multiple evidence-grounded explanations for a failure, independently evaluates their severity, and deterministically routes the verified root cause to the correct intervention level (hypothesis, experimental design, or implementation). To guarantee scientific honesty, SAGE further employs a grounded reporting mechanism that explicitly constrains drafted results to actual measured values, redacting hallucinated numbers. On a 12-topic, 5-domain benchmark, SAGE increases metrics-bearing outputs from 42% to 92% over a reflection baseline, improves artifact quality from 5.00 to 6.75/10, and blindly outscores AI-Scientist-v2 (52.0 vs. 48.2), with gains concentrated in code development and execution. While fully autonomous scientific writing and generating conference-ready papers remain notoriously difficult open problems for the entire field, SAGE successfully produces significantly more reliable and higher-quality scientific artifacts. Ultimately, by coupling structured recovery with explicit grounding constraints, SAGE significantly outperforms monolithic reflection paradigms, establishing a highly trustworthy foundation for future autonomous research.

AI.tldr: SAGE introduces structured failure recovery for autonomous research agents using Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution to systematically diagnose failures and route them to appropriate interventions, significantly improving output reliability and quality.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous research agents are brittle when experiments fail, with failure recovery limited to single free-form reflections that often lead to inefficient trial-and-error or wasteful pivots that discard useful context. This creates a bottleneck in autonomous scientific discovery.
AI.method: SAGE uses Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution (MHFA) to analyze dynamic trajectory features, systematically generate multiple evidence-grounded explanations for failures, evaluate their severity independently, and deterministically route verified root causes to correct intervention levels (hypothesis, experimental design, or implementation). It also employs a grounded reporting mechanism that constrains drafted results to actual measured values to prevent hallucination.
AI.result: On a 12-topic, 5-domain benchmark: increased metrics-bearing outputs from 42% to 92% over reflection baseline; improved artifact quality from 5.00 to 6.75/10; outperformed AI-Scientist-v2 (52.0 vs. 48.2); with gains concentrated in code development and execution.
AI.conclusion: SAGE significantly outperforms monolithic reflection paradigms by coupling structured recovery with explicit grounding constraints, establishing a highly trustworthy foundation for future autonomous research, though fully autonomous scientific writing and conference-ready papers remain open challenges for the field.

title: Fork-Think with Confidence
summary: Parallel thinking has enjoyed great success for boosting LLM performance on reasoning tasks without the need for any re-training. However, existing methods follow a think-first-then-decide paradigm, i.e., they first sample multiple reasoning paths, which inevitably leads to overgeneration, then prune or stop unnecessary paths to compensate. In contrast, decide-first-then-think, i.e., first identifying points that are likely to lead to desirable generations, has been underexplored so far. Following this paradigm, we propose Fork-think with confidence, that first identifies forking points using model confidence in a single seeding path, then triggers thinking, sampling multiple continuations and aggregating them for the final response. Our experiments across three models and three reasoning benchmarks show that Fork-think reduces the token consumption by up to 30% and run-time by up to 57%, while performing comparable to or better than parallel thinking. Our analysis reveals that Fork-think is able to identify forking points that are meaningful with respect to the downstream task and that sampling at later positions can lead to substantially better generations. Finally, we demonstrate how combining Fork-think with existing mechanisms such as early stopping and weighted voting can further boost the performance and perform comparably to existing state-of-the-art methods, without requiring any warm-up or offline training. Our results establish pre-determined forking as a promising research direction for efficient LLM reasoning.

AI.tldr: Fork-think is a "decide-first-then-think" approach that identifies forking points using model confidence in a single seeding path before sampling multiple continuations, achieving comparable or better performance than parallel thinking with significantly reduced token consumption and runtime.
AI.motivation: Existing parallel thinking methods follow a "think-first-then-decide" paradigm that leads to overgeneration by sampling multiple reasoning paths first and then pruning unnecessary ones. The "decide-first-then-think" paradigm has been underexplored, where points likely to lead to desirable generations are identified first.
AI.method: Fork-think first identifies forking points using model confidence in a single seeding path. It then triggers thinking by sampling multiple continuations from these identified points and aggregates them for the final response. Can be combined with existing mechanisms like early stopping and weighted voting.
AI.result: Experiments across three models and three reasoning benchmarks show Fork-think reduces token consumption by up to 30% and runtime by up to 57% while performing comparable to or better than parallel thinking. Analysis shows it identifies meaningful forking points relative to downstream tasks, and sampling at later positions leads to substantially better generations.
AI.conclusion: Pre-determined forking is a promising research direction for efficient LLM reasoning. Fork-think demonstrates that combining it with existing mechanisms can achieve state-of-the-art performance without requiring warm-up or offline training.

title: Surprise as a Signal for Plasticity and Metacognition
summary: We study a single idea across two settings: that a prediction-error signal, computed by a small predictor over the latent space of a frozen encoder, can serve both as a gate on plasticity and as a substrate for metacognition. In the first system, a non-parametric episodic memory writes a new concept only when this surprise is high, and a periodic offline replay phase consolidates recent traces into a slow linear readout. On a continual stream of 1000 ImageNet classes with a frozen DINOv2 or I-JEPA backbone, the consolidation phase recovers 17.7 points of retention on the oldest classes for DINOv2 and 51.3 points for I-JEPA (single-seed runs), and an ablation shows that replaying only a recent window is worse than no replay at all. In few-shot evaluation the same memory reaches 91.6% on 5-way 1-shot mini-ImageNet, above a task-specific baseline, while a harder 500-way regime exposes the true difficulty. In the second system, the same surprise signal, computed in a shared text-image space, modulates the behaviour of a vision-language model: it answers assertively when a concept is known, hedges when it is partially familiar, and refuses to identify the object and asks for an explanation when it is novel, learning the concept from a single user utterance. The external detector separates known from novel concepts at an AUROC of 0.966 (95% CI +/-0.024), far above the model's own verbalised confidence (0.618), while its token-level confidence sits below chance under greedy decoding; after a sleep phase that empties the fast store, the system recalls 99.2% of fifty taught facts from the consolidated store while a base model recovers none. We report both systems as proof-of-concept, with explicit limitations, and position the second against recent episodic-memory and personalised-VLM work.

AI.tldr: A dual-system approach using prediction-error signals as both plasticity gates and metacognition substrates, showing effective continual learning in vision systems and intelligent concept handling in vision-language models.
AI.motivation: To explore how prediction-error signals can serve dual purposes: as gates for plasticity in continual learning systems and as substrates for metacognition in AI systems, enabling more intelligent and adaptive behavior.
AI.method: Two systems: 1) Non-parametric episodic memory with surprise-based writing and offline consolidation replay using frozen backbones (DINOv2/I-JEPA); 2) Vision-language model where surprise modulates behavior based on concept familiarity, with assertive/hedged/refusal responses and single-example learning.
AI.result: System 1: Achieved 17.7 points retention improvement on oldest classes for DINOv2 and 51.3 for I-JEPA; reached 91.6% on 5-way 1-shot mini-ImageNet; System 2: External detector achieved AUROC 0.966 vs model's own 0.618; after consolidation phase, recalled 99.2% of taught facts vs base model's 0%.
AI.conclusion: The prediction-error signal effectively serves dual roles for plasticity gating and metacognition, enabling efficient continual learning and adaptive behavior in AI systems, though with explicit limitations as proof-of-concept systems.

title: RaBitQCache: Rotated Binary Quantization for KVCache in Long Context LLM Inference
summary: Long-context Large Language Model inference is severely bottlenecked by the massive Key-Value (KV) cache, yet existing sparse attention methods often suffer from static fixed-budget (Top-k) retrieval or rely on proxy scores that are computationally expensive and biased. To address these limitations, we propose RaBitQCache, a novel sparse attention framework that utilizes randomized rotated binary quantization and high-throughput binary-INT4 arithmetic to efficiently estimate attention weights. Our proxy score serves as an unbiased estimator with a proven error bound, enabling adaptive Top-p retrieval that dynamically adjusts the token budget based on actual attention sparsity. We further implement a hardware-aware system with asynchronous pipelining and lazy updates to mask overhead. Evaluations demonstrate that RaBitQCache significantly accelerates inference and reduces memory I/O while preserving generation quality compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/Sakuraaa0/RaBitQCache.git.

AI.tldr: RaBitQCache is a sparse attention framework that uses randomized rotated binary quantization and binary-INT4 arithmetic to efficiently estimate attention weights, enabling adaptive Top-p retrieval for KV cache reduction while preserving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked by massive KV cache requirements, and existing sparse attention methods have limitations: fixed-budget (Top-k) retrieval or computationally expensive, biased proxy scores.
AI.method: Proposes RaBitQCache using randomized rotated binary quantization and high-throughput binary-INT4 arithmetic to estimate attention weights efficiently. Implements hardware-aware system with asynchronous pipelining and lazy updates to mask overhead.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant inference acceleration and memory I/O reduction while preserving generation quality compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Provides unbiased estimator with proven error bound enabling adaptive Top-p retrieval.
AI.conclusion: RaBitQCache effectively addresses KV cache bottlenecks in long-context LLM inference through efficient sparse attention with adaptive token budgets, maintaining quality while improving performance and memory efficiency.

title: Design and Implementation of Agentic Orchestrations and Orchestration of Agents
summary: Agentic Business Process Management has gained momentum recently. The prospect is that the autonomy of AI agents, i.e., predominantly LLM-based agents, can be balanced with a certain level of robustness, tractability, and traceability through a combination with process technology. In this paper, we provide a classification framework for agentic orchestration options along properties such as task specificity, traceability and tractability, autonomy and reactivity, and correctness assurance and present qualitative decision criteria for realizations of different scenarios. We also provide metrics for the quantitative assessment of realization properties and show them through different agentic implementations of a predictive light sensing scenario. Altogether, this work aims at providing properties, criteria, and metrics for the design and implementation of agentic orchestrations and orchestration of agents.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a classification framework and metrics for designing and implementing AI agent orchestrations in business processes, balancing autonomy with robustness, tractability, and traceability.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of balancing the autonomy of AI agents (predominantly LLM-based) with requirements for robustness, tractability, and traceability in business process management, as agentic approaches gain momentum.
AI.method: The authors develop a classification framework for agentic orchestration options along key properties: task specificity, traceability and tractability, autonomy and reactivity, and correctness assurance. They provide qualitative decision criteria and quantitative metrics for assessment, demonstrated through different agentic implementations of a predictive light sensing scenario.
AI.result: The paper provides a structured framework with properties, criteria, and metrics for designing agentic orchestrations, demonstrated through a concrete predictive light sensing scenario to show practical implementation variations.
AI.conclusion: This work aims to provide systematic guidance for designing and implementing agentic orchestrations and orchestration of agents, enabling better balance between AI agent autonomy and the necessary robustness, tractability, and traceability required in business process management contexts.

title: On the Convergence of Self-Improving Online LLM Alignment
summary: The Self-Improving Alignment (SAIL) algorithm addresses distribution shift by reducing a bilevel formulation of the problem to an efficient, single-level method. Empirically, SAIL has demonstrated strong performance on this task. However, a formal analysis of its convergence properties has been lacking. We identify a key theoretical challenge: the standard SAIL objective function is not guaranteed to be strongly concave due to unfavorable properties of its Hessian. To address this limitation, we propose a regularized objective, SAIL-RevKL, which incorporates a reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence penalty to improve the optimization landscape. Our central theoretical contribution is to prove that this regularized objective satisfies the Polyak-Lojasiewicz (PL) condition within a bounded parameter space. We establish global convergence guarantees, achieving a near-linear sample complexity. We further validate the effectiveness and stability of SAIL-RevKL through empirical evaluations, demonstrating that it outperforms the vanilla SAIL on both MuJoCo benchmarks and LLM alignment tasks.

AI.tldr: SAIL-RevKL: A regularized version of SAIL algorithm with reverse KL divergence penalty that ensures strong concavity and provides theoretical convergence guarantees while maintaining strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: The original SAIL algorithm has shown strong empirical performance for addressing distribution shift through bilevel optimization, but lacks formal convergence analysis due to theoretical challenges with its objective function not being guaranteed to be strongly concave.
AI.method: Proposed SAIL-RevKL, which incorporates a reverse KL divergence penalty into the SAIL objective to create a regularized objective that improves the optimization landscape and satisfies the Polyak-Lojasiewicz (PL) condition within bounded parameter space.
AI.result: Established global convergence guarantees with near-linear sample complexity, and empirically validated that SAIL-RevKL outperforms vanilla SAIL on both MuJoCo benchmarks and LLM alignment tasks with better effectiveness and stability.
AI.conclusion: The SAIL-RevKL regularization successfully addresses the theoretical limitations of vanilla SAIL by ensuring strong concavity and providing formal convergence guarantees, while maintaining and even improving empirical performance across different application domains.

title: A time-series classification framework for individual-level absenteeism prediction under severe class imbalance
summary: Staff absenteeism imposes substantial operational costs in high-demand work environments such as healthcare, emergency services, meat processing, construction, and courier and delivery services, where proactive workforce planning depends on reliable individual-level absence prediction. Existing regression and classification approaches share a structural limitation; they map features observed at time t to labels at the same time t, reproducing already-realised outcomes rather than predicting future events, and discard the sequential behavioural structure inherent in individual attendance histories. We propose a Time Series Classification (TSC) framework that separates historical attendance sequences from future absence labels, enabling genuinely proactive prediction. Due to the lack of public longitudinal attendance data, we construct a reproducible simulated dataset calibrated to the UCI dataset. We analyse Binary Focal Loss (BFL) and Geometric Mean (G-Mean) loss under severe class imbalance using only the imbalance ratio $ρ$. For BFL, the initial gradient ratio is $ρα/(1-α)$, implying the balanced weight $α= 1/(1+ρ) \approx 0.023$. Experiments show that performance is governed mainly by $α$, with BFL achieving specificity 0.813 and balanced accuracy 0.888, comparable to G-Mean. Unlike BFL, G-Mean adapts automatically without parameter calibration. Among three deep learning architectures evaluated, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and the hybrid LSTM-Fully Convolutional Network (LSTM-FCN), the LSTM-FCN delivers strong precision and specificity. Stable performance is obtained with batch sizes >= 64 and window sizes between 40-80 days, yielding balanced accuracy of approximately 80% on held-out test data.

AI.tldr: A time series classification framework for predicting individual staff absenteeism using attendance sequences, addressing class imbalance with Binary Focal Loss and G-Mean loss, achieving ~80% balanced accuracy with LSTM-FCN architecture.
AI.motivation: Staff absenteeism imposes substantial operational costs in high-demand work environments where proactive workforce planning requires reliable individual-level absence prediction. Existing regression and classification approaches have structural limitations as they map features at time t to labels at the same time t, reproducing already-realized outcomes rather than predicting future events, and discard the sequential behavioral structure inherent in individual attendance histories.
AI.method: Propose a Time Series Classification (TSC) framework that separates historical attendance sequences from future absence labels, enabling genuinely proactive prediction. Construct a reproducible simulated dataset calibrated to UCI dataset due to lack of public longitudinal attendance data. Analyze Binary Focal Loss (BFL) and Geometric Mean (G-Mean) loss under severe class imbalance using only the imbalance ratio ρ. For BFL, determine balanced weight α=1/(1+ρ)≈0.023. Evaluate three deep learning architectures: LSTM, CNN, and hybrid LSTM-FCN.
AI.result: BFL achieves specificity 0.813 and balanced accuracy 0.888, comparable to G-Mean. G-Mean adapts automatically without parameter calibration unlike BFL. LSTM-FCN delivers strong precision and specificity. Stable performance obtained with batch sizes ≥64 and window sizes between 40-80 days, yielding balanced accuracy of approximately 80% on held-out test data. Performance governed mainly by α parameter in BFL.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TSC framework effectively addresses the limitation of existing approaches by enabling genuine proactive prediction of absenteeism using sequential attendance data. The hybrid LSTM-FCN architecture combined with appropriate loss functions (BFL or G-Mean) and optimized parameters (batch sizes ≥64, window sizes 40-80 days) provides a practical solution for workforce planning in high-demand industries, achieving ~80% balanced accuracy despite severe class imbalance challenges.

title: Beyond the Expressivity-Trainability Paradox: A Dynamical Lie Algebra Perspective on Navigating Barren Plateaus in Quantum Machine Learning
summary: As Quantum Machine Learning (QML) transitions toward practical implementation, the field faces a critical architectural bottleneck that challenges the fundamental assumptions of classical statistical learning theory. In classical deep learning, increasing model capacity typically risks overfitting. However, this study advances a counter-intuitive paradigm: unstructured contemporary QML architectures suffer from a profound state of quantum underfitting, driven by the "expressivity-trainability paradox." We demonstrate that the vast Hilbert space capacity of Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs)-traditionally chased as the source of quantum advantage is the direct mathematical cause of Barren Plateaus (BPs), where gradient landscapes become exponentially flat. By synthesizing recent breakthroughs in Dynamical Lie Algebras (DLAs) and Geometric QML, we establish a comprehensive framework linking the algebraic dimension of circuit generators to their optimization dynamics. Furthermore, we empirically validate this framework on a non-linear binary classification task, illuminating a uniquely quantum manifestation of the bias-variance tradeoff: while unstructured architectures achieve near-perfect training accuracy via unscalable parameterization (quantum overfitting), embedding group-theoretic geometric priors acts as a structural regularizer. By restricting the DLA growth to a polynomial regime, our symmetry-preserving approach sacrifices raw memorization capacity to guarantee scalable, gradient-rich training landscapes, offering a robust roadmap for "Trainability-by-Design" in scalable quantum neural networks.

AI.tldr: QML architectures suffer from quantum underfitting due to the expressivity-trainability paradox where high Hilbert space capacity causes Barren Plateaus; geometric priors and symmetry-preserving designs enable scalable training.
AI.motivation: As QML moves toward practical implementation, it faces a critical architectural bottleneck challenging classical learning theory assumptions. The field needs to address the paradox where unstructured QML architectures suffer from quantum underfitting despite their vast capacity.
AI.method: The study synthesizes Dynamical Lie Algebras (DLAs) and Geometric QML to link algebraic dimension of circuit generators to optimization dynamics. Uses group-theoretic geometric priors as structural regularizers that restrict DLA growth to polynomial regimes. Empirically validates on non-linear binary classification.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the vast Hilbert space capacity of PQCs causes Barren Plateaus where gradients become exponentially flat. Shows symmetry-preserving approaches sacrifice raw memorization but guarantee scalable, gradient-rich training landscapes by restricting DLA growth to polynomial regimes.
AI.conclusion: Embedding group-theoretic geometric priors acts as structural regularization, offering a "Trainability-by-Design" roadmap for scalable quantum neural networks that addresses the quantum bias-variance tradeoff and enables practical QML implementation.

title: Modality-Driven Search with Holistic Trace Judging for ARC-AGI-2
comment: 37 pages, 4 figures; source code available at https://github.com/beetree/ARC-AGI
summary:
Large language models can produce fluent, internally coherent reasoning traces for abstract reasoning tasks while still being confidently wrong - making selection among candidates, not just generation, the central challenge. I present a solver for ARC-AGI-2, a few-shot visual reasoning benchmark, built around two principles: (i) treating reasoning modalities as search operators, generating diverse candidates independently across text, image, and code channels, and (ii) context-preserving holistic judging, in which a judge model jointly compares all candidate reasoning traces within a single long-context prompt. Unlike self-consistency or majority voting, this approach reliably recovers correct minority hypotheses on tasks where the modal answer is wrong.
  On the ARC Prize semi-private evaluation set, the solver achieves 72.9 percent at USD 38.99 per task - the highest score on the verified leaderboard at the time of writing, exceeding the best standalone frontier models, GPT-5.2 Pro at 54.2 percent and Gemini 3 Pro at 54.0 percent, by +18.7 percentage points. On the public evaluation set, it achieves 76.1 percent at USD 19.69 per task. I release the full source code and document extensive negative results, including the finding that prescriptive prompting templates and iterative refinement systematically reduce hypothesis diversity and degrade performance.

AI.tldr: A novel solver for visual reasoning benchmark uses multi-modal candidate generation with holistic judging, outperforming frontier LLMs like GPT-5.2 Pro and Gemini 3 Pro on ARC-AGI-2 tasks.
AI.motivation: Large language models can produce fluent but incorrect reasoning traces for abstract reasoning tasks, making candidate selection (not just generation) the key challenge in solving complex visual reasoning benchmarks.
AI.method: Two-principle approach: (1) Treat reasoning modalities as search operators generating diverse candidates across text, image, and code channels independently, (2) Context-preserving holistic judging where a judge model jointly compares all candidate reasoning traces within a single long-context prompt.
AI.result: Achieves 72.9% accuracy on ARC Prize semi-private evaluation set at $38.99 per task (highest on leaderboard), outperforming GPT-5.2 Pro (54.2%) and Gemini 3 Pro (54.0%) by +18.7 percentage points. On public set: 76.1% at $19.69 per task.
AI.conclusion: The holistic judging approach reliably recovers correct minority hypotheses where majority voting fails, and the paper releases full source code while noting that prescriptive prompting and iterative refinement reduce hypothesis diversity and degrade performance.

title: Introduction to Stochastic Differential Equations for Generative Machine Learning: A Variational Perspective
summary: The use of ordinary and stochastic differential equations has led to substantial progress in generative machine learning with applications to, for example, image, video and biomolecule generation. This paper provides a self-contained and informal introduction to the differential equations, the probabilistic framework for using them in generative modeling and the Fokker--Planck equation that governs the temporal evolution of the marginal distribution of the stochastic variables of the differential equations. The variational lower bound on the log-likelihood (the evidence lower bound, ELBO) is derived and used as a general starting point for a discussion of diffusion models, score matching, and flow matching. All of these approaches may be viewed as specific parameterizations of the most general variational approach. A one-dimensional density modeling problem is used as a simple example to compare different parameterizations.

AI.tldr: This paper provides an introductory overview of differential equation-based generative models, explaining how diffusion models, score matching, and flow matching are all specific parameterizations of a general variational framework for generative modeling.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to provide an accessible introduction to differential equations in generative machine learning, as they have shown substantial progress in applications like image, video, and biomolecule generation but can be mathematically challenging.
AI.method: The paper offers a self-contained informal introduction to ordinary and stochastic differential equations, the probabilistic framework for generative modeling, the Fokker-Planck equation, and derives the ELBO as a general starting point. It then shows how diffusion models, score matching, and flow matching are specific parameterizations of this variational approach.
AI.result: The paper establishes a unified framework showing that diffusion models, score matching, and flow matching are all specific instances of a general variational approach, and demonstrates this through a one-dimensional density modeling problem comparing different parameterizations.
AI.conclusion: This work provides an accessible bridge between mathematical theory and practical applications of differential equation-based generative models, unifying various approaches under a single variational framework to help readers understand their relationships and differences.

title: ACE: Pluggable Adaptive Context Elasticizer across Agents
summary: The increasing complexity of agentic tasks has led to rapidly growing trajectory lengths, which poses significant challenges for large language model (LLM) based agents with fixed context windows. Existing context management techniques, such as truncation and summarization, suffer from inherent inflexibility and irreversibility: once information is discarded or compressed, it cannot be recovered even when it becomes critically relevant in later decision steps. To address these limitations, we propose the Adaptive Context Elasticizer (ACE), a plug-and-play module that elastically orchestrates historical step information into the agent's context at each decision step. ACE maintains a lossless message maintenance layer that stores both raw messages and compressed abstractions for each historical step, while a context orchestration layer adaptively assigns each step an elastic type as raw, abstract, or drop, at every decision step based on the current task state. This reversible design ensures that the main LLM always receives a compact yet information-rich context. We adapt ACE to four diverse agent frameworks, including ReAct, DeepAgent, WebThinker, and MiroFlow, without training or architectural modifications. Experiments show that ACE consistently outperforms truncation and summarization baselines, and brings consistent performance gains across all four agent frameworks.

AI.tldr: ACE is a plug-and-play module that dynamically manages context for LLM-based agents by elastically selecting between raw, abstracted, or dropped historical information based on current task state, outperforming traditional truncation/summarization methods.
AI.motivation: Increasing complexity of agentic tasks leads to rapidly growing trajectory lengths that exceed LLM context windows. Existing context management techniques like truncation and summarization suffer from inflexibility and irreversibility - once information is discarded or compressed, it cannot be recovered even when it becomes critically relevant later.
AI.method: ACE has two layers: (1) a lossless message maintenance layer that stores both raw messages and compressed abstractions for each historical step, and (2) a context orchestration layer that adaptively assigns each step an elastic type (raw, abstract, or drop) at every decision step based on current task state. This enables reversible context management where the main LLM always receives compact yet information-rich context.
AI.result: ACE was adapted to four diverse agent frameworks (ReAct, DeepAgent, WebThinker, and MiroFlow) without training or architectural modifications. Experiments show ACE consistently outperforms truncation and summarization baselines, and brings consistent performance gains across all four agent frameworks.
AI.conclusion: ACE provides an effective solution to the context window limitation problem in LLM-based agents through its elastic, reversible context management approach, demonstrating consistent improvements over existing methods across multiple agent frameworks without requiring modifications to the underlying systems.

title: Robustness of neural networks to random noise perturbations of their inputs
summary: We investigate the problem of the robustness of a trained neural network to the perturbation of its input values. More specifically, we examine the interplay between the accuracy of the network, as measured by the mean squared error, and robustness. Accordingly, we present a robustness measure, which, with high probability, suggests an upper bound on the mean squared error of the network, with respect to an input data set, for a given perturbation of the input values of the network. The measure we propose is both simple and efficient to compute, treating the neural network as a black box. We provide experimental results on several real-world data sets showing the efficacy of the proposed method. We also introduce the concept of robustness curves, which allows us to further analyse robustness within and between data sets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a simple, efficient black-box robustness measure for neural networks that bounds mean squared error under input perturbations, with validation on real-world datasets using robustness curves for analysis.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to investigate the relationship between neural network accuracy (measured by mean squared error) and robustness to input perturbations. The motivation is to understand how networks maintain performance when inputs are perturbed, which is crucial for real-world applications where inputs may be noisy or adversarial.
AI.method: The authors propose a robustness measure that treats neural networks as black boxes. The measure computes an upper bound on the mean squared error for given input perturbations with high probability. It's designed to be simple and computationally efficient. They also introduce "robustness curves" to analyze robustness patterns within and between datasets.
AI.result: Experimental results on several real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed robustness measure. The robustness curves provide additional insights into robustness characteristics, allowing for comparative analysis across different datasets and network configurations.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully develops a practical, black-box approach for measuring neural network robustness to input perturbations. The proposed method provides theoretical guarantees (upper bounds with high probability) while remaining computationally tractable. The robustness curves offer a valuable tool for analyzing and comparing robustness properties in different contexts.

title: Which Tokens Matter? Adaptive Token Selection for RLVR with the Relative Surprisal Index
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a powerful tool for propelling Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond imitation-based training towards more robust reasoning capabilities. Among existing approaches, RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a pivotal paradigm for advancing LLM reasoning. Despite its empirical success, recent studies have offered different insights. One line of inquiry advocates prioritizing high-entropy token positions during training, while another perspective cautions against allowing low-probability tokens to dominate gradient updates. Notably, although high-entropy tokens are usually correlated with low probability, both paradigms empirically yield substantial performance gains. In this work, we argue that evaluating sampled-token probability or entropy in isolation is insufficient to capture the policy optimization dynamics. To resolve this tension, we introduce the Relative Surprisal Index (RSI), a principled, information-theoretic metric that naturally couples the token's entropy with the probability of the selected token. We show that, under mild conditions, RSI is related to the local ratio between the first-order variations of the logit-gradient norm and predictive entropy under a selected-logit perturbation. Building on RSI, we propose RSI Selection (RSI-S), an entropy-adaptive token filtering method that retains tokens within a stable RSI interval. RSI-S successfully reconciles previous contradictory paradigms and filters out both redundant low-surprisal tokens and unstable high-surprisal tail tokens. Empirical evaluations show that RSI-S achieves higher avg@32 accuracy across different model scales (Qwen2.5-1.5B, 3B, and 7B) on AIME and AMC benchmarks: RSI-S improves avg@32 accuracy by 2--3 percentage points over GRPO. Overall, RSI offers a promising perspective for RLVR improvement.

AI.tldr: RSI-S is a new RL method that reconciles conflicting paradigms in LLM reasoning training by using Relative Surprisal Index to filter tokens in a stable entropy-probability range.
AI.motivation: There's a tension in RL for LLM reasoning between prioritizing high-entropy token positions and avoiding low-probability tokens dominating gradient updates. Current approaches evaluate probability or entropy in isolation, which is insufficient for understanding policy optimization dynamics.
AI.method: Introduced Relative Surprisal Index (RSI) - an information-theoretic metric coupling token entropy with selected token probability. Showed RSI relates to local ratio of logit-gradient norm and predictive entropy variations. Built RSI-S method that filters tokens within a stable RSI interval, removing both redundant low-surprisal and unstable high-surprisal tail tokens.
AI.result: RSI-S improves avg@32 accuracy by 2-3 percentage points over GRPO across Qwen2.5 models (1.5B, 3B, 7B) on AIME and AMC benchmarks. Successfully reconciles contradictory paradigms in RLVR.
AI.conclusion: RSI provides a principled perspective for improving RL with Verifiable Rewards, offering a unified framework that resolves previous contradictory paradigms and achieves better performance.

title: Evil Spectra: How Optimisers can Amplify or Suppress Emergent Misalignment
summary: Emergent misalignment (EM) is a recently discovered phenomenon in LLMs where fine-tuning on a narrow misaligned task, such as writing insecure code, leads to broadly misaligned behaviour on unrelated prompts. Previous work has noted that the severity of EM is highly sensitive to training choices; however, we still lack a systematic characterisation of this sensitivity. We perform a sweep over several Qwen3 models, optimisers, datasets, and batch sizes, and find that the choice of optimiser has the largest effect, producing a 7x spread in misalignment rate. Surprisingly, model size has a negligible effect within the Qwen3 family. An additional sweep over 12 models from three families using Adam confirms that model scale (1B-235B) and family have negligible effects for that optimiser. Analysing the loss-alignment relationship on Qwen3-8B, we find that final log training loss is a strong predictor of alignment, and that stratifying by optimiser captures nearly all the residual variance. Training dynamics reveal that each optimiser follows a different trajectory through loss-alignment space, and that after significant training, the optimiser becomes more important than training loss as a predictor of alignment. Muon, the adaptive optimiser that preserves alignment the best, implicitly regularises for a more uniform distribution of singular values of the LoRA adapter. We evaluate this insight by training with an additional loss term that incentivises a flatter singular value spectrum, and find that this substantially recovers alignment for the more EM-prone adaptive optimisers (Adam and Lion), with negligible cost to training loss. These results identify optimiser choice as a key factor in EM severity, but show that spectral regularisation can substantially mitigate the effects of EM-prone optimisers.

AI.tldr: Study finds optimiser choice has biggest impact on emergent misalignment in LLMs, with model size having negligible effect. Spectral regularization can mitigate EM-prone optimisers.
AI.motivation: Emergent misalignment in LLMs causes broad misaligned behavior from fine-tuning on narrow misaligned tasks, but sensitivity to training choices lacks systematic characterization.
AI.method: Swept over Qwen3 models, optimisers, datasets, batch sizes; analyzed loss-alignment relationship; investigated training dynamics; examined singular value distributions; tested spectral regularization.
AI.result: Optimiser choice produces 7x spread in misalignment rate; model size has negligible effect; final training loss predicts alignment; Muon optimiser best preserves alignment; spectral regularization recovers alignment for EM-prone optimisers.
AI.conclusion: Optimiser choice is key factor in EM severity, but spectral regularization can substantially mitigate effects of EM-prone optimisers.

title: Scientific Explanations in Health Sciences: Causality, Trust, and Epistemic Adequacy
summary: Medical Artificial Intelligence (AI) is widely expected to transform clinical practice, yet the decision-making processes of many Machine Learning (ML) models remain opaque. Explainability has been advanced as a partial remedy to clarify why AI generates predictions, particularly in high-stakes contexts. Despite ongoing efforts, debates on what constitutes an adequate medical explanation remain unsettled. Yet, explanation has long been a central topic of inquiry in the philosophy of science and medicine. The insights developed in these fields, however, have been largely overlooked in contemporary explainable AI (XAI) research, leaving its foundational assumptions insufficiently examined. To address this gap, this paper develops a critical review at the intersection of philosophy of science and XAI. It examines prevailing accounts of what counts as an explanation in the health sciences and assesses their adequacy for informing XAI in medicine, arguing that they provide necessary conditions for a philosophically grounded approach to explainability in this domain. Building on this foundational philosophical literature, the discussion identifies three central axes of analysis: the role of causality in medical reasoning, the epistemic and relational dimensions of medical trust, and the criteria of explanatory adequacy as shaped by the pragmatic needs of diverse stakeholders. By integrating philosophical analysis with current developments in medical AI, the paper outlines principles for designing XAI systems that offer explanations that are not only epistemically robust but also aligned with the epistemic and practical requirements of clinical decision-making, shaping ongoing debates in medical XAI toward underexplored conceptual foundations.

AI.tldr: This paper bridges philosophy of science with medical explainable AI (XAI), arguing that philosophical insights about explanation are essential for developing epistemically robust and clinically useful AI explanations.
AI.motivation: Current medical AI models are opaque despite high-stakes clinical applications, and existing XAI research overlooks foundational philosophical insights about what constitutes adequate explanation in medicine, leaving XAI's assumptions insufficiently examined.
AI.method: Critical review at the intersection of philosophy of science and XAI, examining prevailing accounts of explanation in health sciences and assessing their adequacy for informing medical XAI, identifying three central analytical axes.
AI.result: Identifies necessary conditions for philosophically grounded XAI in medicine and outlines principles for designing XAI systems that offer explanations meeting both epistemic robustness and clinical decision-making requirements.
AI.conclusion: Philosophical analysis from science and medicine provides essential foundations for developing adequate medical AI explanations, guiding XAI toward addressing causality, medical trust, and stakeholder pragmatic needs in clinical contexts.

title: Calibration, Not Compilation: Detecting and Repairing Misspecified Probabilistic Programs Written by Language Models
summary: Language models increasingly write probabilistic programs (in NumPyro, Stan, or Pyro), but a program that compiles, runs, and passes every unit test can still be \emph{statistically} wrong -- a Gaussian likelihood for heavy-tailed data, a Poisson for over-dispersed counts, an invalid prior support, or a pathological parameterization. The right verifier is therefore not a test suite but the Bayesian workflow itself: posterior predictive checks, simulation-based calibration, sampler diagnostics ($\hat R$, divergences, ESS), and held-out predictive density. We study this calibration oracle along three axes. \textbf{Detection:} on a benchmark of $14$ misspecification types across $10$ model families ($200$ instances), it flags the bug with AUC $0.97$ ($88\%$ at $2\%$ FPR \emph{when handed the correct reference program, an upper bound}) -- and a fully \emph{reference-free} version that uses no correct program reaches $62$--$78\%$ (the upper figure from a small automated model search), versus $0\%$ for a unit-test oracle. \textbf{Repair:} used as feedback in an LLM repair loop across fifteen models, calibration significantly outperforms unit-test feedback -- which is itself \emph{significantly worse than no feedback at all}, a passing test inducing false confidence that suppresses repair -- and improves over no feedback on strong-but-unsaturated models (GPT-5.1 $33{\to}92\%$, Claude $75{\to}100\%$; paired McNemar, $n{=}228$). \textbf{Reality:} on programs LLMs write from scratch for neutral briefs, $15$--$47\%$ of runnable ones are statistically misspecified (unit tests catch none), and calibration-guided repair significantly beats LLM-as-judge review, a Bayesian-workflow checklist, and data-summary self-debug. Across all three, the lesson is the same: for probabilistic programs, correctness is calibration, not compilation.

AI.tldr: Correctness of probabilistic programs is about statistical calibration, not just compilation and unit tests.
AI.motivation: LLMs increasingly generate probabilistic programs, but programs that compile and pass unit tests can still be statistically wrong (e.g., wrong likelihood assumptions, invalid priors). Traditional testing fails to catch statistical misspecifications.
AI.method: Proposes using Bayesian workflow as a calibration oracle: posterior predictive checks, simulation-based calibration, sampler diagnostics (R-hat, divergences, ESS), and held-out predictive density. Tests detection on 200 instances across 14 misspecification types and 10 model families, and uses calibration as feedback in LLM repair loops.
AI.result: Calibration detection achieves AUC 0.97 (88% at 2% FPR with correct reference), and reference-free version reaches 62-78% vs 0% for unit tests. In LLM repair, calibration feedback significantly outperforms unit-test feedback (which is worse than no feedback) and improves repair rates (GPT-5.1: 33→92%, Claude: 75→100%). On real LLM-generated programs, 15-47% are statistically misspecified (unit tests catch none), and calibration-guided repair beats other methods.
AI.conclusion: For probabilistic programs, correctness is calibration, not compilation. Bayesian workflow verification is essential for detecting and repairing statistical misspecifications in LLM-generated probabilistic code.

title: Think in English, Answer in Korean: Efficient Adaptation of Multilingual Tool-Using Agents
summary: We present LuckyStar 111B, a 111B-parameter hybrid reasoning model developed through a collaboration between Cohere and LG CNS for Korean-English enterprise agents under practical memory and serving constraints. The model trains from Cohere's fully post-trained Command A model rather than a new pretraining run, and uses preamble conditioning to switch between concise non-reasoning behavior and longer tool-oriented reasoning. We study four choices for scaling tool-using agents efficiently: multilingual supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tool-use tasks, language-consistency rewards for Korean user-facing responses, and 4-bit quantization for single-GPU serving. The adapted model improves mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic natural-language-to-SQL (NL2SQL) performance while preserving general Korean and English instruction-following quality. These results provide a practical recipe and failure-mode analysis for adapting post-trained multilingual models to verifiable agentic workflows under memory-constrained deployment.

AI.tldr: LuckyStar 111B is a 111B-parameter hybrid reasoning model developed for Korean-English enterprise agents that efficiently scales tool-using agents through multilingual fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, and 4-bit quantization for single-GPU serving.
AI.motivation: To develop a practical enterprise agent model for Korean-English applications under memory and serving constraints, addressing the need for efficient tool-using agents that can handle both concise responses and complex reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Uses Cohere's Command A model as base with preamble conditioning; implements multilingual supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tool-use tasks, language-consistency rewards for Korean responses, and 4-bit quantization for deployment.
AI.result: The adapted model improves mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic NL2SQL performance while maintaining general Korean and English instruction-following quality.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical framework for adapting post-trained multilingual models to verifiable agentic workflows in memory-constrained deployments, with analysis of failure modes.

title: ECHO: Prune to act, trace to learn with selective turn memory in agentic RL
summary: Long-horizon language agents must repeatedly interact with tools, accumulate evidence, and make decisions under bounded context windows. Existing context-management methods make such rollouts feasible by truncating distant history, folding past turns into summaries, or selecting compact memory states. However, these breakthroughs introduce two coupled limitations. First, as the number of turns grows, historical observations are progressively removed or collapsed into compressed states, making it harder for the policy to reuse fine-grained evidence. Second, once the original turns are no longer source-addressable, outcome-based RL loses an explicit path for aligning policy updates with the evidence that supported a successful final answer. To this end, we propose ECHO, a selective turn-memory framework that jointly addresses history collapse and traceable learning through source-indexed reconstruction. Specifically, ECHO compresses each completed environment turn into a compact memory record, reconstructs bounded policy contexts by selecting from these records, and reuses the selected source indices to route positive outcome credit to the evidence and selection actions that support successful answers. On BrowseComp-Plus, ECHO reaches 43.4% held-out accuracy, outperforming GRPO (28.9%) and the rolling-summary baseline SUPO (36.1%), while using fewer turns and lower trajectory volume than SUPO (Figure 1). Additionally, the trained policy improves zero-shot generalization across multi-objective QA, code generation, and deep information-seeking benchmarks on both dense and MoE backbones.

AI.tldr: ECHO is a selective turn-memory framework for long-horizon language agents that addresses history collapse and enables traceable learning through source-indexed reconstruction, improving performance and efficiency across various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing context-management methods for long-horizon language agents introduce two limitations: 1) historical observations are progressively removed or compressed, making it harder to reuse fine-grained evidence, and 2) once original turns are no longer source-addressable, outcome-based RL loses traceability for aligning policy updates with evidence supporting successful answers.
AI.method: ECHO compresses each completed environment turn into a compact memory record, reconstructs bounded policy contexts by selecting from these records, and reuses the selected source indices to route positive outcome credit to the evidence and selection actions that support successful answers.
AI.result: On BrowseComp-Plus, ECHO reaches 43.4% held-out accuracy, outperforming GRPO (28.9%) and SUPO (36.1%), while using fewer turns and lower trajectory volume than SUPO. The trained policy also improves zero-shot generalization across multi-objective QA, code generation, and deep information-seeking benchmarks on both dense and MoE backbones.
AI.conclusion: ECHO's selective turn-memory framework effectively addresses the coupled limitations of history collapse and traceable learning in long-horizon language agents, achieving superior performance, efficiency, and generalization capabilities compared to existing methods.

title: FARS: A Fully Automated Research System Deployed at Scale
summary: Recent automated research systems show that language-model agents can generate hypotheses, run experiments, and write complete manuscripts, but most evidence still comes from selected examples, human-framed topics, or a few pre-defined research tasks. We present FARS (Fully Automated Research System), a fully automated AI-for-AI research system designed to operate across research topics at scale. FARS autonomously generates and advances projects through ideation, planning, experimentation, and writing, using stage-specific agents coordinated through a shared workspace that records proposals, code, logs, results, and manuscripts. In its first public deployment, FARS produced 166 complete research papers spanning 67 fine-grained AI/ML topics while preserving intermediate artifacts as an auditable corpus rather than a curated set of successes. We evaluate this corpus with 282 structured reviews from volunteer reviewers covering 140 papers, including overall ratings, sub-scores, integrity checks, and LLM-use disclosure. The reviews indicate that FARS can produce review-worthy and occasionally strong AI/ML research artifacts in a large-scale public deployment, while also exposing recurring failure modes in narrow experimental scope, methodological limitations, and integrity issues.

AI.tldr: FARS is a fully automated AI research system that autonomously generates complete research papers across AI/ML topics, producing 166 papers with auditable artifacts and receiving structured human reviews.
AI.motivation: Current automated research systems are limited to selected examples, human-framed topics, or predefined tasks, lacking evidence of scalable autonomous research across diverse topics.
AI.method: FARS uses stage-specific agents (ideation, planning, experimentation, writing) coordinated through a shared workspace to autonomously advance projects, with all artifacts recorded for auditability.
AI.result: In first public deployment, FARS produced 166 complete research papers spanning 67 AI/ML topics, with 282 structured reviews showing it can produce review-worthy research but with limitations in scope and methodology.
AI.conclusion: FARS demonstrates scalable automated research is possible but reveals recurring failure modes in narrow experimental scope, methodological limitations, and integrity issues that need addressing.

title: Improving Certified Robustness via Adversarial Distillation
summary: Certified training aims to produce models whose predictions can be formally verified against adversarial perturbations, typically by optimising upper bounds on the worst-case loss over an allowed perturbation set. For neural networks, certified training methods based purely on tight relaxation bounds produce networks that are amenable to certification, but sacrifice standard accuracy. Conversely, adversarial training often yields stronger empirical robustness and standard accuracy, but the resulting models are generally difficult to certify with neural network verifiers. Recently, the literature has shown that better standard-certified accuracy trade-offs can be achieved by combining adversarial training objectives with loose over-approximations based on Interval Bound Propagation (IBP), effectively interpolating between lower and upper bounds of the worst-case loss. Building on this, we introduce AD-CERT, a certified training objective that combines adversarial distillation with an IBP upper bound. We show that distilling adversarial information over the logit space from an empirically robust teacher provides an effective lower bound surrogate for certified training, with AD-CERT achieving state-of-the-art certified performance on several robustness benchmarks. Furthermore, in a unified setup, distilling adversarial information at the logit-level is shown to improve certified accuracy over a robust feature-space distillation objective by up to 5.40 percentage points.

AI.tldr: AD-CERT combines adversarial distillation with Interval Bound Propagation to achieve state-of-the-art certified robustness performance while maintaining better standard accuracy than previous methods.
AI.motivation: Current certified training methods face a trade-off: tight relaxation bounds produce certifiable networks but sacrifice standard accuracy, while adversarial training yields better empirical robustness but creates models difficult to certify. There's a need for methods that achieve better trade-offs between standard and certified accuracy.
AI.method: AD-CERT introduces a certified training objective that combines adversarial distillation (distilling adversarial information from an empirically robust teacher over the logit space) with an IBP (Interval Bound Propagation) upper bound. This approach uses adversarial distillation as an effective lower bound surrogate for certified training.
AI.result: AD-CERT achieves state-of-the-art certified performance on several robustness benchmarks. Additionally, distilling adversarial information at the logit-level improves certified accuracy by up to 5.40 percentage points compared to robust feature-space distillation objectives in a unified setup.
AI.conclusion: Combining adversarial distillation with IBP-based certified training objectives enables better standard-certified accuracy trade-offs, with adversarial distillation over logit space providing an effective lower bound surrogate that outperforms feature-space distillation approaches for certified robustness.

title: Arena-T2I Hard: Benchmarking and Improving Faithfulness with Dependency-Aware Checklist
summary: Faithfulness -- how precisely a generated image aligns with its prompt -- is increasingly central to the real-world utility of text-to-image (T2I) models. Existing faithfulness benchmarks, however, rely on simple atomic instructions, on which top-tier systems already achieve near-perfect scores. As T2I models enter creative workflows, users issue multi-faceted requests combining intricate spatial relationships, stylistic constraints, and complex text rendering. In this setting, a single binary VLM-judge score no longer captures which specific constraints the model fails to satisfy. We introduce Arena-T2I Hard, a 310-prompt stress benchmark drawn from real arena T2I logs, with approximately 30 decomposed yes/no constraints per prompt spanning six categories, including text rendering. The strongest closed-source system we evaluate reaches 0.855 with a 33~pp performance gap across 11 systems, demonstrating substantial discriminative power. Moreover, high public-arena rankings fail to predict faithfulness, confirming that holistic Bradley-Terry (BT) preference scores prioritize aesthetics over fine-grained prompt adherence. We propose a dependency-aware checklist reward that decomposes each prompt into a DAG of yes/no questions and zeroes descendants of failed parents, turning faithfulness into a per-constraint training signal. Combined with a BT aesthetic reward via group-decoupled normalization (GDPO), which standardizes each reward within its rollout group so neither collapses, the recipe attains a strictly better faithfulness-aesthetics trade-off on SD3.5-Medium and FLUX.1-dev under MMRB2 pairwise comparisons than every single-reward, naive weighted-sum, or 4-reward BT-ensemble baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Arena-T2I Hard, a challenging benchmark for evaluating text-to-image model faithfulness with decomposed constraints, and proposes a dependency-aware checklist reward method that improves faithfulness-aesthetics trade-off.
AI.motivation: Existing faithfulness benchmarks use simple atomic instructions where top models already achieve near-perfect scores, failing to capture how models handle real-world complex prompts with intricate spatial relationships, stylistic constraints, and text rendering. Current holistic Bradley-Terry preference scores prioritize aesthetics over fine-grained prompt adherence.
AI.method: 1) Introduces Arena-T2I Hard benchmark with 310 prompts from real T2I logs, each decomposed into ~30 yes/no constraints across 6 categories including text rendering. 2) Proposes dependency-aware checklist reward that decomposes prompts into DAGs of yes/no questions and zeroes descendants of failed parents. 3) Combines this with BT aesthetic reward via group-decoupled normalization (GDPO) to prevent reward collapse.
AI.result: The benchmark shows strong discriminative power with 33pp performance gap across 11 systems, revealing that public-arena rankings don't predict faithfulness. The proposed method achieves strictly better faithfulness-aesthetics trade-off on SD3.5-Medium and FLUX.1-dev than all baselines (single-reward, weighted-sum, or 4-reward BT-ensemble).
AI.conclusion: Current T2I faithfulness evaluation is inadequate for complex real-world prompts. The Arena-T2I Hard benchmark provides granular assessment, and the dependency-aware checklist reward with GDPO offers an effective training approach to improve faithfulness while maintaining aesthetics.

title: When to Truncate a Feature Ranking: A Residual-Overlap Stopping Rule for Subset Selection
summary:
Feature rankings are widely used in supervised feature selection because they are simple, scalable and easy to interpret. Variables are first ranked by a relevance score, and a subset is then obtained by retaining the top-ranked variables. Although the first stage has been extensively studied, the second is often governed by an arbitrary cardinality, an empirical threshold or cross-validation, without a direct interpretation. This raises a basic question: given a feature ranking, when is there enough accumulated class-separation evidence to stop selecting features?
  This paper develops a distributional framework for transforming supervised feature rankings into class-independent subsets through an explicit risk-calibrated stopping rule. For each variable and each pair of classes, marginal separation is measured by the Bhattacharyya coefficient between the corresponding class-conditional distributions. The proposed method selects a single global subset shared by all classes by retaining the shortest prefix of a ranking whose residual product overlap falls below a prescribed threshold for every relevant class contrast. We derive binary and multiclass Bayes-risk bounds for the labelled product marginal problem, and obtain prior-dependent and prior-free calibrations of the residual-overlap threshold from a target all-pairs risk level.
  An empirical comparison on high-dimensional genomic datasets illustrates that the rule can reduce tens of thousands of variables to a few dozen while maintaining predictive performance statistically comparable to the all-features baseline. As the stopping rule only requires one-dimensional marginal overlap estimates and scans a precomputed ranking, it is well suited to very high-dimensional settings where exhaustive subset search is infeasible and interpretable truncation of feature rankings is essential.

AI.tldr: A method for determining when to stop selecting features from rankings using distributional risk calibration, measuring marginal separation via Bhattacharyya coefficients and selecting the shortest ranking prefix that meets risk thresholds.
AI.motivation: Feature rankings are commonly used but the stopping point for selecting top-ranked features is often arbitrary or based on empirical thresholds without direct interpretability. There's a need for a principled approach to determine when accumulated class-separation evidence is sufficient to stop feature selection.
