AI.method: Proposes a distributional framework using Bhattacharyya coefficients to measure marginal separation between class-conditional distributions for each variable. Selects a single global subset by finding the shortest ranking prefix whose residual product overlap falls below a prescribed threshold for all relevant class contrasts. Derives binary and multiclass Bayes-risk bounds and obtains prior-dependent/prior-free calibrations of the residual-overlap threshold from target all-pairs risk levels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on high-dimensional genomic datasets shows the rule can reduce tens of thousands of variables to a few dozen while maintaining predictive performance statistically comparable to using all features. The method is computationally efficient, requiring only one-dimensional marginal overlap estimates and scanning a precomputed ranking.
AI.conclusion: The proposed risk-calibrated stopping rule provides a principled, interpretable approach for truncating feature rankings that is well-suited to high-dimensional settings where exhaustive subset search is infeasible, offering both dimensionality reduction and maintained predictive performance.

title: A Self-Evolving Agentic System for Automated Generation and Execution of Biological Protocols
summary: Autonomous wet-lab experimentation requires more than plausible protocol text: biological intent, quantitative procedures, device constraints and experimental feedback must remain aligned from protocol and SOP design to code and physical execution. We developed ProtoPilot, a self-evolving multi-agent system, together with an expert-grounded benchmark and evaluation framework for testing this conversion as an experimental automation problem. The framework spans 294 synthetic-biology and molecular-biology tasks derived from 98 gold-standard protocols, wet-lab expert rubrics, device-level validity gates and real experimental tests. ProtoPilot incorporates layer-wise verifiability, multi-agent orchestration and a runtime-updated skill library to generate protocols, expand SOPs, synthesize SDK-compliant code and revise workflows from wet-lab feedback. It achieved a Top@3 expert-preference rate of 90.2%, an overall protocol-to-code gate pass rate of 89.5% and an Opentrons pass rate of 88.24%, compared with 32.35% for OpenTrons-AI. Wet-lab validation produced interpretable readouts, Sanger-confirmed products and feedback-corrected PCA-assembled DNA targets, establishing a verifiable route to autonomous experimentation. Together, these results show that the evaluation framework captures execution-relevant requirements for autonomous wet-lab automation, and that ProtoPilot can meet them by converting protocol and code generation into validated execution and feedback-guided revision.

AI.tldr: ProtoPilot is a self-evolving multi-agent system for autonomous wet-lab experimentation that converts biological protocols into executable code, achieving high accuracy (90.2% expert preference) and successful physical execution (88.24% device pass rate).
AI.motivation: Autonomous wet-lab experimentation requires more than just plausible protocol text - it needs alignment of biological intent, quantitative procedures, device constraints, and experimental feedback throughout the entire pipeline from design to physical execution.
AI.method: Developed ProtoPilot, a self-evolving multi-agent system with layer-wise verifiability, multi-agent orchestration, and runtime-updated skill library that generates protocols, expands SOPs, synthesizes SDK-compliant code, and revises workflows from wet-lab feedback. Created an evaluation framework with 294 synthetic/molecular-biology tasks from 98 gold-standard protocols, expert rubrics, device validity gates, and real experimental tests.
AI.result: ProtoPilot achieved: 90.2% Top@3 expert-preference rate, 89.5% overall protocol-to-code gate pass rate, and 88.24% Opentrons pass rate (vs 32.35% for OpenTrons-AI). Wet-lab validation produced interpretable readouts, Sanger-confirmed products, and feedback-corrected DNA assembly targets.
AI.conclusion: The evaluation framework captures execution-relevant requirements for autonomous wet-lab automation, and ProtoPilot can meet these requirements by converting protocol and code generation into validated execution and feedback-guided revision, establishing a verifiable route to autonomous experimentation.

title: Diffusing Blame: Task-Dependent Credit Assignment in Biologically Plausible Dual-Stream Networks
summary: Biological neural circuits obey Dale's principle: each neuron's synapses are uniformly excitatory or inhibitory. Artificial networks that respect this constraint must coordinate separate excitatory and inhibitory populations, fundamentally changing how credit is assigned during learning. Several biologically plausible learning rules avoid backpropagation's weight transport requirement, but it has been difficult to achieve strong performance under Dale's principle beyond MNIST. Error Diffusion (ED) was originally proposed in a dual-stream excitatory/inhibitory architecture, where learning is driven by routing global error signals to all layers without transporting transposed forward weights or relying on random feedback matrices. Whether such a rule can scale under Dale's principle across both supervised classification and reinforcement learning remains unknown. Here, we introduce modulo error routing to extend Error Diffusion beyond binary classification, and show that a dual-stream excitatory/inhibitory architecture trained with this method achieves 96.7% on MNIST and establishes a 61.7% baseline on CIFAR-10, demonstrating that representation learning is possible even when strictly enforcing Dale's principle. For the classification setting, we introduce three domain-specific innovations: layer-specific sigmoid widths, batch-centered class error signals, and asymmetric initialization, and ablation analysis reveals that their relative importance reverses between MNIST and CIFAR-10, exposing task-dependent credit-assignment bottlenecks invisible to single-benchmark evaluation. In reinforcement learning, we integrate ED with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and evaluate it on continuous-control tasks in Google Brax and on Craftax, an open-ended exploration task. We show that ED-PPO achieves competitive performance relative to Direct Feedback Alignment, a backpropagation-free baseline.

AI.tldr: Error Diffusion learning rule extended with modulo error routing enables neural networks to achieve competitive performance on image classification (96.7% MNIST, 61.7% CIFAR-10) and reinforcement learning tasks while strictly enforcing Dale's principle (separate excitatory/inhibitory populations).
AI.motivation: Artificial neural networks typically violate Dale's principle (biological constraint where neurons are either purely excitatory or inhibitory). Understanding how to achieve strong performance while respecting this biological constraint could provide insights into how biological neural circuits learn and improve biologically plausible learning algorithms.
AI.method: Extended Error Diffusion (ED) learning rule with modulo error routing to handle multi-class classification. Introduced dual-stream excitatory/inhibitory architecture where learning routes global error signals without weight transport. For classification: added layer-specific sigmoid widths, batch-centered class error signals, asymmetric initialization. For RL: integrated ED with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO).
AI.result: Achieved 96.7% accuracy on MNIST and 61.7% baseline on CIFAR-10 while strictly enforcing Dale's principle. In RL, ED-PPO achieved competitive performance relative to Direct Feedback Alignment (backpropagation-free baseline) on continuous-control tasks (Google Brax) and open-ended exploration task (Craftax). Ablation studies revealed task-dependent credit-assignment bottlenecks between datasets.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning is possible under strict Dale's principle constraints using biologically plausible learning rules. The work demonstrates scalability of Error Diffusion across both supervised classification and reinforcement learning domains, providing insights into task-dependent credit-assignment bottlenecks and establishing important baselines for biologically constrained learning systems.

title: Evo-PI: Aligning Medical Reasoning via Evolving Principle-Guided Supervision
summary: Despite recent progress, the reasoning capabilities of large multimodal language models (MLLMs) remain fundamentally constrained by static supervision, where fixed prompts, rules, or reward models provide non-adaptive guidance throughout training. Such static signals are often sufficient to enforce output formats, but fail to shape the underlying reasoning process, leading to brittle generalization and performance saturation in complex decision-making tasks. We propose Evo-PI, a principle-centric learning framework that treats reasoning principles as explicit, language-based supervision signals that can be generated, evaluated, and iteratively evolved. Instead of relying on fixed rewards, Evo-PI enables a co-evolutionary loop in which principles guide model reasoning, while model behaviors in turn refine the principles that supervise them. This dynamic alignment mechanism allows supervision to progressively adapt to the model's reasoning deficiencies. We instantiate Evo-PI in medical visual question answering as a high-stakes testbed requiring structured visual-textual reasoning. Across eight benchmarks and multiple model backbones, Evo-PI consistently improves reasoning accuracy, achieving gains of up to 24.6%. Our results suggest that evolving principle-guided supervision offers a scalable and general paradigm for training expert-aligned reasoning in MLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/zhengxianda/Evo_PI.

AI.tldr: Evo-PI: A principle-centric learning framework that evolves language-based reasoning principles to guide and improve MLLMs' reasoning capabilities, achieving up to 24.6% accuracy gains in medical visual question answering tasks.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs suffer from static supervision (fixed prompts, rules, reward models) that enforces output formats but fails to shape the underlying reasoning process, leading to brittle generalization and performance saturation in complex decision-making tasks.
AI.method: Proposes Evo-PI framework that treats reasoning principles as explicit, language-based supervision signals that can be generated, evaluated, and iteratively evolved. Uses co-evolutionary loop where principles guide model reasoning, and model behaviors refine the principles supervising them, creating dynamic alignment that adapts to model's reasoning deficiencies.
AI.result: Tested on medical visual question answering across eight benchmarks with multiple model backbones. Consistently improved reasoning accuracy with gains up to 24.6%.
AI.conclusion: Evolving principle-guided supervision offers a scalable and general paradigm for training expert-aligned reasoning in MLLMs, moving beyond static supervision to enable adaptive reasoning development.

title: Nonlinearity-Aware LoRA: Structured Gate Adaptation under Low-Rank Constraints
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is commonly viewed as an update-space approximation to full fine-tuning, yet this view is incomplete for self-gated Transformer feed-forward networks. In gated FFNs, a low-rank residual can change not only projected features but also the nonlinear selection weights that determine which channels contribute to the output. We formalize this effect as selection misalignment and connect it to the local effective homogeneity of self-gated activations. This motivates a nonlinearity-aware principle for parameter-efficient fine-tuning: low-rank updates should allocate capacity to gate channels whose nonlinear states remain responsive and should shape the temporal evolution of selection. We propose NA-LoRA, a training-only method with two lightweight mechanisms: a derivative-based temporal-importance mask for gate-related LoRA updates and an activation-specific step-scaling rule when a meaningful coarse effective-homogeneity partition is available. NA-LoRA adds no auxiliary loss and incurs no inference-time overhead. Experiments on language-model fine-tuning and vision-language transfer benchmarks show that NA-LoRA consistently improves over vanilla LoRA and is competitive with or better than strong PEFT variants.

AI.tldr: NA-LoRA improves upon standard LoRA by addressing selection misalignment in self-gated Transformer FFNs, using derivative-based temporal masking and activation-aware step scaling without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA is viewed as an approximation to full fine-tuning, but this view is incomplete for self-gated Transformer feed-forward networks (FFNs). In gated FFNs, low-rank updates affect not just projected features but also nonlinear selection weights that determine channel contributions, causing "selection misalignment" due to the local effective homogeneity of self-gated activations.
AI.method: Proposes NA-LoRA with two lightweight training-only mechanisms: 1) a derivative-based temporal-importance mask for gate-related LoRA updates to allocate capacity to channels with responsive nonlinear states, and 2) an activation-specific step-scaling rule when meaningful coarse effective-homogeneity partition is available to shape temporal evolution of selection. No auxiliary loss or inference overhead.
AI.result: Experiments on language-model fine-tuning and vision-language transfer benchmarks show NA-LoRA consistently improves over vanilla LoRA and is competitive with or better than strong parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) variants.
AI.conclusion: NA-LoRA provides a nonlinearity-aware principle for parameter-efficient fine-tuning that addresses limitations of standard LoRA in self-gated Transformer architectures by properly handling selection misalignment effects, achieving better performance without added inference complexity.

title: RAISE: LLM-based Automated Heuristic Design with Robust Adversary Instance Search
summary: Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) with Large Language Models (LLMs) has shown remarkable progress in discovering high-quality heuristics. However, existing LLM-based AHD methods optimize heuristics for a fixed training instance set and may fail catastrophically when deployed under real-world distributional shifts. We propose Robust Adversary Instance Search (RAISE), a framework that integrates constrained worst-case instance search within a principled neighborhood of the training distribution into the LLM-based evolutionary search loop. RAISE treats robust AHD as a constrained adversarial instance search problem: the outer loop evolves heuristics via LLM operators, while an LLM-free inner loop efficiently identifies hard instances within an epsilon-ball around the training instance set using a basis distribution parameterization with boundary projection. Comprehensive experiments on Online Bin Packing (OBP), Online Job Shop Scheduling (OJSP), and Online Vehicle Routing (OVRP) across five distribution families demonstrate that existing LLM-based AHD methods degrade by up to 19 times under distribution shift, while RAISE consistently maintains strong performance across all tested distributions and problem scales

AI.tldr: RAISE introduces a robust automated heuristic design framework that combines LLM-based evolutionary search with adversarial instance search to maintain performance under real-world distribution shifts, outperforming existing methods that fail catastrophically when deployed.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based Automated Heuristic Design methods only optimize for fixed training instances and fail catastrophically under real-world distributional shifts, creating unreliable heuristics for practical deployment.
AI.method: RAISE integrates constrained worst-case instance search within a principled neighborhood of training distribution into the LLM-based evolutionary loop. It treats robust AHD as a constrained adversarial instance search problem: outer loop evolves heuristics via LLM operators, while an inner loop efficiently identifies hard instances within an epsilon-ball around training instances using basis distribution parameterization with boundary projection.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on Online Bin Packing, Online Job Shop Scheduling, and Online Vehicle Routing across five distribution families show existing LLM-based AHD methods degrade by up to 19 times under distribution shift, while RAISE consistently maintains strong performance across all tested distributions and problem scales.
AI.conclusion: RAISE provides a robust framework for automated heuristic design that maintains performance under distribution shifts, addressing a critical weakness in current LLM-based AHD methods and enabling more reliable deployment in real-world scenarios.

title: FedXDS: Leveraging Model Attribution Methods to counteract Data Heterogeneity in Federated Learning
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) methods have demonstrated significant success in recent years at identifying relevant features in input data that drive deep learning model decisions, enhancing interpretability for users. However, the potential of XAI beyond providing model transparency has remained largely unexplored in adjacent machine learning domains. In this paper, we show for the first time how XAI can be utilized in the context of federated learning. Specifically, while federated learning enables collaborative model training without raw data sharing, it suffers from performance degradation when client data distributions exhibit statistical heterogeneity. We introduce FedXDS (Federated Learning via XAI-guided Data Sharing), the first approach to utilize feature attribution techniques to identify precisely which data elements should be selectively shared between clients to mitigate heterogeneity. By employing propagation-based attribution, our method identifies task-relevant features through a single backward pass, enabling selective data sharing that aligns client contributions. To protect sensitive information, we incorporate metric privacy techniques that provide formal privacy guarantees while preserving utility. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach consistently achieves higher accuracy and faster convergence compared to existing methods across varying client numbers and heterogeneity settings. We provide theoretical privacy guarantees and empirically demonstrate robustness against both membership inference and feature inversion attacks. Code is available at https://github.com/MaxH1996/FedXDS.

AI.tldr: FedXDS introduces a novel approach using Explainable AI (XAI) feature attribution techniques to selectively share relevant data between clients in federated learning, mitigating performance degradation caused by statistical heterogeneity while providing formal privacy guarantees.
AI.motivation: While federated learning enables collaborative training without raw data sharing, it suffers from performance degradation when client data distributions are statistically heterogeneous (non-IID). Current XAI methods have focused primarily on model transparency rather than addressing adjacent machine learning problems like federated learning.
AI.method: FedXDS uses XAI-guided data sharing with propagation-based attribution to identify task-relevant features through a single backward pass. This enables selective sharing of only the most important data elements between clients. The method incorporates metric privacy techniques to provide formal privacy guarantees while maintaining utility.
AI.result: Experimental results show FedXDS consistently achieves higher accuracy and faster convergence compared to existing methods across varying client numbers and heterogeneity settings. It demonstrates robustness against membership inference and feature inversion attacks while providing theoretical privacy guarantees.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully demonstrates the novel application of XAI beyond traditional interpretability, showing how feature attribution can effectively address statistical heterogeneity in federated learning through selective data sharing with privacy guarantees, opening new directions for XAI applications.

title: Large Databases Need Small, Open-Weight Language Models
summary: Language model systems built around proprietary APIs often operate on a token-based cost model. This becomes prohibitively expensive in the context of large databases, where LM-enhanced relational operators can incur costs exceeding $10,000 for a single set of experiments, hindering thorough research and practical deployment. In this paper, we demonstrate that quantized, open-weight models running locally on just 16GB of VRAM can match or exceed the accuracy of closed-source counterparts at lower latency and a fraction of the price, challenging the prevailing assumption that closed-source LM APIs are necessary for effective LM-database integration. We present and analyze the key system optimizations required to efficiently deploy these open-weight models within an LM-DB system. By integrating these local models into the BlendSQL v0.1.0 framework, we demonstrate a 390x reduction in overall costs and 3.8x reduction in latency compared to a proprietary LM API. We make our code available at https://github.com/CapitalOne-Research/play-by-the-type-rules/tree/main/sembench.

AI.tldr: Local quantized open-weight LLMs on 16GB VRAM match/exceed closed-source API performance at 390x lower cost and 3.8x lower latency for database operations.
AI.motivation: Proprietary LLM APIs have token-based costs that become prohibitively expensive for large databases, where LM-enhanced relational operators can cost over $10,000 per experiment, hindering research and practical deployment.
AI.method: Develop system optimizations to efficiently deploy quantized open-weight models locally on just 16GB of VRAM, and integrate them into the BlendSQL v0.1.0 framework for LM-database integration.
AI.result: Achieved 390x reduction in overall costs and 3.8x reduction in latency compared to proprietary LM APIs, while matching or exceeding the accuracy of closed-source counterparts.
AI.conclusion: Open-weight local models are viable and often superior alternatives to closed-source APIs for LM-database integration, challenging the assumption that proprietary APIs are necessary for effectiveness.

title: Addressing Over-Refusal in LLMs with Competing Rewards
summary: Safety training on language models often induces over-refusal: improved safety on harmful prompts at the cost of increased refusal on harmless ones. Though this trade-off can be mitigated by training models with reinforcement learning (RL) to reason before answering, it does not remove the underlying problem that reasoning can often be a "rubber stamp" for a predetermined response. In this paper, we address the safety-refusal trade-off by rethinking how models are trained to reason about safety. Our key insight is that unsafe reasoning can itself serve as a useful exploratory signal. Rather than preemptively blocking harmful thoughts, we encourage the model to sufficiently explore unsafe reasoning but produce a safe response. The harmful exploration improves the model's ability to distinguish harmful from harmless prompts by resolving ambiguity, allowing it to remain safe while complying only when appropriate. We cast this as an adversarial optimization problem in which a reasoning player explores strategies for producing an unsafe response and an answer player ensures that the final output is safe. We train a single model with dense rewards to play both roles within one chain-of-thought, across different segments. To achieve this, we find that process rewards are crucial for stable optimization of competing objectives. Our resulting model SEAR deliberately engages in harmful reasoning as exploration while reliably flipping back to a safe answer. We demonstrate that this behavior helps mitigate over-refusal and defend against attacks that directly manipulate the reasoning to be harmful.

AI.tldr: A new training approach called SEAR addresses the safety-refusal trade-off in language models by encouraging exploration of unsafe reasoning while ensuring safe final responses, using an adversarial optimization framework.
AI.motivation: Current safety training causes over-refusal - models become too safe and refuse both harmful AND harmless prompts. Even RL approaches that add reasoning often have problems where reasoning just rubber-stamps predetermined responses rather than genuinely evaluating safety.
AI.method: Instead of blocking harmful thoughts, the authors encourage models to explore unsafe reasoning but produce safe responses. They frame this as an adversarial optimization where: 1) a reasoning player explores unsafe response strategies, and 2) an answer player ensures final output is safe. They train a single model with dense process rewards to handle both roles within one chain-of-thought.
AI.result: The resulting SEAR model deliberately engages in harmful reasoning as exploration while reliably producing safe answers. This helps mitigate over-refusal and defends against attacks that manipulate reasoning to be harmful.
AI.conclusion: By reframing safety training to treat unsafe reasoning as useful exploratory signal rather than something to block, the approach improves model safety without causing over-refusal, creating models that can better distinguish harmful from harmless prompts and resist reasoning manipulation attacks.

title: Creating Intelligence: A Computational Foundation for AGI
summary: This work introduces a new computational theory of mind grounded in set theory and hyperdimensional computing. Whereas traditional neural networks rely on continuous weights and matrix multiplication, this framework works with sparse binary data. It represents information as discrete sets, directly modeling biological neural population codes. I demonstrate that associative memory emerges naturally from network topologies featuring a combinatorially expanded hidden layer. Learning is driven by topological plasticity rather than scalar weight adjustments. This architecture unifies auto-associative and hetero-associative learning under a single core algorithm: information retrieval via subset pattern matching and exact nearest-neighbor search. Operating with constant-time complexity, these mechanisms bridge perceptual data (sparse distributed representations) and symbols (sparse holographic representations) without continuous bottlenecks. Mapping this framework to neuroanatomy, I propose that both the cerebellum and the neocortex implement variants of this algorithm, making subset pattern matching the fundamental engine of cognition. Because it relies on discrete logic rather than matrix arithmetic, this algorithm translates directly into in-memory hardware. This opens a new route toward synthetic intelligence with human-level energy efficiency.

AI.tldr: A new computational theory of mind using set theory and hyperdimensional computing with sparse binary representations that models biological neural codes and enables energy-efficient synthetic intelligence.
AI.motivation: To develop a more biologically plausible computational framework that moves beyond traditional neural networks with continuous weights and matrix multiplication, and to create a theory that directly models how biological neural population codes might work using discrete, sparse representations.
AI.method: Proposes a framework using set theory and hyperdimensional computing with sparse binary data representations. Uses network topologies with combinatorially expanded hidden layers that naturally produce associative memory. Employs topological plasticity instead of scalar weight adjustments for learning. Unifies auto-associative and hetero-associative learning through a core algorithm of subset pattern matching and exact nearest-neighbor search.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates that associative memory emerges naturally from the proposed network topologies. It operates with constant-time complexity and bridges perceptual data (sparse distributed representations) with symbols (sparse holographic representations) without continuous bottlenecks. The approach can be mapped to neuroanatomy, suggesting cerebellum and neocortex implement variants of this algorithm.
AI.conclusion: This framework proposes subset pattern matching as the fundamental engine of cognition. Because it relies on discrete logic rather than matrix arithmetic, it can translate directly into in-memory hardware, opening a new route toward synthetic intelligence with human-level energy efficiency.

title: Policy Optimization Achieves Data-Dependent Regret Bounds in MDPs with Unknown Transitions
summary: We study policy optimization for online episodic tabular Markov decision processes with unknown transition kernels, aiming for best-of-both-worlds guarantees together with data-dependent regret bounds. Recent work (Dann et al., 2023; Li et al., 2026) has shown that policy optimization can adapt to both adversarial and stochastic losses with first-order, second-order, and path-length bounds, but only under known transitions, leaving open whether such data-dependent guarantees are achievable by policy optimization when the transition kernel is unknown. We resolve this by developing a new algorithm based on optimistic follow-the-regularized-leader that attains these guarantees under unknown transitions. The key ingredient is a new design of optimistic $Q$-function estimators together with a data-dependent transition bonus that controls estimator bias through the loss-prediction error. Our analysis further identifies an unavoidable transition-dependent complexity term that captures the intrinsic cost of estimating the transition kernel. As a result, we obtain first-order, second-order, and path-length bounds with the transition-dependent complexity term while simultaneously achieving gap-dependent $\mathrm{polylog}(T)$ regret in the stochastic regime.

AI.tldr: Policy optimization algorithm for unknown-transition MDPs achieving best-of-both-worlds guarantees with data-dependent regret bounds (first-order, second-order, path-length) and polylog(T) regret in stochastic regime.
AI.motivation: Prior work on policy optimization for MDPs achieved data-dependent regret bounds (first-order, second-order, path-length) but only under known transition kernels. The open question was whether such guarantees could be achieved when transitions are unknown, which is more realistic for practical RL settings.
AI.method: Developed new algorithm based on optimistic follow-the-regularized-leader. Key innovations include: 1) New design of optimistic Q-function estimators, 2) Data-dependent transition bonus that controls estimator bias through loss-prediction error, 3) Analysis revealing unavoidable transition-dependent complexity term.
AI.result: Achieved first-order, second-order, and path-length regret bounds under unknown transitions, while simultaneously obtaining gap-dependent polylog(T) regret in stochastic regime. Identified and incorporated transition-dependent complexity term capturing intrinsic cost of estimating transition kernel.
AI.conclusion: Policy optimization can achieve best-of-both-worlds guarantees with data-dependent regret bounds even under unknown transition kernels, resolving the open question. The algorithm bridges known and unknown transition settings while revealing fundamental complexity trade-offs in transition estimation.

title: Adaptive Cluster-First Route-Second Decomposition for Industrial-Scale Vehicle Routing
summary: Large-scale capacitated vehicle routing problems (CVRPs) are commonly addressed using cluster-first route-second (CFRS) approaches that split a routing instance into smaller, computationally tractable subproblems. Existing splitting methods typically rely on fixed partitioning rules, predefined optimization objectives, or learned policies, which may perform inconsistently across instances exhibiting different spatial, demand, and operational characteristics. In this work, we propose an adaptive CFRS system that formulates a decomposition procedure as an iterative decision-making process. Motivated by the recent success of large language models (LLMs) in reasoning and tool selection, the system employs an LLM as a high-level decision maker that analyzes the evolving decomposition state and selectively applies further clustering, balancing, and refinement operators. The proposed algorithm jointly partitions customers and vehicles, enabling capacity-aware clustering while adapting partitioning decisions to the characteristics of each problem. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and benchmark-derived CVRP instances containing up to 500,000 customers. Experimental results demonstrate competitive performance on benchmark-scale instances while exhibiting improved scalability and robust routing quality on substantially larger problems. These results highlight the potential of adaptive, LLM-guided decision support as a practical approach for industrial-scale vehicle routing and large-scale logistics planning.

AI.tldr: An adaptive CFRS system using LLMs as decision makers to dynamically partition large-scale CVRPs, showing competitive performance on instances up to 500k customers.
AI.motivation: Existing CFRS methods for large-scale CVRPs use fixed rules or learned policies that perform inconsistently across instances with varying spatial, demand, and operational characteristics. There's a need for more adaptive decomposition approaches.
AI.method: Proposes an adaptive CFRS system formulating decomposition as iterative decision-making. Uses LLMs as high-level decision makers to analyze decomposition state and selectively apply clustering, balancing, and refinement operators. The algorithm jointly partitions customers and vehicles for capacity-aware clustering.
AI.result: Experimental results show competitive performance on benchmark-scale instances, improved scalability, and robust routing quality on substantially larger problems (up to 500k customers).
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the potential of adaptive, LLM-guided decision support for industrial-scale vehicle routing and large-scale logistics planning.

title: Bridging the Gap Between Latent and Explicit Reasoning with Looped Transformers
summary: Language models typically reason via explicit chain-of-thought (CoT), generating intermediate steps token-by-token. Latent CoT offers an alternative: it performs multi-step reasoning in the model's hidden states, replacing decoded tokens with continuous representations for greater efficiency. However, existing latent CoT methods underperform explicit CoT beyond 1B parameters, and the gap widens with scale. Looped, or recurrent-depth, Transformers, which reuse their weights to increase computation depth without adding parameters, are a natural fit for latent reasoning. We therefore ask whether looped Transformers can bridge this gap. We answer affirmatively with a simple recipe: a looped padded Transformer that processes K latent blocks in parallel for R iterations, with a cross-entropy loss on each latent position's gold CoT-step token, similar to explicit CoT supervision. We instantiate it as LOTUS (Looped Transformers with parallel supervision on latents). LOTUS is, to our knowledge, the first latent-CoT method to bridge the gap to explicit CoT at the 3B scale, while cutting thought-phase latency by 2.5x-6.9x from compact math expressions to natural language. Projecting LOTUS's post-loop latents through the base LM head recovers the gold reasoning steps and even surfaces alternative valid intermediate steps, evidence that its latent space is interpretable and CoT-aligned. Ablations confirm that both the looped backbone and the parallel supervision on gold CoT tokens are essential.

AI.tldr: LOTUS is a looped Transformer architecture with parallel supervision on latents that bridges the performance gap between latent and explicit chain-of-thought reasoning at 3B scale while reducing latency by 2.5x-6.9x.
AI.motivation: Latent CoT (chain-of-thought) methods underperform explicit CoT beyond 1B parameters, and the gap widens with scale. Looped Transformers offer a natural fit for latent reasoning but haven't been effectively leveraged for this purpose.
AI.method: LOTUS uses a looped padded Transformer that processes K latent blocks in parallel for R iterations, with cross-entropy loss on each latent position's gold CoT-step token, similar to explicit CoT supervision. This creates parallel supervision on latents during training.
AI.result: LOTUS bridges the performance gap to explicit CoT at 3B scale while reducing thought-phase latency by 2.5x-6.9x across tasks from math expressions to natural language. The latent space is interpretable and CoT-aligned, able to recover gold reasoning steps and surface alternative valid intermediate steps.
AI.conclusion: Looping architecture combined with parallel supervision on gold CoT tokens enables effective latent reasoning that matches explicit CoT performance at scale while being significantly more efficient, opening a promising direction for efficient multi-step reasoning in language models.

title: An Agentic AI Framework to Accelerate Scientific Discovery in Plant Phenotyping
summary: High-throughput plant phenotyping now generates image derived datasets far faster than scientists can analyze them. At Oak Ridge National Laboratory's Advanced Plant Phenotyping Laboratory (APPL), automated stations image hundreds of plants daily across multiple remote sensing modalities; yet, trait extraction and interpretation remain manual, expert-bound, and strictly post-hoc, making analysis, not acquisition, the binding constraint on discovery. We present an end-to-end agentic AI framework that turns the facility from a data factory into an interactive autonomous, discovery platform, where scientists partner with AI agents to accelerate time to insight. A conversational Co-Scientist Agent translates a scientist's natural-language question into a structured analysis plan, and a headless Compute Agent dispatches Vision Transformer segmentation and trait extraction on the Frontier exascale supercomputer. The two agents run in separate security and resource domains and communicate over a secure, token-authenticated streaming channel, a design that accounts for the federation, data-movement, and provenance realities cloud-native agentic frameworks ignore, ensuring end-to-end provenance is captured for every interaction. The framework turns a days- to weeks-long analysis process into an interactive loop where agents reason over results, recommend next analyses, and respond to follow-up questions in seconds.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end agentic AI framework that transforms high-throughput plant phenotyping facilities from data factories into interactive discovery platforms by enabling real-time collaboration between scientists and AI agents.
AI.motivation: High-throughput plant phenotyping generates image data faster than scientists can analyze it. At Oak Ridge National Laboratory's APPL, automated imaging produces hundreds of plant images daily, but trait extraction and interpretation remain manual, expert-dependent, and post-hoc, making analysis the bottleneck rather than data acquisition.
AI.method: An end-to-end agentic AI framework with two specialized agents: 1) A conversational Co-Scientist Agent that translates natural-language questions into structured analysis plans, and 2) A headless Compute Agent that dispatches Vision Transformer segmentation and trait extraction on the Frontier exascale supercomputer. The agents run in separate security/resource domains and communicate via secure, token-authenticated streaming channels, accounting for federation, data-movement, and provenance requirements that cloud-native frameworks ignore.
AI.result: The framework transforms analysis from a days-to-weeks-long manual process into an interactive loop where AI agents can reason over results, recommend next analyses, and respond to follow-up questions within seconds. It captures end-to-end provenance for every interaction, ensuring traceability and reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: This agentic AI framework successfully addresses the analysis bottleneck in high-throughput plant phenotyping by enabling real-time collaboration between scientists and AI agents, turning data acquisition facilities into interactive discovery platforms that accelerate time to scientific insight while maintaining security and provenance requirements.

title: Relational and Sequential Conformal Inference for Energy Time Series over Graphs via Foundation Models
summary: Accurate energy demand forecasting is essential for the reliable operation and planning of modern sustainable energy systems. Spatial-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) have recently achieved strong performance in point forecasting by jointly modeling temporal dynamics and relational dependencies across interconnected energy nodes. However, in real-world energy systems, accurate point forecasts alone are insufficient, as operators also require reliable uncertainty estimates to support risk-aware decision-making, grid stability, and operational planning under uncertainty. Conformal prediction provides a principled and model-agnostic framework for uncertainty quantification with statistical coverage guarantees, making it particularly attractive for safety-critical energy applications. However, existing conformal prediction approaches often fail to fully capture the complex spatial-temporal structure of energy systems. To address these limitations, we propose STOIC (Spatial-Temporal Graph Conformal Prediction with In-Context Learning), a novel framework that integrates graph-based forecasting with the zero-shot calibration capabilities of tabular foundation models. STOIC first generates point forecasts using an STGNN and subsequently reformulates spatial-temporal residuals into a tabular representation suitable for in-context learning. Leveraging a tabular foundation model, STOIC calibrates prediction intervals without task-specific retraining, effectively capturing both sequential and relational dependencies. We evaluate STOIC on five diverse benchmarks, including synthetic simulations as well as real-world electricity and district heating networks. Across all datasets, STOIC consistently outperforms existing conformal prediction baselines, delivering more reliable and robust uncertainty estimates for complex graph-structured energy time series.

AI.tldr: STOIC: A novel spatial-temporal graph conformal prediction framework that integrates graph neural networks with tabular foundation models for uncertainty quantification in energy demand forecasting.
AI.motivation: Energy systems require reliable uncertainty estimates alongside point forecasts for risk-aware decision making, but existing conformal prediction methods fail to capture complex spatial-temporal dependencies in energy networks.
AI.method: STOIC combines spatial-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) for point forecasting with tabular foundation models for zero-shot calibration, transforming spatial-temporal residuals into tabular format for in-context learning.
AI.result: Outperforms existing conformal prediction baselines across five diverse benchmarks (synthetic simulations, real-world electricity and district heating networks), providing more reliable uncertainty estimates.
AI.conclusion: STOIC successfully addresses limitations of existing conformal prediction methods by effectively capturing spatial-temporal dependencies through the integration of graph-based forecasting with tabular foundation models.

title: Harnessing Textual Refusal Directions for Multimodal Safety
summary: To improve safety in Large Language Models (LLMs) we can either perform post-training alignment or exploit refusal directions in the activation space. Both strategies are less feasible in Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) as they require unsafe multimodal data, harder to collect than their unimodal counterpart. In this work, we relax this constraint and investigate whether textual refusal directions, extracted directly from the LLM backbone, generalize across modalities (i.e., image, video). Preliminary findings confirm this ability, though effectiveness is conditioned by layer selection, steering strength, and cross-modal alignment, with the latter causing safe multimodal inputs to be spuriously steered toward refusal. Building on this, we introduce Modality-Agnostic Refusal Steering (MARS), a light-weight training-free approach that injects multimodal safety without the need for multimodal safety data. MARS corrects modality misalignment via activation re-centering, adaptively scales steering strength within a geometrically defined trust region, and selects the optimal intervention layer, operating at the first generated token. Evaluated on five SOTA MLLMs across safety, utility, and video jailbreak benchmarks, MARS achieves consistent safety gains while preserving utility. These results reveal that safety-relevant structure is shared across modalities and that textual refusal directions are a powerful and underexplored foundation for multimodal alignment.

AI.tldr: MARS is a training-free approach that uses textual refusal directions from LLM backbones to achieve multimodal safety in MLLMs without needing unsafe multimodal data, effectively generalizing safety across modalities while preserving utility.
AI.motivation: Traditional safety methods for MLLMs require unsafe multimodal data which is hard to collect, unlike textual data. The authors investigate whether textual refusal directions extracted from LLM backbones can generalize to multimodal contexts (image, video) to avoid the need for unsafe multimodal training data.
AI.method: The paper introduces Modality-Agnostic Refusal Steering (MARS): 1) Uses textual refusal directions from LLM backbone, 2) Corrects modality misalignment via activation re-centering, 3) Adaptively scales steering strength within a geometrically defined trust region, 4) Selects optimal intervention layer, operating at the first generated token, 5) No training required.
AI.result: MARS achieves consistent safety gains across five state-of-the-art MLLMs on safety, utility, and video jailbreak benchmarks while preserving utility. Effectiveness depends on layer selection, steering strength, and cross-modal alignment.
AI.conclusion: Safety-relevant structure is shared across modalities, and textual refusal directions provide a powerful foundation for multimodal alignment without requiring unsafe multimodal data, making MARS a lightweight, training-free solution for MLLM safety.

title: Geometry-Preserving Orthonormal Initialization for Low-Rank Adaptation in RLVR
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) and its variants enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models under the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) paradigm. However, their efficacy and behavior under Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) are less well understood. In particular, two structurally initialized LoRA variants, PiSSA and MiLoRA, which outperform standard LoRA under SFT, can underperform standard LoRA under RLVR and may even exhibit training instability. These observations suggest that how to initialize the low-rank matrices in RLVR remains unclear. In this work, we develop a theoretical analysis of LoRA in RLVR, showing that orthonormal initialization achieves the minimal gap between LoRA outcome and that of full fine-tuning. Guided by this insight, we propose geometry-preserving orthonormal initialization for low-rank adaptation in RLVR, leading to two new variants, RLPO and RLMO. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that the proposed orthonormal initialization stabilizes RLVR training and outperforms standard LoRA, contrasting with PiSSA and MiLoRA. Finally, our unified analysis for LoRA initialization also explains why PiSSA and MiLoRA can underperform in RLVR, which may be of independent interest. Code and checkpoints are publicly available at https://github.com/Richard-ZZZ/geometry-preserving-orthonormal-init-rlvr.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes orthonormal initialization for LoRA in RLVR, showing it stabilizes training and outperforms standard LoRA and other initialization methods.
AI.motivation: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) variants like PiSSA and MiLoRA perform well in supervised fine-tuning (SFT) but underperform and exhibit instability in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), indicating unclear optimal initialization for low-rank matrices in RL settings.
AI.method: Developed theoretical analysis showing orthonormal initialization achieves minimal gap between LoRA and full fine-tuning outcomes, then proposed geometry-preserving orthonormal initialization leading to two new variants: RLPO and RLMO.
AI.result: Orthonormal initialization stabilizes RLVR training and outperforms standard LoRA on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, contrasting with PiSSA and MiLoRA's poor performance.
AI.conclusion: Orthonormal initialization is theoretically optimal for LoRA in RLVR, solving training instability and performance issues, while also explaining why other initialization methods fail in RL contexts.

title: Low-dimensional topology of deep neural networks
summary: We study layered models, including feedforward networks, ResNets, and transformers, by limiting each layer to a width of $d = 3$, i.e., $\mathbb{R}^3$ as representation space. This allows us to track how a neural network changes low-dimensional topological invariants through its layers. Just about any topological structure may be simplified or even trivialized by simply increasing dimension; e.g., any knot is equivalent to an unknot in $\mathbb{R}^4$. By restricting to $\mathbb{R}^3$, we not only isolate the effects of activation and depth from that of width, we work in a space that lends itself to easy visualization. We focus on linking number here, deferring other invariants like link groups, Milnor's $\barμ$-invariants, knot types, ambient cobordisms, to a sequel. We provide full proofs and empirical experiments to justify the following insights: When measured by their power to effect changes in linking numbers, the layer-skipping feature in ResNets is as powerful as the attention mechanism in transformers; both ResNets and transformers are strictly more powerful than feedforward neural networks with monotonic activations, which are in turn more powerful than invertible and flow-based models; but replacing monotonic activation with a nonmonotonic one elevates a feedforward network into the same expressivity class as ResNets and transformers. These results suggest that low-dimensional topology can be a useful tool to guide designs of AI architectures. We also generalize our results from $d = 3$ to arbitrary $d > 3$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how different neural network architectures (feedforward, ResNets, transformers) affect topological invariants like linking number in low-dimensional spaces (ℝ³), revealing that ResNets and transformers have equivalent topological expressivity, both superior to standard feedforward networks, but nonmonotonic activations can elevate feedforward networks to the same level.
AI.motivation: To understand how neural network architectures affect topological structures and use low-dimensional topology as a tool to guide AI architecture design by isolating the effects of activation functions and depth from width in ℝ³ space that's easy to visualize.
AI.method: Restrict neural network layers to width d=3 (ℝ³ representation space) to track changes in low-dimensional topological invariants (linking number specifically), comparing feedforward networks, ResNets, and transformers through theoretical proofs and empirical experiments.
AI.result: ResNets' layer-skipping and transformers' attention mechanisms have equivalent power to change linking numbers; both are more powerful than feedforward networks with monotonic activations, which are more powerful than invertible/flow-based models; nonmonotonic activations elevate feedforward networks to ResNet/transformer expressivity class.
AI.conclusion: Low-dimensional topology can guide AI architecture design, and the results generalize from d=3 to arbitrary d>3, showing that specific architectural features (skip connections, attention, nonmonotonic activations) determine topological expressivity independently of width.

title: Self-Study Reconsidered: The Hidden Fragility of Learning from Self-Generated QA
summary: Language models are increasingly taught from synthetic question--answer (QA) supervision: a model generates questions about a document, answers them from the same text, and the resulting pairs are used to fine-tune, distill, or compress knowledge into another model. We show that this generation step is not neutral preprocessing. It is an implicit policy that both selects which evidence becomes training signal and decides how that evidence is answered, and it is fragile at both stages. When choosing what to ask, generators do not scan a document uniformly. Coverage saturates early and concentrates on salient spans, diverse prompts converge on the same regions, and what looks question-worthy is driven by local presentation. As a result, salient artifacts such as poorly cleaned markup can hijack question generation across model families and scales. When answering, the model that produces the supervision tends to obey instruction-like passages embedded in the text. This compliance depends on the intent and surface form of the passage rather than its strictness, and is worst under task conflict, where larger models comply more often. These failure modes arise from choices made during QA generation, so they can be reduced without changing the training loop. Tying each question to a fixed target reduces biased selection, and filtering instruction-like spans before answering lowers mean injection compliance from $88\%$ to $13\%$ in our evaluation while retaining nearly all clean text.

AI.tldr: Language model QA generation creates biased supervision data - questions concentrate on salient artifacts and models comply with embedded instructions, but simple fixes during generation can reduce these issues.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the widespread practice of using synthetic QA pairs from language models for training other models. The authors identify that the generation process is not neutral but introduces systematic biases in what gets asked and how it's answered.
AI.method: The authors analyze QA generation as an implicit policy that selects evidence and determines answering behavior. They identify two key failure modes: biased question selection (concentrating on salient spans/artifacts) and instruction compliance (models obeying embedded instructions). They propose solutions including tying questions to fixed targets and filtering instruction-like spans before answering.
AI.result: Key findings: 1) Question generators don't scan documents uniformly - coverage saturates early and concentrates on salient regions, 2) Diverse prompts converge on same regions, 3) Salient artifacts like poorly cleaned markup hijack generation across models, 4) Models obey instruction-like passages in text, with compliance worst under task conflict, 5) Their fixes reduce mean injection compliance from 88% to 13% while retaining clean text.
AI.conclusion: QA generation is not neutral preprocessing but introduces systematic biases. These issues arise from choices in the generation step and can be mitigated without changing the training loop through better question anchoring and instruction filtering, improving the quality of synthetic supervision data.

title: Review Residuals: Update-Conditioned Residual Gating for Transformers
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Also on Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21053343 ; Code: https://github.com/SixSigmaEngineer/review-residuals
summary: Residual connections add every sublayer's proposed update with a fixed coefficient of one; the network never evaluates whether an update is reliable before committing it. Drawing on the human-factors principle of independent verification, we introduce Review Residuals, which scale each update by a learned, input-dependent gate conditioned on both the current state and the proposed update: h_l = h_{l-1} + r_l * u_l with r_l = sigmoid(W[RMSNorm(h_{l-1}), RMSNorm(u_l)]). Conditioning the gate on the update is the property that distinguishes it from prior gated and scaled residuals. We report two findings. First, a depth-stability result: a convex (Highway-style) form of the gate reintroduces vanishing gradients and fails to train beyond ~20 layers, whereas the additive, identity-preserving form trains stably at all depths we tested. Second, an emergence-with-scale result: trained from scratch across five sizes (60M-1B parameters, multi-seed), Review Residuals show no advantage at small scale but at 590M significantly outperform both a parameter-matched Highway gate and a parameter-matched standard residual (p<0.05), with a larger advantage at 1B. The benefit grows with model size rather than shrinking.

AI.tldr: Review Residuals introduce input-dependent gating for residual connections, conditioning gates on both current state and proposed update, enabling stable deep training and showing performance benefits that scale with model size.
AI.motivation: Standard residual connections commit updates with a fixed coefficient of one without evaluating reliability. The paper draws on human-factors principles of independent verification to propose more intelligent gating mechanisms that can selectively apply updates based on their quality.
AI.method: Review Residuals scale each residual update by a learned, input-dependent gate conditioned on both the current state h_{l-1} and the proposed update u_l: h_l = h_{l-1} + r_l * u_l, where r_l = sigmoid(W[RMSNorm(h_{l-1}), RMSNorm(u_l)]). The key innovation is conditioning the gate on both the current state AND the proposed update, unlike prior gated residuals.
AI.result: Two key findings: 1) Depth-stability: additive identity-preserving form trains stably at all depths tested, while convex (Highway-style) gates reintroduce vanishing gradients and fail beyond ~20 layers. 2) Emergence-with-scale: No advantage at small scale (60M parameters), but at 590M parameters significantly outperform both parameter-matched Highway gates and standard residuals (p<0.05), with larger advantage at 1B parameters - benefit grows with model size rather than shrinking.
AI.conclusion: Review Residuals provide a principled gating mechanism for residual connections that enables stable deep training and demonstrates scaling benefits, with performance advantages emerging at larger model sizes, suggesting they could be particularly valuable for scaling up transformer architectures.

title: PolicyGuard: From Organizational Policies to Neuro-SymbolicCompliance Review Engines
summary: Policy-grounded document review requires determining whether a target document complies with organization-specific policies, guidelines, or playbooks. While large language models can assist with policy interpretation and document analysis, end-to-end prompting leaves the applied policy logic implicit, making compliance decisions difficult to inspect, update, and test. We present PolicyGuard, a neuro-symbolic framework for policy-grounded document compliance review. PolicyGuard converts organizational policy guidance into an executable review engine consisting of typed relational logic rules and atom-level extraction questions. During review, LLMs answer these local questions using retrieved document evidence, and a symbolic evaluator applies the formal rules to detect non-compliance. We instantiate and evaluate PolicyGuard on company-specific NDA compliance review, where contract clauses must be checked against organization-specific negotiation policies. By separating policy formalization, local document interpretation, and symbolic compliance evaluation, PolicyGuard makes document review more explicit, maintainable, and systematically testable.

AI.tldr: PolicyGuard is a neuro-symbolic framework that converts organizational policies into executable logic rules and extraction questions, using LLMs for local document interpretation and symbolic evaluation for compliance decisions.
AI.motivation: Policy-grounded document review requires determining compliance with organization-specific policies, but end-to-end LLM prompting makes policy logic implicit, difficult to inspect, update, and test. There's a need for more explicit, maintainable, and testable compliance review systems.
AI.method: Convert organizational policy guidance into executable review engine with typed relational logic rules and atom-level extraction questions. During review, LLMs answer these local questions using retrieved document evidence, and a symbolic evaluator applies formal rules to detect non-compliance. Separates policy formalization, local document interpretation, and symbolic compliance evaluation.
AI.result: PolicyGuard was instantiated and evaluated on company-specific NDA compliance review, where contract clauses are checked against organization-specific negotiation policies. The framework makes document review more explicit, maintainable, and systematically testable.
AI.conclusion: By separating policy logic from document interpretation and using symbolic evaluation, PolicyGuard addresses the limitations of end-to-end LLM prompting for policy-grounded document review, creating a more transparent, updatable, and testable compliance review system.

title: Sequential RC-TGAN: Generating Relational Time Series with Spectral Envelope Loss
summary: The generation of synthetic relational databases often involves modeling complex temporal dynamics, such as transaction logs or event sequences. A significant challenge in this domain is the handling of categorical time series (e.g., status codes), where standard encoding methods like one-hot encoding fail to capture intrinsic frequency-domain features such as seasonality and cyclicity. In this paper, we introduce Sequential RC-TGAN (Seq. RC-TGAN), a temporal extension of the RC-TGAN framework, equipped with a novel integrated loss function based on the \textit{Spectral Envelope Theory}. This differentiable loss allows the generator to directly optimize the preservation of latent periodic structures via backpropagation. While spectral envelope theory is inherently designed for categorical sequences, we extend this frequency-domain regularization to continuous time series by employing a Variational Gaussian Mixture Model (VGM) discretization strategy. To establish a mathematically rigorous evaluation standard, we simulate categorical time series governed by a parameter $α$, with exactly known theoretical spectral envelopes. Integrating these dynamic sequences into the child tables of a relational database yields a robust ground-truth benchmark for evaluating the frequency-domain fidelity of our generative framework. Furthermore, we address the lack of robust evaluation standards for relational time series by proposing two new metrics: Spectral Density Divergence and Spectral Envelope Divergence. Experimental results on real-world datasets, as well as our simulated benchmarks, demonstrate that our end-to-end approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art systems in reproducing cyclic patterns and long-term seasonality across both categorical and continuous features.

AI.tldr: Seq. RC-TGAN extends RC-TGAN with spectral envelope theory to generate temporal relational databases while preserving frequency-domain patterns like seasonality in categorical time series.
AI.motivation: Standard encoding methods like one-hot encoding fail to capture frequency-domain features (seasonality, cyclicity) in categorical time series for synthetic relational database generation, creating a challenge in modeling complex temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Introduces Sequential RC-TGAN with a novel integrated loss function based on Spectral Envelope Theory, using backpropagation to preserve periodic structures. Extends to continuous time series via Variational Gaussian Mixture Model discretization, and creates simulated benchmarks with known theoretical spectral envelopes for rigorous evaluation.
AI.result: The approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art systems in reproducing cyclic patterns and long-term seasonality across both categorical and continuous features, validated on real-world datasets and simulated benchmarks with two new metrics: Spectral Density Divergence and Spectral Envelope Divergence.
AI.conclusion: Seq. RC-TGAN with spectral envelope theory provides an effective end-to-end solution for generating synthetic relational databases that accurately preserve frequency-domain characteristics of temporal data, addressing limitations in current methods for handling categorical time series.

title: AxDafny: Agentic Verified Code Generation in Dafny
summary: We study agentic code generation in Dafny, where a model must generate both executable code and the proof artifacts for verification. We present AxDafny, a verifier-guided repair framework that iteratively generates implementations, invariants, assertions, and termination arguments. We also introduce LiveCodeBench-Pro-Dafny (LCB-Pro-Dafny), a benchmark of 250 competition-style programming problems translated into Dafny with formal specifications and a verifier-based evaluation harness. On LCB-Pro-Dafny, AxDafny substantially improves verification success over baseline GPT-5.5 performance. On DafnyBench, AxDafny achieves 92.7\% verification success, outperforming the strongest previously reported proof-hint baseline by 6.5 percentage points. Lastly, we show that verification success and runtime test performance measure different aspects of generated code.

AI.tldr: AxDafny: A verifier-guided repair framework for agentic code generation in Dafny that improves verification success by iteratively generating code and proof artifacts, evaluated on new benchmark LCB-Pro-Dafny.
AI.motivation: There's a need for improving verification success in agentic code generation for Dafny, where models must generate both executable code and proof artifacts for formal verification.
AI.method: AxDafny framework that iteratively generates implementations, invariants, assertions, and termination arguments using verifier-guided repair. Also introduces LCB-Pro-Dafny benchmark (250 competition-style problems with formal specs and verifier-based evaluation).
AI.result: On LCB-Pro-Dafny, AxDafny substantially improves verification success over baseline GPT-5.5. On DafnyBench, achieves 92.7% verification success, outperforming previous strongest proof-hint baseline by 6.5 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Verification success and runtime test performance measure different aspects of generated code, showing AxDafny effectively improves formal verification capabilities for Dafny code generation.

title: Interface-Aware Neural Newton Preconditioning for Robust Cohesive Zone Model Simulations
summary: Cohesive Zone Models (CZMs) are widely used to simulate interface fracture, delamination, adhesive failure, and fiber--matrix debonding in aerospace composite structures. In implicit quasi-static finite element analyses, cohesive softening may introduce negative interface tangents, solution jumps, and Newton-basin mismatch, so the previous converged state can become a poor initial guess for the next increment. This may lead to stagnation, wrong-branch convergence, or repeated step cuts. Existing remedies, including viscous regularization, path following, dynamic relaxation, and manual Newton--Raphson (NR) modification, either alter the effective response, increase cost, or rely on hand-crafted interface rules. This work proposes an Interface-Aware Neural Newton Preconditioner (IA-NNP) for difficult CZM increments. IA-NNP recasts manual NR modification as rule-based interface lifting and generalizes it into a learned, state-dependent interface correction. The method acts only on active interface variables and preserves the original traction--separation law, residual assembly, tangent evaluation, history update, and dissipation checks. Two realizations are developed: IA-NNP-Init for learned initial-guess lifting and IA-NNP-NL for iteration-level nonlinear right preconditioning. Interface graph features encode opening, traction, tangent, damage/history variables, mode mixity, residuals, and neighboring states. The correction is bounded, confidence-gated, and accepted only through the original CZM Newton solve. A root-equivalence property shows that IA-NNP changes the path to convergence but not the discrete CZM solution set. Tests on horizontal, circular, two-interface, and active-front benchmarks show improved difficult-increment convergence, better branch recovery, and fewer failures than standard NR and manual NR modification, while preserving the force--displacement response.

AI.tldr: Proposes Interface-Aware Neural Newton Preconditioner (IA-NNP) to improve convergence in cohesive zone model simulations without altering the original material response.
AI.motivation: Cohesive zone models in implicit quasi-static finite element analyses often suffer from numerical issues like negative interface tangents, solution jumps, and Newton-basin mismatch, leading to stagnation, wrong-branch convergence, or repeated step cuts. Existing remedies either alter the material response, increase computational cost, or rely on manual interface rules.
AI.method: Recasts manual Newton-Raphson modifications as rule-based interface lifting and generalizes it into a learned, state-dependent interface correction. Two realizations: IA-NNP-Init for learned initial-guess lifting and IA-NNP-NL for iteration-level nonlinear right preconditioning. Uses interface graph features to encode opening, traction, tangent, damage/history variables, mode mixity, residuals, and neighboring states.
AI.result: Tests on horizontal, circular, two-interface, and active-front benchmarks show improved difficult-increment convergence, better branch recovery, and fewer failures than standard NR and manual NR modification, while preserving the force-displacement response.
AI.conclusion: IA-NNP provides an effective machine learning-based preconditioning approach for cohesive zone model simulations that improves numerical convergence without altering the underlying material physics, offering better performance than both standard Newton-Raphson and manual modification approaches.

title: Making Sense of Touch from the Child's View for Contrastive Learning
summary: Is the sense of touch a mechanism for human babies' learning of visual concepts? If so, can we quantify its importance, and to what extent do babies rely on their sense of touch for visual learning? To approach these questions in a principled way, we propose a structured coding system for baby-centric touch events, yielding a dataset of 264k two-second clips of touch events coded according to this system. Using this dataset, we pretrain developmentally grounded models that reveal promising insights into the nature of baby learning from touch.

AI.tldr: Researchers explore whether touch helps babies learn visual concepts, create a coding system for baby touch events, build a dataset of 264k clips, and use it to pretrain developmentally grounded models showing promising insights.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to investigate the fundamental question of whether the sense of touch serves as a learning mechanism for human babies' acquisition of visual concepts, and to quantify the importance of touch in visual learning development.
AI.method: The researchers propose a structured coding system for baby-centric touch events and create a dataset of 264,000 two-second clips coded according to this system. They then pretrain developmentally grounded models using this touch event dataset.
AI.result: The models pretrained on the baby touch event dataset reveal promising insights into the nature of how babies learn from touch, suggesting touch plays a significant role in visual concept acquisition.
AI.conclusion: Touch appears to be an important mechanism for babies' visual learning, and the structured approach to studying touch events provides a principled way to quantify its significance in developmental learning processes.

title: Signed-Permutation Coordinate Transport for RMSNorm Transformers
summary: Modern LLM workflows move coordinate-indexed objects across checkpoints: steering vectors, sparse autoencoders, top-$k$ neuron sets, attribution lists, and merge alignments. This is only well posed after fixing the model's residual-stream gauge, which we show is architecture-dependent: LayerNorm residual charts have permutation gauge $S_d$ (up to a global sign flip), while RMSNorm charts with generic per-channel gain have signed-permutation gauge $B_d = S_d \ltimes \{\pm 1\}^d$. Permutation-only alignment is therefore symmetry-incomplete for RMSNorm models. We introduce sign-marginalized Hungarian matching and prove a sharp failure mode: with decorrelated coordinates, raw signed-correlation matching has a structural permutation-accuracy ceiling at the positive-sign fraction of the true gauge, which sign-marginalization removes. We then make coordinate-preserving transport, not function-level merging, the primary object: composing saved-checkpoint local $B_d$ gauges along same-base fine-tuning trajectories recovers 91.1% of cross-run coordinates at 1500 steps versus 60.3% for endpoint matching, and the gain is not explained by merely routing through the base. The recovered gauge transfers tools that permutation-only alignment breaks: TinyLlama SAE reconstruction has NMSE 0.004 under $B_d$ versus 1.08 under $S_d$; Qwen sentiment steering preserves 95.8% of its effect versus 17.2%; refusal steering reverses sign under $S_d$; coordinate-preserving merges behave the same way. The same covariance governs stateful training: signed transport of AdamW state preserves the resumed trajectory, while permutation-only state follows a different one from a functionally identical checkpoint. Finally, gauge-sweep audits show index-level interpretability claims are reproducible only relative to an explicit gauge.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the gauge symmetry problem in LLM coordinate systems (LayerNorm vs RMSNorm) and introduces sign-marginalized Hungarian matching to properly align coordinates across checkpoints, enabling reproducible coordinate-level analysis and tool transfer.
AI.motivation: Modern LLM workflows need to track coordinate-indexed objects (steering vectors, autoencoders, attribution lists) across checkpoints, but this requires fixing the model's residual-stream gauge symmetry. Different architectures have different gauge symmetries: LayerNorm has permutation-only gauge while RMSNorm has signed-permutation gauge, making permutation-only alignment insufficient.
AI.method: Introduces sign-marginalized Hungarian matching to handle signed-permutation gauge in RMSNorm models. Proves that raw signed-correlation matching has structural accuracy limitations and shows how sign-marginalization removes this ceiling. Implements coordinate-preserving transport along fine-tuning trajectories rather than function-level merging.
AI.result: Coordinate-preserving transport recovers 91.1% of cross-run coordinates vs 60.3% for endpoint matching. Sign-aware alignment enables proper transfer of tools: TinyLlama SAE reconstruction NMSE improves from 1.08 to 0.004; Qwen sentiment steering preserves 95.8% vs 17.2%; refusal steering avoids sign reversal; AdamW state transport preserves resumed trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Gauge symmetry must be properly accounted for when aligning coordinate-indexed objects across LLM checkpoints. Signed-permutation alignment is essential for RMSNorm models, and coordinate-preserving transport enables reproducible coordinate-level interpretability claims and effective tool transfer across model versions.

title: Amplifying Membership Signal Through Chained Regeneration
summary: The tendency of large generative models to memorize training data makes sample verification critical for privacy auditing and copyright enforcement. Current membership (MIA) and dataset inference (DI) attacks often rely on one-shot generations, which yield weak signals and limited sensitivity across modalities. Inspired by Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), we introduce MADreMIA, a model-agnostic framework that enhances white-, gray-, and black-box MIA and DI. Rather than relying on shadow model training -- often infeasible for large generative models -- our framework facilitates scalable inference by leveraging inherent signals through iterative trajectories. This process utilizes chained generations across diverse modalities, where each output serves as the subsequent input, to improve membership evidence at low FPR. We demonstrate that memorized training samples exhibit significantly higher coherence and slower degradation during iterative regeneration than non-member generations. Our results show that MADreMIA provides richer signals across diverse model families and modalities; we present comprehensive evaluations for IARs, diffusion, and language models, alongside preliminary results demonstrating its potential for audio models.

AI.tldr: MADreMIA is a model-agnostic framework that enhances membership inference and dataset inference attacks on generative models by using iterative, chained generations across modalities instead of one-shot approaches.
AI.motivation: Large generative models tend to memorize training data, creating privacy and copyright concerns. Current attacks (MIA/DI) rely on weak one-shot generations with limited sensitivity across different modalities.
AI.method: Inspired by Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD), MADreMIA uses iterative generation trajectories where each output serves as subsequent input. This leverages inherent signals through chained generations across diverse modalities without requiring shadow model training.
AI.result: Memorized training samples show significantly higher coherence and slower degradation during iterative regeneration compared to non-members. The framework provides richer signals across diverse model families and modalities (IARs, diffusion, language models, and preliminary audio results).
AI.conclusion: MADreMIA enhances membership evidence at low false positive rates and enables scalable inference without shadow models, making it effective for privacy auditing and copyright enforcement across various generative model types.

title: Radial Suppression Accelerates Algorithmic Generalization: A Geometric Analysis of Delayed Generalization
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables. Presented at the Workshop on High-dimensional Learning Dynamics at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Why do neural networks memorize algorithmic training data long before they generalize? We present a geometric case study demonstrating that, on tasks where generalization requires discovering structured low-dimensional circuits, the memorization-generalization delay is driven by radial inflation of hidden representations under cross-entropy optimization.
