We formalize a radial-angular decomposition of activation-space dynamics and derive three testable propositions: (i) that penalizing radial inflation induces anisotropic, data-dependent weight regularization; (ii) that it suppresses radial gradient energy below the isotropic random baseline, forcing predominantly angular updates; and (iii) that it biases convergence toward flatter minima. To empirically validate these propositions, we study a single-hyperparameter norm penalty that softly constrains activations to a sqrt(d)-radius hypersphere. On modular arithmetic, this penalty accelerates grokking up to 6x across MLPs and Transformers, and halves training steps for a 10M-parameter nanoGPT on 3-digit addition.

AI.tldr: A norm penalty that softly constrains activations to a sqrt(d)-radius hypersphere accelerates grokking (up to 6x faster) by suppressing radial inflation of hidden representations, which delays generalization in neural networks.
AI.motivation: Neural networks often memorize algorithmic training data long before they generalize (the "memorization-generalization delay" or "grokking"). The paper investigates why this happens, particularly on tasks where generalization requires discovering structured low-dimensional circuits.
AI.method: The authors present a geometric case study with a radial-angular decomposition of activation-space dynamics. They derive three testable propositions about how penalizing radial inflation affects learning. They empirically validate using a single-hyperparameter norm penalty that softly constrains activations to a sqrt(d)-radius hypersphere, testing on modular arithmetic tasks with MLPs and Transformers.
AI.result: The norm penalty accelerates grokking up to 6x across MLPs and Transformers. For a 10M-parameter nanoGPT on 3-digit addition, it halves the training steps required to achieve generalization.
AI.conclusion: The memorization-generalization delay is driven by radial inflation of hidden representations under cross-entropy optimization. Constraining this radial inflation forces more angular updates, induces anisotropic regularization, and biases convergence toward flatter minima, thereby accelerating generalization on algorithmic tasks.

title: Surrogate Fidelity: When Can Open LLMs Explain Closed Ones?
summary: Mechanistic interpretability (MI) requires full access to model internals, yet the APIs for most widely deployed language models at best expose log-probabilities over output tokens. This creates a surrogate problem: when do measurements made on open models allow us to make claims about a closed model? We evaluate surrogate fidelity at the prediction, attribution, and representation levels. For binary classification tasks, log-odds provide an API-compatible scalar readout of the model's representation space, and leave-one-out attributions provide insight into model behavior. Across eleven models spanning four families (Llama, Qwen, GPT, and Gemini), we find that prediction fidelity substantially overstates attribution fidelity: models that agree on what the answer is often disagree on why. We document an access-validity inversion: white-box signals like attention patterns and perturbation magnitudes are highly stable across models but only weakly predictive of causal attributions, which black-box input ablations capture by design. Mechanistic insight does not automatically transfer to closed targets, and prediction-level agreement is insufficient to warrant such transfer. Code and results are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/surrogate.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates surrogate fidelity in mechanistic interpretability, finding that prediction agreement between models overstates attribution fidelity, and mechanistic insights don't automatically transfer from open to closed models.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic interpretability requires full access to model internals, but most widely deployed language models only expose limited APIs (log-probabilities over output tokens). This creates a surrogate problem: when can measurements on open models allow claims about closed models?
AI.method: Evaluated surrogate fidelity at three levels: prediction, attribution, and representation. Used binary classification tasks with log-odds as API-compatible scalar readouts and leave-one-out attributions. Tested across 11 models spanning four families (Llama, Qwen, GPT, and Gemini). Examined white-box signals like attention patterns and perturbation magnitudes versus black-box input ablations.
AI.result: Prediction fidelity substantially overstates attribution fidelity: models that agree on answers often disagree on why. Found access-validity inversion: white-box signals are highly stable across models but only weakly predictive of causal attributions, while black-box input ablations capture attributions by design.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic insight does not automatically transfer to closed targets, and prediction-level agreement is insufficient to warrant such transfer. There's a need to be cautious about making claims about closed models based on open model measurements.

title: CoMet: Context and Multiplicity Decomposition for Multimodal Uncertainty Estimation
summary: Uncertainty estimation has been a long-standing challenge in AI models; it amounts to "knowing what you don't know," and metacognition is notoriously difficult even for humans (cf. the Dunning-Kruger effect). Although it is still far from solved even in simpler classification systems, tackling it in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is becoming increasingly important. Within MLLMs, uncertainty can stem from any of the diverse sources as well as from their relationships, and further can stem from the unbounded answers in the open-ended setting. To tackle the issues, we propose CoMet, an MLLM uncertainty estimation method by decomposing uncertainty into a context-specific term and a multiplicity-specific term. The former captures ambiguity induced by the given context (e.g., task or prompt), while the latter captures how many plausible answers determined by the context remain compatible with the given input. We train a lightweight post-hoc uncertainty module to estimate these quantities, which enables efficient uncertainty estimation without autoregressive answer generation or repeated sampling. Experiments on various open-ended multimodal benchmarks, hallucination detection, and multiple-choice visual question answering benchmarks show that CoMet consistently improves uncertainty estimation over existing baselines while remaining efficient in practice. Code is available at https://github.com/princetonvisualai/comet_uncertainty

AI.tldr: CoMet is a new uncertainty estimation method for multimodal large language models that decomposes uncertainty into context-specific and multiplicity-specific components, enabling efficient uncertainty estimation without autoregressive generation or repeated sampling.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty estimation is a fundamental challenge in AI, especially for multimodal large language models where uncertainty can stem from diverse sources (multiple modalities, their relationships, and open-ended answers). Existing approaches struggle with the complexity of MLLM uncertainty estimation.
AI.method: CoMet decomposes uncertainty into two components: (1) context-specific uncertainty that captures ambiguity induced by the given context (task/prompt), and (2) multiplicity-specific uncertainty that captures how many plausible answers remain compatible with the input. A lightweight post-hoc uncertainty module is trained to estimate these quantities without requiring autoregressive answer generation or repeated sampling.
AI.result: Experiments on open-ended multimodal benchmarks, hallucination detection, and multiple-choice visual question answering benchmarks show that CoMet consistently improves uncertainty estimation over existing baselines while remaining computationally efficient in practice.
AI.conclusion: CoMet provides an effective and efficient approach to uncertainty estimation in MLLMs by decomposing uncertainty into interpretable context-specific and multiplicity-specific components, addressing the complex uncertainty sources in multimodal open-ended settings.

title: FedLAB: Traceable Semantic Codebooks for Federated Multimodal Graph Foundation Learning
summary: Multimodal graph foundation models aim to learn reusable knowledge from graphs enriched with text, images, attributes, and relational topology, thereby supporting diverse graph-centric and modality-centric tasks. In practice, however, such multimodal graphs are often distributed across decentralized clients, where raw contents and local structures cannot be centrally shared due to privacy constraints. This motivates federated multimodal graph foundation learning, which requires not only transferable representation learning but also intrinsic semantic traceability under strict data isolation. Existing methods usually exchange or store knowledge through parameters, prototypes, embeddings, or compact codebooks, which support optimization and transfer but do not explicitly expose how modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context jointly support predictions. To bridge this gap, we propose FedLAB, a traceable semantic codebook framework that organizes multimodal graph knowledge into typed hierarchical codebooks for modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context. FedLAB further refines these trace units through federated semantic barycenter pre-training while keeping raw multimodal contents and graph structures local. Extensive experiments on 10 benchmarks and 6 downstream tasks show that FedLAB improves over state-of-the-art baselines by up to 7.53\%, while preserving a native semantic trace interface.

AI.tldr: FedLAB is a federated multimodal graph foundation model that organizes knowledge into traceable semantic codebooks while preserving privacy through data isolation.
AI.motivation: Multimodal graphs are often distributed across decentralized clients with privacy constraints preventing central data sharing. Existing federated methods lack explicit traceability of how modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context jointly support predictions.
AI.method: FedLAB proposes a traceable semantic codebook framework that organizes multimodal graph knowledge into typed hierarchical codebooks for modality evidence, node semantics, and topology context. It refines these trace units through federated semantic barycenter pre-training while keeping raw data local.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 10 benchmarks and 6 downstream tasks show FedLAB improves over state-of-the-art baselines by up to 7.53% while preserving native semantic trace interface.
AI.conclusion: FedLAB successfully bridges the gap between federated learning and traceable multimodal graph foundation models, enabling both transferable representation learning and intrinsic semantic traceability under strict data isolation constraints.

title: TRIAGE: Role-Typed Credit Assignment for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Agentic reinforcement learning requires assigning credit to environment-facing actions such as searches, clicks, edits, navigation commands, and object interactions. Standard GRPO uses the final verifier outcome as a uniform advantage over all action tokens. This outcome signal is useful but structurally incomplete: it punishes useful exploration in failed rollouts and reinforces redundant or regressive actions in successful rollouts. We propose TRIAGE, a role-typed credit assignment framework that adds a semantic role axis to outcome credit. A structured judge classifies each segment as decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, or regression, and a fixed role-conditioned rule maps these labels to bounded segment-level process rewards. This keeps verifier outcomes as the source of optimization direction while correcting the two main blind spots of outcome-only credit. We further show that role-conditioned credit is the optimal segment-level correction expressible from role labels alone -- a projection of the per-segment advantage residual onto the role variable -- so that the fixed role constants reduce advantage estimation error whenever the judge is reliable, and we connect this to lower-variance policy gradients. Across ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop, TRIAGE improves success rates over GRPO for two policy models and outperforms both a scalar judge-derived process reward and an outcome-supervised shared-backbone value baseline. Ablations show that the gain comes from role typing rather than merely adding dense rewards: reliable detection of regression inside successful trajectories is the dominant contributor, while exploration credit provides a consistent secondary gain; on completed ALFWorld and WebShop rollouts, TRIAGE also reduces environment-facing turns by an additional $10.4\%$ and $14.8\%$ relative to GRPO.

AI.tldr: TRIAGE is a role-typed credit assignment framework for agentic reinforcement learning that classifies action segments into semantic roles (decisive progress, useful exploration, infrastructure, regression) and applies role-conditioned process rewards to fix blind spots in standard outcome-only credit assignment.
AI.motivation: Standard GRPO uses final verifier outcome as uniform advantage over all action tokens, which is structurally incomplete: it punishes useful exploration in failed rollouts and reinforces redundant/regressive actions in successful rollouts. Need better credit assignment that distinguishes different semantic roles of actions.
AI.method: Propose TRIAGE framework with two components: 1) A structured judge that classifies each action segment into four semantic roles (decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, regression), 2) A fixed role-conditioned rule that maps these labels to bounded segment-level process rewards. The framework adds a semantic role axis to outcome credit while keeping verifier outcomes as the source of optimization direction.
AI.result: TRIAGE improves success rates over GRPO across ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop domains for two policy models. It outperforms both scalar judge-derived process reward and outcome-supervised shared-backbone value baseline. Ablations show gains come from role typing rather than just adding dense rewards: reliable detection of regression inside successful trajectories is dominant contributor, exploration credit provides secondary gain. Also reduces environment-facing turns by 10.4% (ALFWorld) and 14.8% (WebShop) relative to GRPO.
AI.conclusion: Role-typed credit assignment (TRIAGE) provides optimal segment-level correction expressible from role labels alone, reducing advantage estimation error when judge is reliable and leading to lower-variance policy gradients. This effectively corrects two main blind spots of outcome-only credit assignment in agentic reinforcement learning.

title: SemRF: A Semantic Reference Frame for Residual-Stream Dynamics in Language Models
summary: Residual-stream analysis asks how language-model computation evolves across depth, but intermediate decoding requires comparable readout coordinates across layers. If embedding anchors and unembedding readout disagree on the chosen span, apparent motion may reflect measurement drift rather than computation. We introduce \emph{Semantic Reference Frames} (SemRF), an anchor-based formalism separating semantic measurement from residual dynamics. A SemRF fixes anchors and measures states against them. Pseudo-inverse tying gives exact synchronization; under restricted bi-invertibility, SemRF yields stable semantic-basis coordinates, distortion bounds, and near-identity changes. With the frame fixed, residual computation becomes a depthwise semantic trajectory. The anchors induce a semantic Voronoi diagram: distance, or evidence such as logits, assigns each layer to a coarse cell, while coordinates retain within-cell motion and margins. We define layerwise steps, contribution profiles, and imbalance diagnostics, then use the Voronoi trace to define a margin-relaxed tube. The canonical trace is the minimum-action path inside this tube; when nonempty with positive quadratic weight, it is unique and obeys a discrete spline equation away from active constraints. Excess action controls step, curvature, and profile mismatch. Low curvature implies piecewise-linear compressibility and local knowledge density: lower trace complexity means fewer semantic knots. Through the parameter-to-trajectory map, this gives a conditional link to parameter efficiency: among admissible settings fitting data, lower-action and lower-complexity traces use fewer semantic degrees of freedom. The guarantees require controlled interface error and small projection residual under explicit tube constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Semantic Reference Frames (SemRF) to separate semantic measurement from residual dynamics in language models, enabling stable analysis of how computation evolves across layers by fixing semantic anchors and measuring states against them.
AI.motivation: Existing residual-stream analysis struggles to distinguish actual computation from measurement drift when embedding anchors and unembedding readout disagree on coordinate systems. Without synchronized reference frames, apparent motion across layers may reflect coordinate system differences rather than genuine semantic computation.
AI.method: Proposes Semantic Reference Frames (SemRF) that fix semantic anchors and measure residual states against them. Uses pseudo-inverse tying for exact synchronization. Introduces semantic Voronoi diagrams for coarse cell assignment while retaining within-cell motion details. Defines layerwise steps, contribution profiles, and imbalance diagnostics, with margin-relaxed tubes and minimum-action canonical traces.
AI.result: SemRF provides stable semantic-basis coordinates, distortion bounds, and near-identity changes. The canonical trace is unique under certain conditions and follows discrete spline equations. Low trace complexity indicates fewer semantic knots and piecewise-linear compressibility. This creates a conditional link to parameter efficiency through the parameter-to-trajectory map.
AI.conclusion: SemRF enables separation of semantic measurement from residual dynamics, providing formal tools to analyze layerwise computation trajectories. This connects model architecture choices to semantic trajectory complexity and parameter efficiency, with theoretical guarantees under controlled interface errors and small projection residuals.

title: AdaJEPA: An Adaptive Latent World Model
summary: Latent world models enable planning from high-dimensional observations by predicting future states in a compact latent space. However, these models are typically kept frozen at test time: when their predictions become inaccurate, planning can fail, especially under test-time distribution shift. To address this, we propose AdaJEPA, an adaptive latent world model that performs test-time adaptation within the closed loop of model predictive control (MPC). After training, AdaJEPA plans and executes the first action chunk, uses the observed next-state transition as a self-supervised adaptation signal, and replans with the updated model. This closed-loop update continuously recalibrates the world model without additional expert demonstrations. Across a range of goal-reaching tasks, AdaJEPA substantially improves planning success with as few as one gradient step per MPC replanning step.

AI.tldr: AdaJEPA is an adaptive latent world model that performs test-time adaptation within model predictive control to improve planning under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Standard latent world models are frozen at test time, leading to inaccurate predictions and planning failures when facing test-time distribution shifts, especially when the environment differs from training conditions.
AI.method: AdaJEPA integrates test-time adaptation within closed-loop model predictive control. It plans and executes an action chunk, uses the observed state transition as self-supervised adaptation signal, performs gradient updates on the world model, and replans with the updated model in a continuous loop.
AI.result: AdaJEPA substantially improves planning success rates across various goal-reaching tasks with minimal computational overhead (as few as one gradient step per MPC replanning step) without needing additional expert demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Closed-loop test-time adaptation enables latent world models to recalibrate continuously during execution, making them more robust to distribution shifts and improving planning performance in real-world deployment scenarios.

title: QVal: Cheaply Evaluating Dense Supervision Signals for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures in main text; 48 pages, 6 figures with appendix
summary: LLM agents increasingly act over long horizons, where a single trajectory can contain hundreds or thousands of actions. In these settings, outcome-only rewards provide too sparse guidance, failing to inform the model about the goodness of intermediate actions. Dense supervision methods aim to solve this problem by scoring intermediate steps, from intrinsic confidence to self-distillation and embedding similarities. However, it is common practice to evaluate them by measuring the downstream performance of a training pipeline that integrates them. This is expensive, conflates supervision quality with training engineering confounders, and renders different methodological families requiring distinct training setups incomparable. As a result, dense supervision methods are rarely benchmarked on common ground. We introduce QVal, a training-free testbed for directly evaluating dense supervision signals. Given a state-action pair, QVal measures how well a method's score is Q-aligned: whether it orders actions according to the Q-values of a strong reference-policy. This lets us compare signals before any training run and separate signal quality from other engineering choices. We instantiate QVal as QVal-v1.0, benchmarking 21 dense supervision methods across four diverse environments and seven methodological families, with over 1.2K evaluation experiments across six open-weight model backbones. We find that simple prompting baselines consistently outperform recent dense supervision methods from the literature, and that performance clusters strongly by family. These findings hold across model sizes, environments, and observation modalities. QVal is designed to be easily extensible to new environments and methods, enabling researchers to iterate on dense supervision methods before any training run.

AI.tldr: QVal is a training-free benchmark for evaluating dense supervision methods in LLM agents by measuring how well their scores align with Q-values from a strong reference policy, enabling comparison of supervision quality separate from training engineering choices.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of dense supervision methods is expensive, conflates supervision quality with training engineering confounders, and makes different methodological families incomparable due to requiring distinct training setups, resulting in a lack of common ground benchmarking.
AI.method: QVal measures Q-alignment: given state-action pairs, it evaluates whether a method's score orders actions according to the Q-values of a strong reference policy, providing a training-free testbed. QVal-v1.0 benchmarks 21 methods across 4 environments, 7 methodological families, and 6 model backbones with over 1.2K experiments.
AI.result: Simple prompting baselines consistently outperform recent dense supervision methods from literature, and performance clusters strongly by methodological family. These findings hold across model sizes, environments, and observation modalities.
AI.conclusion: QVal enables researchers to directly compare dense supervision signals before training runs, separating signal quality from engineering choices, and provides an extensible framework for iterating on dense supervision methods efficiently.
